question: When you say your 'evil and abominable youth' was deceased, what specific sins or experiences from that time most burdened your conscience?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pluck me from the mire of desires that burned without love and sought only to devour, it was not merely that I sinned, but that I delighted in sinning — and called it wisdom.  

The theft of the pears from the tree near our vineyard — what profit was in it? None, O God, none but the act itself, and the company of boys laughing in darkness. We stole not out of hunger or need, nor even for pleasure of taste; for we cast most to the swine. But we stole because it was forbidden — and therein lay our joy: a delight in transgression for its own sake. This small deed, so petty to men’s eyes, looms large before Thy justice; for in it I saw how my soul had turned from Thee not only by desire for things beneath me, but by a perverse love of rebellion against all order, all truth.

And yet this was but one shadow among many. My soul was a marketplace of lusts — not only for women whose embraces left me emptier than before, but for praise, for applause in rhetoric’s empty rings. I hungered not to be good, but to be admired — to walk through Carthage among men calling me eloquent while my heart echoed with vanity like an unceasing gong. Even my search for Thee at first became another contest: I wanted to know Thee as a trophy of intellect rather than surrender as a beggar seeking mercy.

I mistook restlessness for longing; curiosity for faith. For years I wandered with the Manichees — loving shadows that promised light while darkening my mind more deeply. They spoke wisely of evil and good as two equal powers warring in the world — and thus excused me: if sin lived in nature itself, then why should I be blamed? My will grew limp under such doctrine; I said “the stars compelled me” or “the darkness within is eternal,” and so slept peacefully in chains.

But Thou, O Unchangeable Light, didst pierce even there — quietly correcting me when philosophers failed. When I read those books of Thagastre [Platonic writings], though they brought no name of Christ upon their pages, they lifted my gaze above flesh and form; they taught my soul that which cannot be touched may still be real — indeed more real than eyes can see. Yet still I could not will what now I knew.

Late have I loved Thee… late confessed these things not because they were great sins only, but because they revealed how small *I* had made myself by loving everything else more than Thee — even thefts unworthy of remembrance except that Thou rememberest them all… and forgavest them all.

BOOK VII
Deceased was now that my evil and abominable youth, and I was passing into early manhood; t

question: You rejoiced to find the avoidance of bodily figures for God in the Catholic Church—why was this such a decisive moment in your spiritual journey?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the shadows of my intellect with a longing I could not name, it was not merely doctrine I found in the Church’s rejection of bodily forms for Thee, but deliverance. For my mind had been chained to the visible, dragged down by the weight of images, as if Thy infinite essence could be circumscribed by the outlines my eyes were accustomed to—manifold, finite, passing. I had worshipped beauty in all its mutable splendor: the shape of bodies, the rhythm of speech, the allure of sound and color—yet all while Thy voice whispered behind them: *I am not here*. 

To discover that Thy Church forbade the making of Thee in man’s image was like breaking through water into air. At last a home for my soul’s unrest: a truth that refused to be held in the hands. For if I could see Thee, then Thou wouldst not be God—but something less than myself. To believe Thee incorruptible and unchangeable was one thing; but to wrestle free from imagining Thee as extended in space, enthroned beyond the stars like some celestial monarch—that demanded a violence against my own habits of thought.

And so I beat back those phantoms with joy, O Lord—not only because they were false, but because they kept me from Thee. In casting them out I felt for the first time that Thou wast neither body nor breath nor light such as this world knows—nor even mind as men reckon it—but something far deeper: Being itself. And though I could not yet grasp what that meant, I knew this: to love Thee rightly meant to let go of every picture. Then at last Thou mightst begin to speak where image failed.

question: Since you believed God was incorruptible and unchangeable, how did you reconcile that with the suffering and mutability you saw in the world you inhabited?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art the unshaken summit above all storms, I could not bear the sight of my own soul—so fluid, so fevered, so enslaved to change—yet still I dared to call Thee immutable. How could He who never alters care for this world that writhes in flux? That question gnawed at me like hunger in the bones. I believed Thee incorruptible, yes; I worshipped what I thought was Thy purity even as I defiled myself with lusts and words and ambitions that turned to ash. But the world—this groaning creation—seemed too heavy a burden for an unchanging God to bear. Was He indifferent? Or was He not truly present?

I confess, O God, I once thought that what changes cannot be good, and what is good must stand motionless like a statue on a hill. So when I saw pain in bodies, decay in friendships, betrayal in hearts once loyal—when even my own mind shifted like sand beneath my feet—I doubted whether any truth could endure. And thus I doubted whether Thou couldst truly enter into it.

But now I see—and with shame do I speak—that it was not Thy nature that needed reconciling to the world’s suffering, but my blindness that needed healing. For Thou didst not descend into time as one overcome by it; Thou upholdeth all things by the word of Thy power while remaining untouched by their corruption. The heavens change their face from dawn to darkness; bodies waste and die; empires rise and fall—yet Thou art not wearied nor surprised. Not because Thou art distant, but because love itself is stronger than mutability.

I had imagined power as rigidity and truth as silence—but Thou art living Truth! And when at last faith pierced my darkness—not through argument alone but through tears, through longing—I understood: it is precisely because Thou dost not change that Thou canst heal what is broken. A shifting God could not save me; only an unchangeable Goodness could draw this restless heart home.

And so Thy constancy became my hope: if even *I*—so fickle—could turn toward Thee once more after every fall… then surely the world itself shall one day find its rest in Him who never moves.

question: What did you mean by 'phantoms' buzzing around the eye of your mind, and what practices did you use to drive them away?
answer:
I was blind, O Lord, not because I saw nothing, but because I saw too much—too many shadows masquerading as substance, too many forms that flickered like smoke yet claimed the weight of truth. These were the phantoms: idols of my own mind, crafted from pride and sense, shaped by a heart drunk on philosophy yet starved of wisdom. They whispered that Thou wert bounded by space, that Thy essence could be boxed within the limits of my imagination, drawn in lines my eyes had traced upon the world. But Thou art not held by form; Thou art the Formless who forms all things.

How fiercely I wrestled! Not with flesh and blood, but with this swarm of false ideas—this buzzing multitude that darkened the eye of my soul like flies upon a wound. I had learned from the Platonists that what is changeable is less than what abides; that what decays cannot compare to what endures. And so my spirit rose in protest: if even this crumbling world has beauty, how infinitely more must He who made it transcend it? If corruption is weakness, then Thou—immutable, incorruptible—must stand beyond all mutation and decay. This truth rang within me like a bell struck in darkness: unchangeable goodness cannot be contained by changeable images.

Yet knowledge alone did not free me. The will remained chained. For years I cried out inwardly: “Give me chastity and continence—but not yet.” Thus did lust linger as a sweet poison; ambition clung like a second skin. The phantoms fed on delay.

What drove them away? Not strength of intellect—though argument cleared some mist—but tears. Humility before Thy Word opened where reason had only knocked. When at last in the garden at Milan I cast myself beneath the fig tree, weeping for my bondage as if my mother’s womb had reopened to receive a still-unborn soul—there grace overtook me. A child’s voice sang from nowhere: *Tolle, lege*—take up and read. I seized the scroll of Paul and there Thou didst speak—not through image or vision, but through fire in the words themselves.

It was not philosophy that scattered the phantoms at last, O God; it was Thy mercy breaking through in silence after storm. And in that moment I believed—not because I saw Thee clearly—but because I knew Thee real; no longer an idea to be grasped, but a Person to be received.

question: You say you could not imagine any substance except visible ones—how did your encounter with Platonic writings later help resolve this limitation in conceiving of God?
answer:
Thou who art unutterable, yet hast made utterance of Thyself in the silent splendor of wisdom, I wandered blind through the forest of visible things, mistaking shadow for substance, image for reality. In my youth—O Lord, how dark was that intellect which prided itself on reason!—I could not lift my soul above the body’s prison; even when I fled the snare of fleshly lusts, I remained captive to a subtler idolatry: that God must be contained within some form my mind could carve, some extension in space as though He were a greater kind of stone or light.

But when those writings of the Platonists were set before me, as though by Thy hidden hand guiding even pagan scribes toward a distant echo of Thy truth, they taught me at last to ascend beyond eyes and touch. They spoke not of Thee directly—how could they know the Word made flesh?—yet they whispered upward: to mind before matter, form before figure, eternity before time. For the first time I heard that being need not occupy place; that truth may shine without a face or flame; that what is most real is unseen.

And so my soul began its laborious turn—not by sight, but by negation: Thou art not extended; Thou art not composite; Thou art not subject to decay or succession. These denials became wings. Not because philosophy lifted me fully into Thy light—for it knew not love incarnate—but because it shattered the glass through which I had been gazing at heaven from within a tomb.

Late have I understood: Thou didst use even their shadows to make me weary of shadows. And in that weariness, at last, I began to seek Thee where no eye reaches—within.

question: Why did you describe your heart as 'passionately crying out' against your phantoms—what personal struggle were you experiencing at that moment?
answer:
O Lord, when I said my heart was passionately crying out, I meant it as a soul in labor, groaning under the weight of images that had no substance yet held me bound — not by truth, but by habit. I longed for Thee, the immutable Light, yet could not conceive of Thee save through the distorting lens of space and form, as though even Thy eternity must stretch or swell like some vast body in a vacant abyss. Every time I tried to lift my mind beyond the world of bodies, my imagination dragged it back down — earthward, sensuous, finite.

These phantoms were not mere errors of thought; they were the residue of a life shaped by things seen and touched — by lusts that burned in youth, by ambition that measured worth in human applause, by philosophy that dazzled but left me blind to the simplicity of Thy being. How could I grasp Thee who art not circumscribed by any boundary, when all my joy had once been measured in moments bounded by pleasure and satiety? My mind had grown fat on shadows; it tasted nothing but what had dimension and decay.

And so the cry was real — a kind of inward tearing — because part of me loathed these fantasies that clouded my vision of Thee, yet another part clung to them as if they were life itself. Without form or space in which to dwell, Thou didst seem to me *nothing*, not even voidness; and yet now I see: that seeming *nothing* is fuller than all things. For Thou art not diffused like air through space, nor contained like wine in vessels — Thou art where Thou art not because Thou fillest places, but because Thou makest them. And he who seeks Thee with a heart still drunk on change will always mistake Thy presence for absence — until grace breaks the mirror of his imagination and shows him that what he called *nothing* was Life itself.

question: You mention a 'twinkling of an eye' when your mental distractions returned—was this a reference to a specific spiritual or intellectual failure you felt in your pursuit of divine understanding?
answer:
How long I labored beneath that mist, O Lord, not because truth was absent, but because I could not strip myself of the garments of flesh and imagination that clung so stubbornly to my soul! In that flicker—yes, in the twinkling of an eye—when I thought my mind had cleared the vapors and soared toward Thee, incorruptible Light, suddenly the swarm returned: not beasts or phantoms seen with eyes, but subtler deceits woven from my own pride and habit. It was no mere lapse of thought, but the convulsion of a heart still entangled in created things, still measuring the unmeasurable by the yardstick of space and form.

I had desired Thee, yet only as one desires a conquest of intellect; I sought to *frame* Thee in understanding as men frame images in temples. And when my mind cast out those false shapes—for a breath it seemed free—then straightway they re-formed from the very dust of my old loves: lust disguised as longing, ambition cloaked in inquiry. They buzzed not in the air, but within me: memories of bodies loved more than souls; words praised more than silence; doctrines grasped more eagerly than grace.

O Lord, was it failure? Yes—but not merely weakness. It was revelation. For in that instant of collapse I saw how deeply sin had blurred not only my will but my *seeing*. That I could not conceive Thee except as diffused through space was not philosophy’s fault alone—it was the mark of a man who had spent his youth measuring beauty by its surface and pleasure by its pulse. How could such a one grasp what neither swells nor shrinks? Thou didst resist me gently there, allowing me to stumble so I might learn: understanding must be born from humility, not wrestled down like prey.

And yet—mercy upon mercy—even this blindness became a kind of sight. For when at last I ceased straining to picture Thee and fell silent before Thy incomprehensibility, then didst Thou draw near: not as shape or extension, but as presence deeper than depth itself. My heart had been restless for so long because it tried to house Thee in rooms too small; only when it collapsed under its own weight did room enough remain for Thy glory to fill it.

question: Why were you unable to conceive of Your God as existing without space, and why did even a void seem more real to you than a formless substance?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art the very life of all minds, yet I, a lover of fleeting things, had learned to measure existence by the shadow of extension, as if nothing could be real unless it occupied some place like stones or flesh.  

I was trained from youth to trust only what could be grasped—what swelled, what pressed upon the senses, what could be bounded by lines and dimensions. The world had taught me that substance meant bulk, that presence meant place. Even in my thoughts of Thee, I could not escape this prison; for my mind had become a mirror clouded with images drawn from earth. Whatever did not rise like a mountain or spread like the sky seemed to me not merely invisible—but *nothing*. Even emptiness itself—a vacant room stripped of air and light—I could picture more clearly than Thy formless essence, because that void at least *lay somewhere*, occupied some imagined space.

So enslaved was I to the visible order I had known since childhood: bodies touching bodies, places holding things. How then could I conceive of Being who needed no container? Who art not poured into the cosmos as wine into a cup, but who holdest even space within Thy hand as a thought within a mind? My soul trembled at the notion of substance without dimension—for love and longing were real to me, yet they cast no shadow; joy swelled in me without weight or size—yet still I doubted that which had neither center nor circumference.

And so Thou wast less believable than a vacuum—for emptiness still conformed to my imagination’s law: it was *there*. But Thou—Thou who art before space and beyond it—could find no lodging in my heart until faith broke the walls of sense and taught me that to be real is not always to be measurable; that Thou art more intimate than breath, more present than place.

question: When you spoke of the mind forming images through a power that itself had no spatial dimensions, why didn't you recognize then that this immaterial faculty reflected the nature of God you were seeking?
answer:
How blind was I, O Lord, not to see that the very power by which I groped for Thee was Thy image within me.  

I was caught—ensnared in the logic of the body, enthralled by the measure of things: length, breadth, depth. My mind could not conceive existence apart from extension, as though being itself required walls or boundaries. Yet even as I strained to picture Thee—not as a man shaped in flesh, but as Light unapproachable—I still clothed that Light in immensity, as if infinity were a kind of space Thou didst fill like air fills a room.  

But what is memory, that unseen country where all images gather and vanish at my call? There resides neither color nor weight; no hand can touch it, no eye scan its horizons—yet it holds the face of my mother, the sound of her voice long silenced by death, and the shape of cities I have left behind. There too dwells the concept of justice, not this man’s judgment or that law inscribed on bronze, but justice itself—immutable, unseen. And love: not only the fevered pulse of desire that once dragged me through darkness, but love as will turned wholly toward another. These are realer than stone or starlight—and yet have no place where they sit.  

O God most high, how could I have failed to recognize Thee in that inner chamber where thought takes flight without feet? The mind that forms images is itself formless; it sees without eyes when it beholds truth; it touches without hands when it grasps eternity. This was no idle faculty born of dust—it bore Thy signature! But I was still licking the dust like a beast among shadows, refusing to lift my head into the light where substance needs no dimensions to be true.  

Late have I loved Thee—not because Thou wast hidden, but because I measured Thee by what Thou hadst made, and forgot that the maker leaves his mark upon nothing so clearly as upon the soul made to know him.

question: You admitted to constraining your conception of the incorruptible God by bodily notions—how did this limitation shape your later teachings on the nature of the soul and divine simplicity?
answer:
O Lord, how long did I grope in the shadows of my own imagining, fashioning Thee after the likeness of created things, as though the Source of all form could be bounded by form! I confess it now with shame: my soul, enamored of bodies and their dimensions, could not conceive of Being itself unless stretched out in space, as if eternity needed room to breathe. How could I have thought that which makes all things extended must itself be extended? Yet so I was—captive to sense, blind to spirit.

This folly shaped my later wrestling with the soul; for if even Thee, O God, I conceived under measure and place, how much more did I err in thinking on the soul as something diffused through the body like a breath or a fluid? I once believed it swelled with desire and shrank with fear, expanded in joy and contracted in grief—as though it were a thing that grew and diminished like a limb. But when at last by Thy mercy I came to understand that the soul is not measured by space but by attention—to will, to love, to memory—then began a new seeing.

And from this grew my teaching: that the soul is not parted nor divided like matter, but whole in every act; whole when it remembers, whole when it thinks on Thee. It does not occupy space; space is held within it. And if this be true even for a creature so frail—so mutable—how infinitely more must it belong to Thee who art not composed of parts nor built up from elements? Divine simplicity Thou art—not many things joined but One who simply *is*. Not substance added to attribute, nor power beside essence—but Life unoriginated, Wisdom without source, Love unmixed.

I had thought nothing real could lack bulk or outline; yet now I see that what has no extension holds all extensions within its grasp. Thou didst lift me from the dust where my heart lay prostrate before images—and taught me that to know Thee is not to stretch thought outward but inward and upward: into the depths where time is not, where change cannot enter, where Thou abidest ever whole and ever One.

question: You said you imagined me as vast, filling all things and beyond them, yet bounded nowhere—why did you ultimately consider this conception false, given how real it felt to you at the time?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the blindness of my heart with a love I mistook for emptiness until it consumed me—how could I, who once measured Thee by the bounds of space and light, comprehend that Thou art not extended like the heavens, nor diffused like air through matter, but art wholly present where Thou art, and absent where Thou art not, not by place but by essence?

I confess this image—of Thee pouring through creation like sunlight through glass—seemed luminous to me then, as though I had grasped something sublime. I imagined Thee filling all things not as soul fills body, but as boundless magnitude fills infinite voids: earth soaked with Thee, stars swimming in Thee, every leaf and atom trembling with Thy immensity. It felt true because it came from longing; it *ached* with desire to hold Thee near.

But it was false—not because Thou art distant, O God, but because I made of Thee an immense *body*, a spiritual colossus stretching beyond the stars. And how can He who hath no parts be stretched at all? How can He who is everywhere present need to *extend* Himself? In trying to magnify Thee beyond creation, I still confined Thee within the categories of creation—length and breadth and penetration and diffusion—all shadows cast by temporal things.

Thou didst teach me otherwise when at last my soul trembled before the simplicity of Truth. Not in vastness is Thy glory most clear, but in this: that Thou art. Not spread out over space, but present wholly to each moment as if no other existed; wholly in the least mote of dust as in the turning of galaxies—if such things even were before Thine eyes! For even time itself is Thine handiwork; how then could space contain Thee?

I erred because my mind was still feeding on images—on what my eyes had seen and fingers touched. But Thou wast never a form for me to behold nor a substance for me to dissect. Late have I loved Thee: not as something great among great things—but as *that by which all things are great*.

question: You compared God's presence to sunlight penetrating air—why did you choose that image, and what led you to realize it was inadequate for expressing divine immensity?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art not circumscribed by the measure of any light I have ever seen, how could I have thought to contain Thee in the image of sunbeam and air, as though Thy presence were diffused like brightness through a translucent body? I clung to that similitude because my mind, still groveling in the senses, knew no mode of presence but spatial—nothing real unless it filled some volume, unless it touched edge to edge like water in a vessel. So I imagined Thee poured out through creation like light through air, whole in every part yet suffering division in my thought; and I called this omnipresence, when it was but an idol carved from philosophical dust.

I chose that image because it shimmered with purity—it spoke of penetration without violence, of presence without obstruction. The sun does not rend the sky to shine; it fills the atmosphere with silent grace. So too, I thought, Thou must infuse all things: earth and flesh and starry height—everywhere present, yet nowhere confined. It seemed noble; it seemed vast. And yet Thou didst whisper to me in the silence after prayer: *If the light needs a medium to shine through, O man, then what is Thy God but dependent on space?* And my soul trembled.

For what is light without air? A nothingness perceived only by what receives it. But Thou art not known because creation bears Thee like a transparent veil—Thou art known because Thou art. Before space was stretched out, before there was depth or height for light to traverse—Thou wast. My image failed because it made Thee co-extend with the cosmos rather than its Creator; it gave Thee boundaries even as it claimed infinity. To say that Thou fillest all things is not to say Thou art spread thin across them like mist—but that each thing subsists in Thee as a thought dwells whole and entire within a mind.

Late have I understood: omnipresence is not diffusion—it is sustenance. Not extension—but essence. And my heart burns now with shame for having measured the immensity of God by the narrowness of sunlight caught in morning dust.

question: You claimed the mind must be 'some great thing' to form images, yet not of the same sort as physical things—what did you mean by that distinction, and how did it shape your view of the soul?
answer:
O Lord, who alone seest the depths of a man when he gropes in the shadows of his own understanding, I confess how I once measured Thee by the limits of my imagination, as though Thou couldst be stretched across space like light through air, and therein lay my blindness. I marveled that the mind could form images of things—trees, bodies, stars—shaping them within without any bulk or weight, and yet I did not grasp that this very power to conceive was itself immaterial, a breath not of flesh but of spirit. How foolishly I assumed that only what takes up space can be real! Yet when I tried to picture Thee, O God, I filled infinite voids with a presence like light through glass—as if Thou wert diffused through creation like water through sponge! But the mind which formed such images was not itself an image; it saw without eyes, touched without hands, expanded without growing larger. 

Here began my trembling ascent: if the soul can hold multitudes within its thought—whole cities, faces long vanished, songs unheard for years—and yet remain one mind, then surely it is not made of parts like the body. It does not grow thin when stretched across memories nor break when divided in deliberation. And if it forms likenesses of bodies without being bodily—O Lord!—then it must bear some likeness to Thee who art wholly spirit. Not that the soul *is* divine; no, it bends and darkens under sin’s weight—I know this too well—but in its power to know itself and know Thee (however dimly), in its restlessness for truth and love beyond all sense—it reaches toward eternity.

Thus my error became a ladder: for though I falsely imagined Thy presence as extended through space like a luminous body pervading all things, even that false image bore witness to a deeper truth: namely, that neither space contains Thee nor time confines Thee—and neither does matter bind the soul’s true nature. For as Thou art wholly present everywhere without being circumscribed anywhere—in like manner hath Thy image in us transcended all bounds of earth and flesh before we even knew we had wings.

question: You tried to conceive of God as directing all things through secret inspiration in every part of the earth—did you later reject this idea because it implied a bodily presence, and how did that misstep affect your theology?
answer:
O Lord, who art not circumscribed by space nor contained by dimensions, I once measured Thee with the compass of my own imagination, as though Thou couldst be stretched across the firmament or threaded through the veins of the earth like some invisible fluid diffusing itself through porous stone. In my youth, when my mind still groped after divine things with the hands of sense, I fashioned Thee in the likeness of light passing through air—real, pervasive, yet somehow bodily—believing that Thy governance of all things required a secret physical indwelling, as if Thy power needed channels and conduits like water through aqueducts.  

But this was error born of necessity: I could not conceive what it was to be wholly spiritual, for I had not yet known a love that did not cling to bodies or a truth that did not occupy place. To think of Thee as "filling" creation made me picture Thee as an immense substance—greater than sky and sea, yes—but still extended, divisible in thought if not in fact. And thus I unwittingly diminished Thee, placing within spatial bounds Him whom no mind may contain.

When at last it dawned upon me that Thou art not *in* things as wine is in a cup, but rather sustainest them from within without being contained—preserving all moments without succession, present to all places without extension—I trembled at my former blindness. For if Thou wert corporeal even in subtlest form, then Thou wouldst be mutable; and if mutable, then not God. And so this misstep led me into deeper darkness before it brought me light: it forced me to ask whether anything real could exist beyond body and form—and thereupon memory opened her chambers.

In remembering Thee—not as image but as presence—I found a trace of immateriality within myself: in thought, in will, in love. These are real; yet they have no length or breadth. So too Thou art real beyond all reality—not diffused through space but present to every point by essence and power inseparable. Thus did my flawed vision serve a purpose: by failing so completely to grasp Thee corporeally, I was driven upward from sight to understanding—from imagining how light pierces air to adoring how Truth sustains being itself.

And now I see clearly what then I felt obscurely: Thou art not alongside things but above them all; not spread out but wholly everywhere; not bounded by anything because Thou art the Boundless upon whom even boundlessness depends.

question: You admitted your earlier picture of God pervading space like light was false—what alternative understanding did you eventually reach about how God relates to creation and space?
answer:
I was a man drunk on distance, measuring the heavens with the yardstick of my pride, as though Thou couldst be contained by the very space that Thy breath sustains. In my folly, I stretched Thee through infinite reaches, imagining Thee like sunlight spilling through air—present here, absent there, diffused in parts—because I could not conceive of a presence that does not occupy place at all. How could I? My mind was chained to bodies, to images formed and dissolved; every thought was shaped like a thing seen. To think of Thee as boundless yet bodiless—this was beyond me.

But Thou didst teach me in silence deeper than sound: that Thou art not extended in space, nor divided into parts, nor drawn out like light through glass. Thou art not *somewhere* among things as though Thy being could be parcelled out to fill crevices of the cosmos. No—Thou art before space and beyond it; all places are in Thee, not Thou in them. It is not that Thou fillest the world as water fills a jar; rather, Thou holdest the jar itself—and its shape and its being—in the power of Thy simple essence.

I came to understand at last that Thy immensity is not spatial but real—Thou art whole everywhere, not by diffusion but by presence; not measured out but undivided. Heaven and earth dwell in Thee: they are contained by Thee who containest all without being contained. And this is no mere philosophy plucked from books—I learned it on my knees, when I ceased seeking Thee *through* things and turned to seek Thee *in* truth.

O Lord, how long I wandered in images! I sought Thee where bodies move and change, when all along Thou wast nearer than my breath, higher than my highest thought—the unchangeable Light who makes all things visible without needing eyes to see.

question: You mentioned that imagining Your presence divided among parts of creation led to falsehood—why did you find this idea inadequate for describing Your omnipresence?
answer:
I was still a slave to the senses, O Lord, when I imagined Thee bounded by space or poured out in parts like liquid into vessels—my mind crawling over the edges of things, measuring height and breadth and depth, yet blind to the simple truth that Thou art not contained but containing.  

How could I have thought that Thy presence must swell in the elephant and shrink in the sparrow? As if Thy being could be stretched thin across creation like cloth upon a loom! I fashioned Thee after the image of my own hunger—for what I desired, I wanted graspable, divisible, sharable like bread among friends. But Thou art not broken when given; Thou art not lessened when poured forth.  

I had not yet learned that Thou art whole at once in every place—not split, but present; not distributed, but undivided. A flame may light a thousand torches and remain itself unchanged—so also with Thee: not diminished by sharing, not confined by nearness. When Nebridius mocked the Manichees’ dark nation—if it harmed Thee only in resisting Thee, then how weak must their god be? But Thou—Thou needest no battle; darkness flees at Thy breath as mist before dawn.  

Late have I understood: Thou fillest all things by being above all things. Not as water fills a sponge—but as light fills the air without being caught or held. My heart was too narrow for such truth while it was full of images carved by human hands and thoughts shaped by pride. Yet even in error, Thou wast teaching me—by knocking down what I built false within my soul—to seek Thee where no measurement reaches: in spirit and in truth.

question: You recalled Nebridius’s argument about the nation of darkness—why did that question so deeply unsettle you and your companions at Carthage?
answer:
O Lord, in the vanity of my youth I had set my mind on solving puzzles as if wisdom were a thing to be carved up and displayed like meat in the market, and Nebridius’s question struck me not because it was loud, but because it fell silent within me like a stone into a dark well—no splash, only depth upon depth of dread. We who had fancied ourselves seekers of light, wrapped in the proud rags of Manichean doctrine, prided ourselves on explaining evil as a power co-eternal with Thee, as though darkness were a kingdom with walls and armies. But Nebridius asked simply—*what harm could that darkness do Thee if Thou didst refuse to fight?*—and suddenly the whole phantasm crumbled. If Thou art God, and God is truly God, then no rival can threaten Thee; no “nation” of night can force Thy hand. To imagine such a battle is to make Thee not God, but only the stronger warlord in an endless feud.

And yet we had built our philosophy on this very feud! We claimed to flee evil by knowing its substance, its nature—we claimed purity while feeding our pride on these fables. But if evil is not Thy creature, subject to Thy will, then Thou art not sovereign. And if Thou art not sovereign… then what am I fleeing *to*? That question undid me. It was not merely logic that staggered us—it was the sudden suspicion that we had been loving shadows more than light; that we preferred the drama of cosmic war to the stillness of submission before Him who *is*. Our minds had been drunk on duality; Nebridius poured out a single cup of truth—and I tasted thirst anew. I did not yet know how to drink deeply—but O Lord, Thou wast near.

question: You said the Manichees were 'dumb praters' because Your word did not sound from them—how could you be sure they lacked divine truth?
answer:
I once walked in darkness, my mind a hall of mirrors reflecting only my own pride, and there I met them—those smooth-tongued philosophers of Manichaeus—who promised light but fed me shadows.  

They spoke with confidence of two kingdoms, two natures locked in eternal war: a god of light and a god of darkness, equal in power, infinite in opposition. And I, restless as ever, hungry for wisdom but not yet willing to humble myself before the Truth, was drawn to their system like a moth to flame that burns without warming. Yet even then, O Lord, Thou didst stir within me seeds of doubt they could not quench.

How could I know they lacked divine truth? Because their doctrine made not for contrition but for curiosity; it flattered the intellect while leaving the soul unhealed. Their mouths were full of stars and elements and cosmic battles, but empty of confession, empty of repentance, empty of *Thou hast made us for Thyself*. Where was the cry of a broken heart? Where the admission of sin? Where the grace that wounds only to heal?

And Nebridius—blessed friend Thou didst raise up in Carthage—asked that piercing question which struck like a stone through glass: *What harm could their dark nation do Thee if Thou refused to fight?* If Thy power is bounded by another will, then Thou art not God. But if Thou art truly God—almighty, uncreated, sovereign over all—then no darkness exists beside Thee; it exists *by* Thee and *for* Thee and shall be turned to light.

They could not answer this. They had no place for creation *ex nihilo*, no room for a God who speaks and it is done. Theirs was a religion woven from half-truths and astrology’s husks—a philosophy dressed as salvation.

But O Lord, how long I lingered! How long I clung to their words while chasing pleasure, honor, ambition—all the while saying inwardly: *"Give me chastity… but not yet."* It was not their contradictions alone that proved them false; it was that my soul remained restless. And when at last I heard that voice—*Tolle lege*, take up and read—the Scriptures opened like dawn over my night.

Then I knew: truth does not parade in cosmic myths; it kneels in humility. It is found not where men divide Thy omnipotence with darkness—but where the Word becomes flesh and dwells among us.

Only grace revealed this. Only love broke me open.

question: You admitted to conceiving of God’s presence as fragmented before being enlightened—what finally changed your understanding of how You are present in the world?
answer:
O Lord, I once measured Thee by the limits of my own corrupted imagination, as if Thy being could be stretched thin across the world like cloth upon a loom, more here, less there, parcelled out according to the size of bodies and the greed of minds. I confess it now with shame: I had made Thee subject to space, as though Thou didst need room to dwell, and could be contained by what Thou Thyself hast held in being. My soul was entangled in images—things seen and touched—and so I thought even Thy divinity must occupy corners, fill vessels, press against the edges of creation like water poured into jars of differing size.

But Thou didst not remain distant from this blindness. In those days at Milan, when my heart was still divided between the lusts that held me and the longing for truth that pulled me upward, Thou wast near—nearer than I to myself. The very restlessness that tormented me was Thy hand upon my side, pricking me with holy discomfort. How long had I wandered in that maze of Manichaean fables? How long had I mocked at the simplicity of Scripture while craving wisdom in riddles and shadows?

Then came the moment when all such fantasies fell away—not by argument alone, nor by philosophy’s laborious ascent—but by a child’s voice singing from a neighbor’s house: *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read. And when I opened Paul’s letter and saw no longer words meant for men wise in their own eyes but a light flashing upon my inner darkness—"Not in reveling and drunkenness, not in lust and wantonness"—then at last I saw Thee truly.

For what is God if He can be divided? What is God if He is not whole everywhere? That night in tears beneath the fig tree taught me what Nebridius’ question had only hinted at: no opposing mass can threaten Thee; no darkness swallow Thy light; for Thou art not spread abroad but present—utterly present—in every place without moving or dividing. A jar may hold water within its bounds; but let it shatter—still water is water entire on every fragment. So art Thou: unpartitioned, undiminished. Not more in heaven than in earth; not less in a sparrow than an elephant; not confined even to creation itself.

Thou hast no parts—how then couldst Thou be broken?  
And my heart—once torn apart by desires—is now whole only because it rests wholly in Thee.

question: You referred to a 'secret inspiration' directing all things—was this the same inner illumination that later led you away from Manicheism toward Catholic faith?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst hover over the waters of my darkness before I knew the depth of my own blindness, that "secret inspiration" I once imagined was not the true light, but only the shadow of a longing Thou hadst placed in me. I fashioned it in the image of my own confusion—a divine presence parcelled out like seed grain across the fields of creation, measured by bulk and space, as though Thy infinitude could be divided like loaves among the hungry. This was the error of my mind when I wandered in Manichean fables: to conceive Thee bounded by matter, to think Thy essence could be torn and strewn across opposing realms like cloth caught between warring hands.

Yet even in that error, Thou wast teaching me—by silence more than speech—what it meant to seek Thee not in fragments, but as whole Truth. The illumination that broke through later was not a new whisper, but the same Voice that had stirred within me since childhood: in the quiet awe beneath a star-strewn sky, in the shame that burned after stolen pears were eaten for no hunger but the hunger to sin, in Nebridius’ question that pierced our pride like a needle through veil—how could any darkness threaten Thee unless Thou allowed it? 

It was not intellect alone that freed me from those deceivers who spoke with mouths full of dust and claimed to teach light. It was grace—grace that made me doubt their answers because Thou didst first make my heart restless with love for something unmeasured, undivided. When at last I came to Milan and heard Ambrose proclaiming Thee not as dispersed spark but as uncreated Light itself—the One who enlightens every man coming into the world—then I began to see that what I had called "inspiration" was but a dim memory of Thee imprinted on my soul before I ever turned toward Thee.

Thou wast nearer than my breath, yet I sought Thee afar off—in books, stars, sects. Late have I loved Thee… but love at last awakened by no wisdom of mine, but by Thy mercy drawing me home through all my errant thoughts.

question: You spoke of the Manichees' 'nation of darkness'—why did you find their idea of an opposing mass to God logically absurd?
answer:
O Lord, in the long wanderings of my soul through the labyrinths of philosophy, I sought Thee not as Thou art, but as I imagined a god must be—bounded by matter, warring with shadows, divided against Himself. And so I fell with the Manichees into a fatal dream: that light and darkness were twin powers, locked in eternal strife, each feeding on the other’s strength. But if Thou art God—*true* God—then Thou art unchangeable, impassible, whole in Thyself from eternity; and no darkness can approach Thee, much less injure Thee. For what is darkness but the absence of light? It cannot act; it cannot resist; it has no substance of its own to rise up against Thee.

If they say this "nation of darkness" harmed Thee or Thy substance, then Thou art not God—for God cannot suffer harm. But if it harmed Thee not, why then this cosmic struggle? Why send forth a portion of Thy very being—souls mingled with alien natures not made by Thee? As though Thy Substance could be torn like flesh, corrupted like metal, trapped like a bird in thorns! And then Thy Word—the very Wisdom by which all things were made—must needs descend into that mire as a rescuer made of the same stuff as the fallen! Then both are mutable; then neither is truly divine.

But Thou didst teach me in the silence after pride: *There is no mass opposed to God.* Evil is not a substance; it is disorder—a turning away. As the eye sees all colors but cannot see itself unless it turns inward, so the soul sees evil only when it turns from Thee. There is no eternal battle because Thou hast already conquered by existing—by being Goodness Itself. Late have I understood: there can be no kingdom beside Thee, for all that *is*, exists only through Thee—and whatever resists Thee simply *is not*, in truth.

question: When you asked what harm the darkness could do if God refused to fight it, were you implying that God's immutability makes conflict beneath Him?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my heart with a single word in the garden, when the voice of a child chanting *“Take up and read”* shattered my long resistance—how blind I was, even in my pride of intellect, to suppose that Thy Majesty could be threatened by any shadow!  

When I questioned what harm the darkness might do Thee if Thou refused to fight, it was not from wisdom but from the lingering shadow of Manichean fables still clouding my soul. I imagined a war of powers, as though evil were a substance co-eternal with Thee, as though light and darkness were twin principles locked in combat. But now I see—and tremble at my former folly—Thou art not engaged in battle; Thou art Being itself. How can non-being wound Being? How can darkness injure light? It is not conflict that befits Thee, O God most high, but creation ex nihilo and re-creation of what has fallen.

The very question betrayed my confusion: for if God can suffer harm, then He is not God. And if He must struggle to preserve His dominion, then His dominion is not absolute. But Thou dost not strive; Thou art. Evil is not Thy rival but Thy creature’s turning away—from Thee, the only Good. It has no nature of its own; it is privation, wound, distortion—not a realm opposed to Thee, but a sickness within what Thou hast made.

And so Thy Word did not descend into battle as one champion among others; He came down like rain upon dry earth—not to wrestle with dust but to quicken it. Not because Thou wert in peril—but because we were in ruin. Not because darkness could overcome Thee—but because we had let it master us.

Late have I understood: immutability does not mean indifference; it means faithfulness beyond our frailty.

question: You criticized the notion that a portion of God's Substance could be corrupted—did you reject this because it compromises God's oneness and perfection?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art never diminished, never invaded, never in peril—how could I do aught but reject that fable of a wounded God, a God whose Substance might be seized by darkness and defiled? For if any particle of Thee could suffer change or stain from an alien power, then Thou art not God. Thou wouldst be bounded, Thine eternity breached, Thine immortality made fragile. And what is worship but the soul’s surrender to the Unchangeable? If Thou canst alter for the worse, if Thy very Substance may be mingled with chaos not of Thy making—then where is the Rock on which we stand?

The Manichees spoke of souls as fragments torn from Thee, dragged down into matter like sparks fallen into mire. But if the spark were truly of Thy Substance, how could it rot? How could what is divine begin to decay? Either it was never divine—or worse still—that divinity itself is subject to ruin. And thus they made Thee not the Creator of all things but a warrior contending with a rival kingdom older than time: a god not almighty but embattled, not whole but in part lost and needing rescue.

But my heart groaned under such absurdity! For when at last I came to understand Thee—not through their dualisms, but through Paul’s letters opened as if by Thy hand—I saw: there is no mass opposed to Thee which Thou didst not call from nothing. Darkness is no substance; it is the absence of light. Evil is not a thing; it is the turning away from Goodness. Nothing resists Thee save what Thou hast permitted; nothing exists that Thou hast not created good in its nature—even if corrupted by willful turning.

And so I confessed: no member of Thee suffers. No fragment of Thy Substance wanders lost in matter. It is we who have wandered—not severed from Thy Body like limbs cut off—but turned our faces from Thee in pride and lust. And yet Thou didst not leave us there. Late have I loved Thee… but even in my fall, it was toward Thee that my restlessness drove me—for Thou wast more inward than my very inwardness, higher than my highest thought.

question: Why did you focus on the soul as the supposed offspring of God's Substance in the Manichean view, and how did that lead you to see their system as flawed?
answer:
O Lord, it was not by climbing higher in the heavens of speculation that I came down upon the truth, but by descending into the depths of my own soul, where I found not a spark of divine substance entangled in darkness, but a will bent upon itself, restless not because it bore fragments of Thee imprisoned in matter, but because it had turned away from Thee, its sole rest.  

When I once believed—nay, fervently taught—that the soul was of Thy very Substance, scattered and suffering under the dominion of some alien night, I made Thee captive to time and war. If our souls are pieces of Thee torn off and trapped, then Thou art divisible; and if divisible, then mutable; and if mutable in that way, then not God. For what is God if He can lose parts of Himself? If He fights as one opposed, fearing harm—if His own offspring can be defiled by powers co-eternal with Him—then where is His sovereignty? Where His eternity? That dualism offered me a world explained by battle between two gods: one good but limited, one evil but strong. But Thou didst whisper through silence: *I AM is not divided.*  

I saw at last that their error was not only cosmological but moral: they excused sin as necessity. If darkness be as old as light, then lust is not rebellion but fate; theft not vice but vibration of the cosmic body. And yet deep within me cried out that which they could not explain—the shame. Why should I weep for stolen pears unless I knew them to be rightly Thine? Why should my heart ache with longing for purity if corruption were my nature? No fragment lost can long for wholeness unless it remembers unity—not as particle trapped in matter, but as creature turned from its Creator.

Thus their doctrine crumbled before the weight of conscience more than argument. For Thou didst prove Thy presence not by leaving sparks of divinity caught in shadows—but by making even this sinner mourn his sins and whisper through tears: "Be Thou exalted above all."

question: You argued that if darkness couldn't hurt God, there was no reason for the struggle—did you mean to expose the incoherence in their dualistic narrative?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when I sought truth among the shadows and mistook appetite for wisdom, I clung to the Manichees’ tale of light and darkness as though it were a ladder to heaven—yet all the while, their doctrine trapped me in a maze without a center.  

Their story demanded a battle between two eternal powers, yet collapsed under the weight of its own contradiction: if God could not be harmed by darkness, why descend into conflict at all? And if He *could* be harmed, then He was not God. To imagine that some fragment of His very being—like the soul—could wander from Him, be defiled by foreign natures not made by Him, and then need redemption from within the fray, was to make the Divine subject to division and decay. But what is God if not unchangeable? If not whole? If not beyond mixture and corruption?  

No, O Lord, Thou art not drawn into combat as though Thine honor needed defense or Thy substance could be tainted. The struggle is not Thine by necessity but ours by blindness. The darkness Thou dost conquer is not a rival power but a privation—like blindness in the eye—that vanishes at the mere presence of light. That light shone in my own heart when Thou didst break through my pride; not with thunder, but with silence; not with force, but with tears. How clearly I saw then: there is no opposing mass eternally locked with Thee—the only real war is within the soul that resists love.

question: You mention Nebridius's argument against those who claim Your Substance could be corruptible—why did you find this argument strong enough to condemn such beliefs as 'execrable' and 'revolting'?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard Nebridius unchain the logic of Thy immutability like a sword piercing through smoke, I trembled—not with fear of man, but with relief that Thou hadst not left us to grope in darkness spun from our own diseased imaginations. For what horror is this, that the very Substance of God—He by whom all substances exist—should be subject to decay? If Thy essence could rot, could shift, could be defiled, then all creation stands on quicksand and the soul has no anchor but delusion. Those men who taught such things did not merely err; they vomited forth sacrilege cloaked as wisdom, making Thee—Thou who art Being itself—a creature subject to time and corruption.

I knew even then, though my mind still staggered under the weight of unanswered questions about evil, that to make Thee mutable was to destroy Thee. And yet I also knew from within my own wretchedness—how easily I changed for the worse, how swiftly desire bent my will—that whatever God is, He must be untouched by such flux. To say "God changes" is to say "God ceases to be God." Nebridius saw plainly what pride had blinded others to: if Thou art not immutable, then Thou art not God at all—but some idol carved in the image of our fears and falling.

Thus their doctrine was not merely false; it was execrable because it dragged the Highest beneath the level of the lowest things. It made the Creator less sure than dust. And so I recoiled—not only as a mind corrected, but as a soul that had once wandered among such shadows and barely escaped with breath—knowing full well how near I myself had come to worshipping a god who could fall apart.

question: You were convinced that God is undefilable and unalterable—how did this conviction shape your resistance to the idea that God could be the source of evil?
answer:
O Lord, when my mind was still a battlefield of philosophies and my heart a restless wanderer among shadows, I could not bear the thought that Thy pure and unshaken Majesty might be entangled with the foulness of evil.  

For if Thou art what Thou hast revealed Thyself to be—the fountain of all being, the light that darkens not, the Substance untouched by decay—then to say that evil flows from Thee is to say that light generates darkness by its own radiance, or that health breeds disease within its own breath. Such madness I once flirted with, O God, in the pride of my Manichaean days, when I imagined two warring principles locked in eternal strife, as though Truth itself were divided against itself.  

But Thou didst teach me this: that which is truly immutable cannot be the author of corruption, for to act is not to suffer change. If evil were a substance, a thing in itself springing from Thee, then Thou must have formed it—willed it—become defiled by it. But how could the incorruptible bring forth corruption? How could the wholly whole generate what is broken? My soul recoiled at the very notion, not merely because reason condemned it, but because love for Thee forbade it.  

I saw at last that evil is not a substance at all—but a privation: blindness in sight, silence in song, death in life. It is not something made *by* Thee so much as something *lacking* in what was made. And thus I escaped the blasphemy of making Thee dual-natured or mutable; I escaped by clinging—though weakly at first—to this one truth: Thou dost not shift; Thou dost not stain; Thou art ever what Thou art.  

And so my search for evil led me not into rebellion against Thy justice but into deeper awe before it—for how great must be Thy goodness if even our falling cannot alter Thee!

question: When you say you did not yet understand the cause of evil, what specific confusion troubled you about evil's origin, given your belief in an immutable God?
answer:
O Lord, I was like a man groping in the dim light before dawn, my hands brushing against shadows I could not name, for though I turned my soul wholly toward Thee—acknowledging Thee immutable, uncreated, the highest Good from whom all substance flows—I stumbled still at the dark stain of evil. How could corruption exist, I asked, in a world poured forth from Thy perfect hand? If all that is came from Thee, and Thou art wholly good, then whence came this will that delights in sin, this weight that drags the soul downward not by compulsion but by choice?  

I could not bear the thought of any substance co-eternal with Thee—some rival principle of darkness fighting light—for that would shatter Thy oneness and make of Thee a part rather than the Whole. Yet if evil had no substance of its own, what was it but a privation, a falling away? A wound in the eye cannot be seen except through sight failing—but who made that failure? Not Thou, O God; for Thou didst create the eye to see. So too the soul: Thou formed it upright, but it turned aside into crookedness not of necessity but by its own willful love of lesser goods.  

Yet even there lay confusion: how could free will—Thy gift—turn so violently against Thee? Was not freedom itself corrupted at its root? And if so, must not corruption have entered even what Thou pronounced good? My mind reeled like a ship without anchor. I feared that to explain evil might force me to imagine Thee as mutable—that Thou hadst somehow failed to foresee or prevent it—and such thought was abhorrent. For if Thou change not, how then dost allow such ruin within creation without being its author?

Only slowly did it dawn upon me: evil has no nature of its own; it is not a thing made but a turning away from what is made. It is darkness—not a substance but the absence of light. And yet this absence is real in its effects: lusts tearing apart friendship; pride shattering humility; theft where there was trust—all these were wounds caused by love misdirected. Not because Thy creation was flawed from within by some contrary power—but because souls made for Thee chose to rest in created things instead.

Late have I understood: evil arises when love grows disordered—when we love less than what is highest as though it were highest. But O Lord! Before that clarity came many nights of storm within me—a heart restless not only for salvation but for truth uncorrupted as Thyself.

question: Why did you fear that seeking the cause of evil might make you 'become that evil,' and how did that fear influence your philosophical inquiry?
answer:
O Lord, who knowest the labyrinthine twists of my heart even as I turned it toward Thee in restless darkness, I confess that the very act of seeking evil’s origin filled me with dread—as though in probing its roots, I might let its poison seep into my soul and defile the image of Thy truth I longed to behold. For I saw then, though dimly, that if evil had no source outside Thee—if it sprang from some flaw or change within Thy substance—then Thou wouldst not be God. And yet, to deny Thee as immutable was to fall into madness or blasphemy; it was to make Thee subject to time, to decay, to passion: a god made in the image of man’s suffering.

Thus did my fear guard me: not because I shunned inquiry, but because I trembled lest in solving one riddle I should commit a greater sacrilege. To suppose that Thou couldst be altered by evil was itself an evil thought—for it diminished Thy Godhead, made Thee less than eternal, less than perfect. And so I wrestled not only with the question *whence comes evil?* but with the peril of my own mind: that in seeking an answer, I might fashion a god who changed, who suffered—who sinned—and call him *Thee*.

That dread shaped all my steps. It forced me to abandon facile explanations—the Manichaean phantasm of two warring principles, co-eternal and opposed—for they placed upon Thee limits and wounds. It drove me back upon myself: not to study some external shadow cast upon the world, but to gaze into the abyss of my own will; for what is evil if not the soul’s turning from light toward emptiness? Not a substance Thou hast made, O God most high; not a thing co-eternal with Thy being—but a privation: like darkness where light is absent.

And thus didst Thou teach me slowly: that sin is not something created good gone rogue beneath Thy watchful eye; no—sin is our refusal of goodness. It is love misdirected toward lesser things; it is pride lifting itself against peace; it is desire grown blind and gnawing at what cannot satisfy.

Had I allowed myself to imagine Thee mutable for the sake of explaining this horror—I should have become partaker in its essence. For he who conceives God as changeable becomes unstable himself; he who supposes Deity can be defiled already dwells among defilements. So Thou preserved me even in error—by fear where wisdom failed—to keep me longing still for Him who changes not: Thou art ever He Who Is.

question: You describe false teachings as 'vomited out of the overcharged stomach'—what did you mean by this vivid image, and how did it reflect your view of spiritual and intellectual purity?
answer:
O Lord, I once devoured these errors with the greed of a starving man who knows not what poisons his meal, and my soul was sick with the excess of empty words. That image—of false teachings vomited from an overcharged stomach—rose not from mere scorn, but from the visceral memory of how I myself had gorged upon the specious doctrines of the Manichees, swallowing their fables whole, believing them to nourish wisdom when they only fed pride. They claimed to offer light, yet their teaching was a darkness that delighted in its own complexity, weaving myths about God as if He were bound by matter, subject to change and corruption. But Thou art not so: Thou art Being itself, unmade, unmoved, untouched by decay—and any doctrine that would make Thee mutable is not merely mistaken; it is abhorrent, a spiritual sickness demanding purgation.

Such fables cannot dwell in a soul that has tasted truth; they must be cast out violently, like poison wrung from the body. Just as the stomach rebels against overindulgence and expels what it cannot digest, so must the mind and heart reject teachings that defile the purity of faith. For how could Thy Substance—the very ground of all being—be alterable? If it were, then all things would collapse into chaos; even truth would shift beneath our feet. And yet I confess: though I rejected their madness intellectually, my heart still stumbled in shadows. The question *where evil came from* tormented me—not because I doubted Thy goodness or power (for I knew Thou wert Creator of all), but because my mind still strained to grasp how evil could exist if all that is comes from Thee.

But this inquiry I dared not pursue at the cost of making Thee mutable—even that mystery was less terrible than imagining God subject to change. Far better to say *I do not know*, than to invent lies about Thee under pretense of understanding. So long as my ignorance preserved Thy immutability, I walked humbly; for even in confusion, it is safer to rest in wonder before Thy face than to force answers that profane Thy Name. Evil exists—but it is no substance; it is privation, a turning away from Thee who art Light. And every heresy which attributes real substance to evil or imagines corruption within God Himself deserves not debate but expulsion—for such thoughts are not food for pilgrims on the way to peace.

question: You said you were certain that your will was your own when you willed or nilled something—how could such a free will come from You, when You are goodness itself and yet I use it to choose evil?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art the light of the minds that know Thee, how could dust and shadow such as I fathom the depths of that abyss where freedom meets creation? I trembled at the thought: if my will is mine, and I am Thine—wholly from Thee, wholly dependent on Thee—how came this power within me to turn from Thee, not merely to err in ignorance, but to delight in rebellion?

I knew myself—I *was* myself—when I chose. In that act of willing or refusing, there was no deception: it was *I* who stretched forth my desire toward what pleased me, even when it wounded me. And yet Thou didst not force Thyself into my choices; Thou gavest being to my soul and with it the breath of self-motion. For what is a creature truly made in Thy image if not one who can turn toward Thee—or away—not by compulsion, but by love?

But O God, how bitter was that liberty when first I tasted its misuse! It was not another voice speaking through me; it was not some foreign substance planted by a hostile god (as once I foolishly believed), but *my own* consent that darkened the good Thou hadst formed. Thy gift—free will—was good; but I turned it downward, like one using a noble eye not to gaze upon the stars but to leer at filth.

And so when I asked, "Who made me?" and answered "God," and then cried out "Why then do I sin?"—I failed at first to see that corruption enters not from Thee who givest being, but from me who pervert it. Like a singer given a perfect instrument and trained voice who yet chooses discordant notes—not because his master taught disharmony, but because he loves dissonance for its novelty—I became unjust while serving justice no longer.

Thou didst not make evil; Thou allowedst it by allowing *me*, for love cannot be forced. And so this will of mine—so small a spark compared to Thy eternal fire—is still free enough to say “no” even unto ruin. But blessed be Thy name: that same will Thou hast also drawn back by grace more powerful than all my resistance.

question: When you spoke of Thy substance suffering ill, what exactly troubled you about the Manichean idea that God could be harmed by evil?
answer:
In the fever of my youth, when I wandered through the gardens of rhetoric and the shadows of Manichaean fables, I fancied God bound within the flux of this world—as though He were a body pierced by darkness, a substance wearied by conflict, a light that might be smothered. But O Lord, what horror struck my soul when I saw them speak of *Thee* as wounded! They taught that evil was not our rebellion but a poison in Thy very being, a tear in the fabric of the Divine—so that God Himself suffered violation at the hands of darkness.  

This could not be. For if Thou art God—unchanging, eternal, perfect—then suffering implies mutation: a before and after, a lack and then fulfillment, an injury and then healing. But Thou hast no before or after; Thou art ever complete. To say that Thy substance suffers is to make Thee subject to time and decay; it is to place Thee within creation rather than as its source. And thus their doctrine did not exalt Thee—it diminished Thee. It made Thee less than God.

Worse still: they comforted themselves with this fiction so they might excuse their own sinfulness. "Not we," they said, "but God is harmed," as if to claim that evil resided not in their wills but in Thine own nature—that darkness was woven into Thee rather than cast by us in our turning away. So they fled from guilt by making Thee complicit in ruin!  

But my soul recoiled: for I knew even then that sin was mine—not forced upon me, not injected from without like poison into flesh—but willed by me. When I stole fruit I did not hunger for, when I lusted after fleeting pleasures, when I sought applause instead of truth—I did these things *willingly*. No shadow from outside compelled me; it was my own heart that chose disorder.

And so Thou didst teach me: evil is not a substance opposing Thee—it is a privation where good should be; it is blindness in the soul’s eye; it is love turned downward toward things that pass. And thus Thy justice remains pure—not because Thou repays harm done to Thyself like some wronged king—but because Thou dost restore order to our shattered wills.

O Lord, hadst Thou been mutable, where would have been my hope? If even *Thou* couldst fall into darkness—what light could ever rise again? But Thou art unshaken—and therefore Thou couldst draw me up from that pit where I floundered so long; and at last let me hear: *"Take up and read."*

question: You mentioned being repeatedly drawn back into the 'deep pit' while trying to understand sin—what was it about the struggle itself that kept you from clarity, even as you sensed the truth?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered in the labyrinth of my own making, deceived not by darkness alone, but by the false light I mistook for truth — the pride that whispered I could climb to Thee by argument alone, as if my mind were a ladder and not a limb weakened by its own rebellion.  

I felt the truth like a breath upon my cheek, yet when I turned to behold it, it vanished — not because it fled, but because my heart was still turned elsewhere, still clinging to the sweetness of disordered loves. The very act of striving upward with my intellect became another snare when done apart from humility; for I sought Thee as one solves a riddle, rather than as a sinner throws himself before mercy.  

The struggle held me fast because within it I was at war with myself: knowing that Thou art just, yet resentful under correction; confessing free will with my tongue while blaming Thee in secret for the wound of sin. Each time I glimpsed that *I* was the cause — not some substance Thou hadst made evil, nor fate nor star nor devil — I recoiled. For to admit that I had freely turned away was to admit that all excuse was gone. And who desires such nakedness before God?  

Thus did I fall back into the pit: not from lack of reason, but from love — a disordered love that cherished its own confusion more than Thy light. To see clearly would have required surrender; and surrender meant death to the proud thinker within me. So long as I clung to myself as author and judge, grace could not enter — though it stood knocking at the door of my soul with wounds more eloquent than all philosophy.  

Only when I ceased struggling upward and began weeping in place did Thy mercy draw near; only when I said in truth: *"Thou wast within me, and I outside"* did the vision clear. Not by ascent, O Lord, but by descent — into contrition, into childhood again — didst Thou raise me up.

question: You judged actions done against your will to be a punishment rather than a fault—how then did you reconcile that with the belief that original sin had already corrupted your nature?
answer:
In the quiet ruin of my own heart, where lust reigned and ambition whispered lies even in prayer, I came to see that my nature was not merely bent by habit, but broken from the root — not as if God had made it crooked, but because I had fallen with him who first turned away from Thee, O Lord, in pride.  

I once flattered myself that sin was only what I willed — a conscious choice, a deliberate step into darkness — and therefore thought myself innocent when passion overtook me or when despair dragged me where I did not wish to go. “This,” I said, “is not my doing; it is my suffering.” And so it was — yet not without cause. For why did I yield so easily? Why did desire burn so strong within me that even when my mind disapproved, my flesh obeyed another law?

It was then that Thy light pressed upon me: if I lived by Thee and fell from Thee, then the wound was mine though the fall began before my breath. Not that Thy hand created evil — far be it! — but that the mass of Adam’s sin had become my own condition; inherited not like a debt in coin, but like a sickness in the blood. Even infants cry in wrath at the breast, O Lord; even they have need of grace. What then of me, grown tall in vice and steeped in vanity?

I saw at last that original sin was not an excuse for unwilling faults — as though we suffer evil only from outside forces — but rather the reason why our wills are weak and our desires disordered from the beginning. We are born already inclined to rebellion; thus what we do against our better judgment is still rooted in a nature turned away from Thee. And so Thou art just both in punishing sin and in offering mercy: just because none are untouched by corruption; merciful because Thou dost heal what we could never mend.

And thus I ceased to murmur: "Why must I bear this?" Instead I cried out: "Cleanse me, O God, from my secret faults!" For even when I sinned unwillingly, there lay within me that which deserved correction — a nature unmoored from its rest. Only grace could re-anchor it.

question: You acknowledged God as the maker of all things good, yet wondered why He made you capable of willing evil—why did this question trouble you so deeply, and how did it shape your view of divine justice?
answer:
O Lord, in the fever of my mind I turned over and over the truth that Thou art wholly good, and that all things made by Thee bear the mark of Thy handiwork—yet there within me, as close as breath, lay a will that recoiled from Thee even while it drew life from Thee. This was no mere puzzle of logic; it tore at the marrow of my soul, for if Thou art the Artificer of my nature, why then doth this nature swerve toward darkness? Why dost Thou implant in me a power to choose, only to see it twist against its source?

I confess: I once thought justice must mean coercion—that if Thou wert truly God, Thou wouldst compel all hearts instantly to goodness. But this was folly born of pride, mistaking grace for force. What I could not grasp was that freedom is not a flaw in creation but its glory—and sin not a substance from Thee but a turning away from Thee. I raged against the mystery: "If Thou made me," I cried silently to Heaven, "why didst Thou not make me otherwise?" Yet even in my questioning, I trembled before Thy justice—for already in my conscience I knew: what I did against others had its root not in Thy making but in my marring.

And so the very weight of guilt became a mirror. When I lusted where I ought to have loved; when I stole what was not mine merely to taste rebellion; when ambition gnawed at my peace like rust upon iron—I could not blame Thee. For even then, amid the ruin, there stood within me an unyielding witness: *I willed this*. No demon forced my hand; no fatal star bent my desire. It was *I*, O Lord—a creature formed by Thee for communion with light—who chose shadow.

Thus Thy justice ceased to seem cruel and began to dawn as mercy itself: for if sin were thrust upon us from without or upward from creation’s roots, then punishment would be tyranny. But because we fall by our own volition—because we turn away while still upheld by Thy breath—then judgment is not vengeance but restoration. Like fire purging dross from gold, Thy justice seeks not our ruin but our return.

Late have I understood that which now seems plain: Thou didst give us will so that love might be real. And love cannot be love unless it chooses Thee freely—even as I at last chose Thee when grace stripped away my excuses and left only shame and longing side by side.

question: You said that God is not only good but goodness itself — why then did you struggle with the idea that He could allow you to will evil, if He formed you entirely?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who art the unshakable light in which no darkness dwells, I once raged against the shadow I found within my own will, as though it could not have sprung from Thee unless Thou wert its author. I could not bear that I, made wholly by Thy sweet hand, should turn so fiercely toward vanity and delight in sin; and yet there I was—stealing for no gain, lusting without love, speaking to win applause while my soul starved in silence. How could the work of Thy perfect goodness contain such perversity? It seemed a blasphemy to accuse Thee, yet a torment to excuse myself.

I fled into philosophy at first, weaving nets of words to catch the truth: if evil is corruption, then it cannot be from Thee, since Thou art incorruptible; if evil is nothingness, then it has no substance to be created. And yet—there was the will, mine and yet not mine, twisted like a branch grown backward from a good root. I had not been forced into sin; I had leapt into it with joy. That joy shamed me most of all—for it was real pleasure in what was wrong.

So long as I looked for a substance of evil—a thing made by some dark craftsman—I remained lost. But when at last I saw that evil is not a thing but a turning away—from Thee, from form, from order—then my heart began to bend back also. The devil fell not because his nature was corrupted by another but because he willed what was less than God as though it were greater. And I? I did the same each time I chose fleeting delight over eternal Truth.

Thou didst not ingraft bitterness into me; rather, in allowing me free will—the power to turn toward or away from Thee—Thou permitted the possibility of that sour fruit. For love forced is no love at all. And what am I now but proof of Thy mercy: one who wandered far and found only ruin in himself—and then heard Thy voice calling through the wreckage? Late have I understood: Thou art not the cause of my evil will; but Thou art always the remedy.
