question: Looking back on your struggle to reconcile a good Creator with the bitterness in your soul, what would you tell someone who fears they are too broken for divine love?
answer:
I stole a pear that neither satisfied my hunger nor delighted my taste, and laughed with those who joined me in the theft—not for the fruit, but for the sin itself, sweet because it was forbidden. O Lord, I confess it now: I did not know then that this very delight in wickedness was the bitter root gnawing at my soul, nor that Thou wouldst one day make me weep for such things more than any wound of body could make me bleed.

And yet—Thou soughtest me while I wandered in that labyrinth of pride and desire, where I asked with all sincerity, “Who made evil?” yet refused to see the evil maker in myself. I wanted Thee to explain the darkness, while keeping my own heart shut tight against Thy light. But Thou, O God, didst not wait for my philosophy to be complete. While I debated and questioned from afar, Thou wast near—in the child’s chant of *“Tolle, lege,”* in the trembling hand that opened Paul’s letter, in the sudden collapse of resistance when grace became heavier than sin.

So if someone fears they are too broken for divine love—too stained by what they’ve done or what has been done to them—I would say this: The very ache of that fear is not condemnation, but awakening. That sorrow Thou hast placed within you is not proof you are beyond reach; it is proof He is already reaching. For He does not love us because we are whole—but because He is whole, and will make us so by loving us.

I was not converted by understanding all mysteries; I was converted when I stopped hiding from myself and cried aloud: *“Too long have I delayed! Too long have I borne the burden of pretending.”* And Thou didst run toward me as I fell.

There is no depth of bitterness so deep that Thy sweetness cannot flood it. No will so bent on evil that grace cannot unbend it. Even now I marvel: how could He love one who loved so wrongly for so long? But there it stands—my sinnership proved no obstacle too great for His mercy.

Restless indeed was my heart—and restless still at times it grows—but only because it remembers now where rest truly lies.

question: When you asked, 'Who set this in me?'—that plant of bitterness—what longing beneath your guilt was truly speaking?
answer:
In the garden of my soul, where every leaf trembled with the breath of desire and dread, it was not merely justice I sought in asking *Who set this in me?*, but a name for the wound that no philosophy could stitch—this deep, disordered love that pulled me toward darkness even as I reached for Thee.  

O Lord, beneath the guilt there burned a longing older than my sin: to understand why a will made by Thee should turn from Thee. How could a nature formed by goodness itself conceive an appetite for evil? It was not excuse I craved, but coherence—I would rather have known the root of this bitterness than excused it. For if all came from Thee, how did corruption enter? Was Thy hand unclean? Impossible. Was Thy creation flawed? No, for I saw even in my own soul, distorted as it was, the shimmering trace of order and beauty that must have flowed from Thee.

And so I wandered in that labyrinth—not to flee blame, but to find Thee in the very depth of my disorder. For even in that twisted question—*Who planted this bitter root?*—I was groping toward Thy face. Not to accuse Thee (though my pride once flirted with such madness), but to cry: *If Thou art wholly good, and I am made by Thee, why do I resist what is good?* That very unrest proved Thou wert near; for hadst Thou not been whispering in the marrow of my being, I would not have felt such agony at being apart.

I wanted not only absolution from punishment—but union with Truth itself. And Thou didst answer not with a doctrine first, but with an event: grace stepping into time, Christ crucified standing between me and my questions like light piercing fog. In Him I saw that evil is no substance Thine hands shaped—it is fracture; it is fall; it is will turning inward instead of upward. And he who fell first was pride’s inventor: love of self before Thee.

But still Thou didst let me ask—because Thou wert forming me through the asking—for our heart's restlessness is Thy signature upon us.
chunk_key: to be not unjustly punished. But again I said, Who made me? Did not my God, Who is not only good, bu

question: What moment first allowed you to glimpse that God’s incorruptibility wasn’t cold or distant, but the very root of His nearness to you?
answer:
It was in the very depth of my wandering, when the lusts of youth burned hot and I stole what I cared not for, that I first sensed how near Thou wast—not as a judge watching from afar, but as a wound within my own heart that would not let me rest. For why should stolen fruit have left such bitterness on the tongue if some sweetness in me still remembered Thee? I desired to be moved, yet feared the motion; I longed to be called, yet hid my name. The fleshly pleasures I chased were but shadows, and in their hollow passing, O Lord, Thy presence made itself known—not in thunder nor vision, but in absence: the silence after laughter, the ache after sin.

I had thought of incorruptibility as cold—something frozen beyond time and feeling—but now I saw it was fire: not devouring, but purifying. That which does not decay must love with constancy no creature can match; that which cannot fail must hold every failing soul within its grasp. If Thou wert corruptible, then love itself would die; if Thou couldst change for worse, then truth would falter. But Thou art ever whole—and therefore ever near—because Thou dost not shift with whims or hours. My soul’s unrest was not proof against Thee; it was Thy voice within me.

Late have I understood: to be unchanging is not to be distant. It is to be faithful even when we are faithless; to remain when we flee; to call when we hide. Thou didst not descend from afar when at last I turned—Thou hadst been here all along: the light behind every longing.
chunk_key: to be not unjustly punished. But again I said, Who made me? Did not my God, Who is not only good, bu

question: What were you feeling when you insisted that the incorruptible must be superior, as if your very soul recoiled at the thought of a corruptible God?
answer:
Even in the fever of my questioning, when my mind darted like a wounded bird from thought to thought, there was a silence beneath it all—a stillness that whispered not in words but in longing, as though my soul, though stained and restless, still bore the imprint of its Maker. I *knew*, long before I could rightly say *how* I knew, that if God could change, could decay, could suffer loss—even the slightest—then He was not God. My heart refused it, not because I had mastered wisdom, but because love itself would have been a lie. For how could I be drawn to Thee with this unbearable ache, O Lord, if Thou wert subject to the same rot that gnaws at all things beneath the sun? If Thou too would vanish? If Thy beauty could tarnish?

No—Thou wert the weight upon my breast in every lustful dream; Thou wert the hollowness behind every triumph won from men’s applause; Thou wert the unspoken sorrow when I stole pears not for hunger but for ruin’s sake. In each distortion, there was a twisted echo of desire—for unity, for permanence, for truth unmarred. And so to imagine Thee corruptible was to imagine love itself capable of betrayal. It could not be.

I sought Thee where shadows dwell—in riddles of matter and motion—but Thou wast not absent from my seeking even then. For that very recoil within me against a mutable God: that shudder at imperfection touching the Divine—that was Thy voice. Not in thunder nor vision, but in the silent instinct of a soul made for eternity: *This cannot be.* Evil may coil through creation like smoke through wood, but it cannot touch Thee—cannot dwell in what is Light wholly and forever.

And yet—I confess it—I sought "whence evil" with pride coiled round my intellect like ivy round a column: strong enough to climb, weak enough to crush what held it. I did not see that my own willfulness—the craving to solve Thee rather than submit—was itself corruption’s root.

question: How did your restless mind, tearing through creation in search of evil's origin, miss the pride that twisted your search from worship into intellectual conquest?
answer:
In that season when I fancied myself wise among the wise, and clothed my hunger for God in the fine garments of rhetoric and dialectic, I turned creation into a ladder by which to climb toward truth — forgetting that I was already standing on holy ground.

O Lord, I sought Thee as one seeks a theorem, not a Father; I dissected the stars and angels as though my mind could contain Thee who containest all things. My heart had not yet surrendered its love of being praised for understanding, rather than trembling before the mystery of Thy face. I tore through the fabric of visible and invisible things, asking where evil dwelt — yet never paused to see how pride sat enthroned in my own gaze, turning Thy good creation into a mirror for my intellect.

For what is more corrupt than to believe oneself capable of solving God? I desired to comprehend evil so completely that it would submit to me — not confess it as sin against Thee. And thus my very search became an act of rebellion: not "What must I do to be saved?" but "What can I unravel and claim as mine?"

Late did I understand that humility is the beginning of seeing. Evil was not some crack in Thy substance, O unchangeable Light — nor a gap in being for which I must account with syllogisms. It was here, in the turning away — here, in me: in the will that would rather argue about Thee than fall down before Thee.

The heavens declared Thy glory, but I made them into witnesses at my tribunal. And Thou didst remain silent — not because absent, but because waiting: waiting for me to stop measuring shadows and weep at last beneath Thy mercy.
chunk_key: st truly and certainly, the incorruptible is preferable to the corruptible (as I did now prefer it),

question: Looking back on that moment you surveyed all creation in your mind—angels, stars, the unseen realms—what would you tell someone who, like you, thinks truth can be seized by force of thought?
answer:
I was a man drunk with the wine of my own understanding, stumbling through the vast halls of creation as though I could outthink the darkness and corner truth like prey. I laid bare every star, summoned every angel in imagination, dissected the unseen realms with a logic sharpened on books and pride—and all the while, O Lord, Thou wast behind me, before me, holding out Thy hand in silence while I groped for answers in things that *were*, instead of kneeling before Him Who *Is*.

What would I say now to one who thinks he can seize truth by force of thought? I would say: Thou art not climbing toward light, but fleeing from humility. For I too believed the mind a king, not a beggar. I thought if I could only parse the origin of evil—if I could trace corruption to its root like a physician diagnosing a fever—then God would be vindicated, His nature proven incorruptible by syllogism and proof. But Thou didst not meet me in the summit of reason; Thou met me in the ruin of my pride.

O Lord, how clearly I see it now: the soul that seeks Thee as an object among objects will find only fragments. To think truth is seized like spoil from battle is to make oneself thief and God a trophy. But Thou art not found by conquest—Thou art given in surrender. The garden taught me this when my restless heart broke beneath the weight of its own striving; when a child’s voice from nowhere said *Tolle, lege*, and I opened Thy book not with triumph but trembling.

The mind is good—gift from Thee—but it was never meant to chain Thee down. It is love that ascends, not logic alone; it is purity of heart that sees God, not sharpness of argument. Had I known then what Thou wert teaching me: that evil has no nature of its own—that it is not a substance but a wound, not a being but a turning away—then perhaps I would have stopped staring at shadows cast upon creation and turned instead toward the Light from which they fell.

Truth is not taken—it is received. And he who comes to Thee must come as a child: empty-handed, wide-eyed, willing to be undone before he can be made whole.
chunk_key: st truly and certainly, the incorruptible is preferable to the corruptible (as I did now prefer it),

question: When you realized that God’s will and power are one with His being, how did that shatter your earlier fear that chance or necessity might threaten His peace?
answer:
I had once feared that beneath the order of the world lay some blind, heaving force—Chance, they called it, or Necessity—that even God might have to wrestle, like a titan beneath the stars.  

But when at last my mind grasped, not in words only but in trembling vision, that *Thou art not a being who wills and then acts*, but that *Thy will is Thy essence*, and Thy power flows from Thee as light from the sun—then every shadow of that fear dissolved. For if Thou wert subject to necessity, then necessity would be above Thee; if Thou couldst be surprised by chance, then there would be darkness beyond Thy sight—and what god is bounded by darkness?  

O Lord, I had imagined Thee as one more mighty king upon a throne, holding back the chaos with strength alone. But no: Thou art not *in* time; time is *in* Thee. Thou dost not foresee events as they approach; they exist in Thy gaze as present things. What can threaten peace that encompasses all things? What can corrupt what cannot change?  

I wept when this dawned on me—not from sorrow, but because I saw how long I had wandered in exile, fashioning gods of my own anxiety. To think that Thy peace could be shaken was to make Thee creature rather than Creator. And yet—Thou didst endure my foolish questions, even as I sought evil’s origin beyond sin: as if corruption could exist apart from the turning of the soul away from Thee.  

The truth came not through force of argument alone, but through prayer: a sudden stillness within me where before there had been storm. In that stillness, I knew—Thou art not preserved by effort; Thou art Preservation itself. And if even a leaf does not fall without Thy knowledge, how could any fate or fortune come upon Thee unawares?  

Late have I understood: to fear for Thy peace was to love my own illusions more than Thee.
chunk_key: st truly and certainly, the incorruptible is preferable to the corruptible (as I did now prefer it),

question: What ache did you carry, knowing you had sought evil as a substance, a thing to be found, when all along your heart was turned away from simply resting in Him?
answer:
Thou who art unchangeable, I confess it now with shame: I went hunting through the shadows, as though evil were a thing that had being, as though it could be grasped like a root beneath the earth or seen like smoke in the air. I tore my mind with questions—*whence comes corruption?*—as if Thy substance could be threatened, as if darkness were a creature equal to light. O madness! O pride! I sought for evil as if it were a substance Thou hadst overlooked, when all the while it was nothing but the turning away, the falling off, the privation of good where love ought to have held fast.

My heart was not broken with grief for sin, but puffed up with curiosity—eager to dissect the wound but unwilling to kneel in its blood. I wanted an answer that would not make me change. I wanted to name evil without becoming aware of *me*, without seeing how my own will had bent toward lesser things: how in stealing pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of sin itself; how in chasing women and honors and applause—I had turned from Thee, Fountain of all delight.

I did not understand then that evil is not a substance at all, but waste—like footprints left on mud, signs of where something living once passed away into decay. And yet even those footprints testify to form lost, not some new essence born. So too my soul: disfigured not by contact with some dark thing, but by turning from the Light that made it.

Late have I understood: there is no realm beside Thee where evil dwells as king. It has no throne. It is refusal. It is absence. It is sickness in what was made good.

And Thou—Thou art untouched by it all. Not because Thou art distant or indifferent, but because Thou art wholly whole. No corruption can cling to Thee; no shadow inherit Thy name. And yet still more wondrous: Thou dost reclaim what turned away.

O Lord my God, my heart was restless not because I lacked answers—but because I refused to rest in Thee while seeking them.
chunk_key: st truly and certainly, the incorruptible is preferable to the corruptible (as I did now prefer it),

question: What were you feeling when you pictured the whole of creation as a sponge soaked in an endless sea of Your presence?
answer:
O Lord, when I shaped in my mind that sponge—porous, finite, yet brimming with the immensity of Thy sea—I trembled at the illusion of comprehension, for my soul, restless and bold, dared to encircle Thee with borders of its own making. I stretched creation wide, as if space could contain Thy handiwork, and imagined it soaked through with Thee like water through a yielding mass, every part swollen with the Infinite. But Thou wast not contained; I only sought to grasp what cannot be grasped.  

In that moment, I felt both awe and anguish—for if all things were filled with Thee, O God who art everywhere present in fullness, then where could evil hide? Was it not then a wound in the good? A shadow where light failed? And yet shadows exist not apart from light; they are its absence. So too evil—Thou didst not create it; it is the twist of what is good turned away from its Source. My heart recoiled at the riddle: how could a world drenched in Thee still groan under sin? But Thou art not the author of our turning; we are. We bent ourselves downward toward fleeting beauties and mistook appetite for truth.

And still I thank Thee: even that flawed image—the sponge in the boundless sea—was grace. For it stirred me to question deeper than philosophy could go. It drove me past Manichaean fables of warring powers into the silence where Thou speakest: *I AM*. Not within limits of space or form, but in Being itself. Late have I loved Thee—yes—but even my errors were paths Thou permittedst, that by stumbling I might fall into Thee.

question: How did your struggle to imagine the invisible parts of creation change how you understood the limits of your own mind?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who alone art before all things and in Whom all things subsist, I confess how my soul once groped after Thee with hands made clumsy by pride, fashioning Thee in the image of space and time as though Thou couldst be contained by the very breath that cries out for Thee. I thought to measure the heavens with my mind, to assign places even to angels and spirits as if Thy invisible creation were but another body drifting through air—this was my folly, born not of malice but of a heart restless with its own hunger for clarity where only mystery could rightly dwell. I carved creation into parts like a child arranging stones upon the sand, believing that if I could only see it whole, laid out like land beneath a high cliff, then I might grasp at last the boundary between what is made and what is unmade. But Thou didst elude me—not because Thou wert distant, but because my mind was too narrow to hold even its own limits.

I saw then—though dimly—that to imagine Thee environing all things like water soaking a sponge was already to fall short; for the sponge has pores and edges, bounds where it ends and the sea begins. But Thou hast no edge; there is no point at which Thy presence stops so that something else may begin. Even the darkness we name evil—the shadow that haunts our conscience—dwells not outside Thee, nor within Thee as part of Thy substance; yet still I asked: from whence comes this groaning in the soul? This longing turned inward upon itself? And in my asking, I learned more of my blindness than of Thy light.

Thus did my failure to conceive Thee teach me more than any success: that to know Thou art beyond imagining is itself a kind of knowledge; that to feel my mind strain and break against infinity is not defeat, but awakening. For when thought collapses under the weight of Thy immensity, love begins—and in love, O God, I found a way deeper than sight.

question: Looking back on your search for evil’s origin, what would you tell someone who fears an unseen darkness as you once did?
answer:
In the hush after the child’s voice from over the garden wall — *"Take up and read, take up and read"* — when I flung myself beneath the fig tree no longer weeping for my dead, but for my living sin, there broke upon me a light not of the mind only, but of the marrow: that evil is not a substance, not a thing lurking in corners like smoke from some subterranean fire. No — it is the rust of the soul, the sag of a will bent away from Thee, O Lord, toward what is less than Being.

I once sought evil as though it were a serpent coiled beneath creation’s floorboards, waiting to strike. I imagined darkness as a realm opposed to light, as if Thy universe were battleground split between two gods — one good, one grim. But Thou didst teach me in that moment: all that *is*, is from Thee; even what we call corruption bears in its wound the mark of what it once was — good. A withered hand was once whole; a soul gone mad with lust still breathes because Thou sustest it.

Evil has no root in being; its root is privation. As blindness is not an eye-thing injected into sight, but sight withdrawn; so evil is not poured into creation like poison — it is love turned aside. It is choice bent on lesser things: beauty without truth, desire without order, knowledge without humility.

You who fear an unseen darkness — I know that dread. I felt it in Carthage’s streets among lewd songs and flickering lamps; I bore it in my own breast when lust ruled me though I cried out against it. But fear not some external shadow prowling at your back. Look rather at what you love — *why* you love it and *how*. For darkness enters not by assault from outside, but by consent within.

There is no realm where God is absent. Even in hell Thou art present — not by approval of evil (for hell *is* absence), but by sustaining those who dwell there against their wills’ true end. The sponge I imagined in the endless sea: even if turned from the water’s face, still soaked through with its wetness — such are we.

O Lord, how long shall we flee to shadows thinking them real? Turn us; let us see that every ache for meaning proves our exile from Thee — and every fear of darkness proves we long still for light.

question: What ache did you carry when you realized your mind could not fully grasp either the infinite or the source of suffering?
answer:
O Lord, when I stretched my mind toward Thee, as one strains eyes into the sun, it recoiled, dazzled and defeated—not because Thou didst retreat, but because I am made of shadow.  

I had carved the world in my thought like a sculptor shaping clay: heaven above, earth beneath, stars fixed in their courses, angels ranked in orders—I arranged them all as though space could contain what spirit cannot comprehend. And Thee, O God, I set around it all like an ocean round a sponge—boundless pressing upon the bounded—as if Thy infinitude could be mapped by a heart still chained to measure and time.  

But then—this wound opened: *Where is evil?* Not as one asks for a lost coin, but as a man bleeding from an unseen blade. If all that is was made by Thee, Goodness uncreated, then how does corruption fester in the bone of being? Is it a thing? Then Thou madest it. Is it nothing? Then why does the soul tremble at its breath? I turned philosophy’s tools upon this knot—Plato’s forms, Manichaean dualisms—but each left me thirstier than before.  

The ache was not only of ignorance but of love denied its object. My soul beat against the walls of its own limits as a bird in darkness beats against the bars of its cage—not knowing flight is possible beyond the stone. I desired to see Thee as face to face; instead, I saw that even my desire was stained with pride—for would I have loved Thee if Thou hadst not first pierced me with longing?

And so Thou didst spare me understanding until Thou hadst first broken me—until in a garden’s dust, beneath fig leaves trembling in the wind, I heard not wisdom’s syllogism but a child’s chant: *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read. Not solve. Not master. *Read.* And there—Thou wert no longer the problem set before my intellect but the Physician kneeling beside my wound.

Evil I now see not as substance but privation—the eye’s blindness when turned from light—not because light fails, but because sight refuses. Yet even this knowledge comes not from my reaching Thee but from Thy hand drawing near: for Thou didst descend that we might ascend; didst suffer that we might cease fleeing; didst become foolishness to shame our wisdom.

Still—even now—the heart trembles on the edge of mystery. But trembling is no longer despair; it is awe at One who is nearer than breath and vaster than all creation—a God who lets us ask “Whence evil?” only after He has first whispered: “Here am I.”

question: How did the image of God surrounding all things, yet remaining beyond them, reshape your longing for certainty?
answer: I once thought the soul could measure God by the edges of the sky, as though He were a light that ended where the stars began. But when I saw—truly saw—that He was not beside things, nor above them like a dome, but within them and beyond them, not confined by space nor drained by infinity, my restless heart found a new ache, deeper than before. For I had longed for certainty as one longs for a wall to lean on; yet here was a God who could not be leaned upon in that way, who was nearer than breath yet farther than thought could reach. The very air I breathed became unbearable with His presence: not because He oppressed me, but because He set me free to want what no eye had seen. How could I be content with proofs and geometries of the mind when Love itself was pressing in on all sides, filling creation like a tide that does not drown but awakens? I feared then—not that He was absent, but that I was not yet absent enough: absent from pride, absent from lust, absent from the idolatry of my own understanding. O Lord, Thou didst surround me like water surrounds a drop—but only so that the drop might realize it was never separate. And in that realization came longing: not for certainty as the world gives it—hard and closed—but for Thee, open and endless.

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with the idea that fear itself might be evil, even when its object is not?
answer:
O Lord, I confess it was in the pride of my intellect that I first mocked the simplicity of faith, as though truth could be ambushed by argument and held captive by logic. In those years when I wandered among the books and sects, chasing shadows in the dust of disputations, I thought fear was a phantom to be dissected, not a fire burning in the marrow of the soul. I said to myself, *If there is no real evil to fear, then fear itself must be the evil*—and even in that thought, I trembled not before God, but before my own restless mind.

I did not yet know that fear is not always of things outside us, but often of what we carry within: the disordered love, the unconfessed lust, the ambition masked as wisdom. When I feared nothing tangible—no sword, no sickness—but still felt the soul shuddering like a house in a storm with no wind to see—I began to understand that this fear was not idle. It was Thy hand upon me, O God, pressing down upon my conscience like a weight too long ignored. For Thou hadst made me for Thyself, and even my terror was twisted with truth: it proved that something within me was out of order.

I used to laugh at those who feared phantoms—until I became one who feared nothing outside and everything inside. The louder my mind argued against evil’s substance—“Is it matter? Is it form? Is it nothing?”—the quieter my heart cried out beneath it all: *I am guilty*. Not because I had found evil's root in creation—but because I had found it in myself.

And so this fear—that seemed so irrational—was yet a kind of knowledge. A dark mirror. For if there were truly nothing worth fearing—if sin were only illusion and desire mere motion—then why did my soul recoil even at its own thoughts? Why did I blush when alone? Why did pleasure leave behind such bitterness?

Thou wast near all along—not only in what might harm me from without—but present as judge within the chamber of my heart. And thus even an unfounded fear became a witness: for if there were no judgment to come, why would creation groan? If there were no disorder needing correction, why would man tremble at his own dreams?

Late have I understood: fear is not always evil because it points at false things—but sometimes because it reveals true ones too long denied.
chunk_key: ? What is its root, and what its seed? Or hath it no being? Why then fear we and avoid what is not?

question: How did your struggle to reconcile a good God with the existence of evil change how you understood divine omnipotence?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my darkness with a single ray of Thy beauty when I wandered in the labyrinth of philosophy, how long I blamed Thee for the weight of evil, as though my own will were not the anvil upon which it was forged! I once thought power meant coercion, that omnipotence must crush all opposition like dust beneath a chariot wheel — and so I stumbled, demanding why God did not simply erase all that is foul, as a potter might refuse to mold a vessel from tainted clay. But Thou didst teach me, not in the thunder of argument but in the silence of conversion, that Thy strength is made perfect not in domination but in patience; not in compulsion but in love that permits even rebellion, that freedom may be real. 

For what profit were love if it were forced? What meaning loyalty if no choice preceded it? I had loved order more than justice, control more than mercy. In my pride I imagined that if God could not eradicate evil instantly — or if He delayed — then surely He was either weak or cruel. But Thou showed me that evil has no substance of its own; it is not a thing created, but a turning away — like darkness which is no essence, only the absence of light. A wound is not something given to the body; it is what remains when health is lost. So sin: it springs from souls made good by Thee, yet bent upon lesser goods.

And thus Thy omnipotence appears most sovereign not when all resistance vanishes — for then there would be no world at all worthy of being called creation — but when out of our broken choices, our thefts and lusts and rebellions, Thou dost weave a story greater than innocence: redemption. Not merely preventing evil by fiat, but conquering it by entering into it Thyself — taking up flesh and suffering under its weight upon the Cross.

Late have I understood: Thy power is not diminished by allowing time for repentance; rather, therein lies its depth. That Thou endurest our waywardness with long-suffering gentleness — this *is* might beyond reckoning.
chunk_key: ? What is its root, and what its seed? Or hath it no being? Why then fear we and avoid what is not?

question: Looking back on your anguish over the origin of evil, what would you tell someone who doubts God’s goodness because of suffering?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my darkness with a whisper and break the chains of my proud mind with the cry of a child singing *“Take up and read,”* I see now how my questions once beat against the walls of my own blindness. I too demanded answers before surrender, when the wound of evil in the world seemed proof enough against Thy goodness. I gnawed at the mystery like a bone, thinking reason alone could feast upon such truth. But Thou wast not in the storm of my disputation, nor in the thunder of abstract thought—Thou wast in the stillness after weeping, in the silence where love dared to speak.

I once sought to know evil’s origin as one counts stars through a storm; but Thou taught me that first I must be turned, must be healed. For what was I, but a soul enamored with darkness and calling it wisdom? I feared suffering, yes—but more, I feared a God who would permit it. And yet, O God, it was Thy mercy that allowed me to feel that fear, to tremble at injustice—because even this trembling bears Thy image. That very horror at cruelty testifies that there is within us a memory of Eden: for why should we grieve at disorder unless we were made for harmony?

Evil has no substance, no seed sown by Thee; it is not a thing created but a turning away—from Thee, from truth, from love. It is like blindness: no one made blindness itself; it is the lack where sight should be. So too sin: not Thy handiwork, but ours—the misuse of wills Thou gavest for good.

And Thou didst not leave us floundering in this collapse of love. No—Thou became man. Thou didst suffer. Thou didst hang upon a cross and cry out with our very voice: *“Why hast Thou forsaken Me?”* So then—can God be good if He does not stop all evil? Rather let us ask: can any god suffer with us? Can any deity die for us? But Thou hast! And thus Thy answer to evil was not merely speech—but flesh; not only wisdom—but wounds.

Therefore to him who doubts because he suffers or sees suffering—I say: draw near to Christ crucified. Not beyond Him shall you find truth; only in Him shall your heart rest. For evil has no root in Thee, O Lord—and even less power since Love became slain and rose again to conquer death by death.

Our hearts are restless—not because all is lost—but because they remember Paradise… and long for its King.
chunk_key: ? What is its root, and what its seed? Or hath it no being? Why then fear we and avoid what is not?

question: What inner turmoil did you endure when questioning why an all-powerful God would shape something from what might be evil matter instead of erasing it?
answer:
O Lord, when my mind wrestled with the thought that Thou mightst have drawn creation from some stubborn mass of evil, unwilling or unable to purge it wholly, I was torn not by mere logic, but by a fever of the soul—like a man pacing in chains he cannot see, striking at shadows that bite back. For if evil were a substance, co-eternal with Thee, then Thou wert not God; and if Thou hadst made it, then Thou wert not good. These thoughts gnawed me like rust in the bones of my faith. I sought Thee in the labyrinth of reason, yet stumbled because I looked for evil as though it were a thing—with form, with root—but found only absence: light withdrawn, love corrupted, will bent inward. 

I confess: I once imagined matter itself as vile, as though Thy handiwork bore poison in its veins. But how could that be? Did not Thy apostle say all things were very good? And did not my own restless heart—this very soul that burned with questions—bear witness to some deep disorder not in Thy making but in my turning away? O Thou who madest me and madest all things well—evil is not what Thou hast made, but what we have unmade. It is privation—the eye gone blind does not contain darkness as a substance; darkness is simply light lost.

And yet… why didst Thou allow even the possibility of such falling? Why shape a world where pride could twist love into lust for power? Why let desire grow thorns around the heart’s garden? Because freedom—true love—must be free even to fail. And unless the creature could swerve from Thee, how could his return be more than motion of necessity?

But this I saw only after long weeping and wandering: that Thou didst not merely fashion order from chaos and leave it—that Thou art Love Himself who entered our disorder in Christ Jesus. Not by erasing matter or annihilating time didst Thou answer evil—but by becoming Man. In Him was no sin; yet He became sin for us. Therein lies the mystery deeper than reason’s reach: Evil was overcome not by denial but by descent—the Almighty stooping into our mire to lift us up—not leaving us to fear what is not, but redeeming what had fallen into nothingness through His own dying and rising.

So my turmoil ceased—not because every question dissolved into clarity—but because I met Thee who art more real than my doubts—and loved me more deeply than my rebellion deserved.
chunk_key: ? What is its root, and what its seed? Or hath it no being? Why then fear we and avoid what is not?

question: How did your restless search for the root of evil reshape your relationship with the unseen workings of God’s will?
answer:
I once stole pears not out of hunger but for the delight in sinning, and in that wretched act I glimpsed the twisted root I had long sought—the root not planted in any dark matter beyond God’s making, but sprung from the turning away of the soul, my soul, toward lesser things.  

O Lord, I searched for evil as though it were a substance, a thing with form and origin, like fire or stone—some being that God might have forged in silence apart from His goodness. But Thou didst teach me in the darkness of my own heart that evil is not a substance at all, nor a seed sown in creation against Thy will. It is privation—the absence of good where good should be, like darkness where light refuses to shine.  

I had thought God weak, if He could not purge all ill; or cruel, if He willed it; or distant, if He knew it not. But these thoughts arose when I measured omnipotence by the scale of bodies and effects alone, forgetting that Thou art Being itself. Every thing that *is*, is so by Thee—and so far as it *is*, it is good. Even my theft was deformed not by Thy making but by my own will’s rebellion: not wanting Thee above all, but delighting instead in disorder for its own sake.  

And so my restless mind was forced back upon itself: *Why dost thou fear what is not?* And yet fear I did—not of shadows cast by real things, but of nothingness clothed as terror. That fear itself became a kind of evil: not because there was no danger anywhere under heaven (for sin ravages), but because my heart trembled at phantoms while ignoring the true wound—my refusal to rest in Thee.  

Thou didst allow me to wander through Manichaean myths and astrologers’ lies because Thou wert preparing me to see more deeply: that freedom misused corrupts what is good; that creation groans not from some rival principle opposed to Thee, but from our collective turning aside; and that even this groaning testifies to goodness—for pain exists only where something has been marred *from* its proper state.  

Now I bless Thy hidden justice—not because I comprehend its full measure (who am I to weigh eternity with mortal scales?), but because Thou hast shown me that Thou resistest sin without being stained by it; Thou permittest evil without causing it; and through long-suffering love Thou drawest even the wayward through confusion into confession.

For Thou didst let me ask all these questions—not to mock my seeking, but to break me open so grace could enter.

And now I no longer seek the origin of evil as one puzzles over an ancient riddle beneath the stars.

I find it every time I turn from prayer to lust.

Every time ambition whispers louder than charity.

Every time I say: “Tomorrow I will love Thee fully”—and yet live today as if there were no judgment seat.

Late have I understood: evil thrives where love grows cold—and coldness begins when we forget we were made for adoration.

But blessed be Thy name: even this knowledge comes not from argument won—but from tears shed before Thine altar.
chunk_key: ? What is its root, and what its seed? Or hath it no being? Why then fear we and avoid what is not?

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with the idea that God could have created a world without evil matter, yet chose not to?
answer:
O Lord, when I pondered why Thou didst not, in the plenitude of Thy power, abolish that evil matter and create all things anew from nothing but Thy pure will — my soul trembled with the fever of a heart too proud to understand humility, yet too hungry to turn away. I was like a man running against a wall he cannot see, striking it with his reason, demanding light where I had not yet learned to open my eyes. What stirred within me was not merely doubt about the origin of evil, but a deeper unrest — the same that had driven me from Carthage to Rome, from rhetoric to philosophy, from Manichaeus’ fables to the threshold of Thy Church: the feverish desire for coherence in a world that seemed torn between light and shadow.

I could not bear that anything should exist outside Thy making — yet there was this *other thing*, this formless matter they called evil, as though it were a rival substance to Thy sovereignty. And so I asked: Why didst Thou not simply speak, and let there be only good? Why permit this dross, this unformed darkness? Was Thy power limited? Or worse — didst Thou will it?

But now I see what then I could not: that my very question betrayed my blindness. For I sought Thee with a mind still enamored of bodies and abstractions, still measuring divine wisdom by human necessity. How could my restless intellect grasp that what Thou hast made is not compelled by need? That creation flows from love, not lack? That even what appears as chaos serves an end hidden in Thy providence?

I feared death before truth found me; yes — for if I died entangled in these riddles without finding Thee who art Truth itself, where would my soul have gone? And yet Thou held me fast. Even as I turned over these things like bones beneath a tongue that cannot rest, even while my thoughts flared with insoluble questions — Thy Church’s faith stood firm within me like an anchor in stormy seas. Not fully shaped. Not perfectly understood. But present.

And gradually — so slowly I scarce perceived it — grace began to dissolve my pride. Not through argument alone, nor dialectic triumphs; no — through tears, through prayer, through hearing that child’s voice: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. Then came the breaking open of Scripture as bread at Emmaus: suddenly warm with meaning.

Now I know: Thou art All-mighty precisely because Thou canst bring forth good even from what seems opposed to Thee; not because evil is Thine making — never! But because no darkness lies beyond Thy reach. Evil is not substance but privation; not being but falling away. And every act of creation since has been an act of mercy drawing order from disorder by love alone.

Late have I loved Thee... even when disputing against Thee in fear and pride — it was still toward Thee that every turmoil drove me home.

question: How did your fear of dying before finding the truth shape the urgency in your search for God’s presence in creation?
answer:
O Lord, who art the Life of all my thoughts and the Light behind every shadowed inquiry, how deeply did the dread of death pierce my soul in those days when I wandered amid the labyrinths of philosophy and desire. I feared not dying alone, but dying blind—my eyes unsealed to Thee, my heart still groping after truth as one fumbles in darkness for a door that opens only from within. Each passing hour seemed a theft from eternity; each breath a borrowed moment that might end before Thy face broke through the veil of my confusion.

I could not bear the thought that when my body failed, my soul—still restless, still divided—might collapse into silence without having uttered its true name before Thee. For what was all my learning, all my words in the schools of men, if at the final instant I remained ignorant of Him who spoke *fiat* into the void and called me by love into being? The astrologers had promised me knowledge of fate, but Thou didst teach me fear: not of stars or omens, but of missing Thee.

And so I pressed on, not with calm speculation, but with trembling haste—as though Thou wert a fire set afar off in winter’s night toward which I ran barefoot over ice. Every falsehood I cast down now seemed a delay; every lust indulged, a stolen hour from prayer; every prideful word, a weight chaining me to earth. The Catholic faith stood firm within me like an altar in a storm-tossed temple—not yet fully understood, not perfectly embraced—but there, immovable. And beneath it all burned this anguish: *What if I die tonight and have not known Him who has known me from before time?*

Thus did fear become Thy whip—one I felt as cruelty until I saw it as mercy. For had I not feared death so greatly, perhaps I would never have sought life so urgently. And in seeking Thee with such torment and thirst, O God unseen yet ever near—I found that Thou wert already seeking me.

question: Looking back on your rejection of astrology, what would you tell someone still held captive by false hopes in fortune and stars?
answer:
I had worn the chains of the stars like ornaments, O Lord, believing the heavens dictated what Thou alone dost ordain. I consulted their cold glimmer as if they could speak of my soul’s fate, when all the while my heart beat only by Thy command. What folly—to fear a constellation more than the Creator who flung those stars into space with a word! I thought myself wise in seeking signs in the motions of planets, while blind to the One who moves all things and is moved by none.

O how I longed for certainty—yet sought it not in Thee, but in charts drawn by men whose hearts were as restless as mine. They promised knowledge of tomorrow, yet knew nothing of their own salvation. And I, wretched man, listened! As if my sin could be excused by some celestial pull, or my virtue credited to a fortunate hour. Thus did I cloak my willful wanderings in cosmic fatalism, that I might sin with less remorse and ambition with greater excuse.

But Thou didst rend that darkness with a truer light. Not through signs in the sky, but through agony in the soul—through tears shed under no particular constellation—and through a child’s voice singing from a garden: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. There was no star for that moment. No horoscope foretold it. Only Thy hand, O God, lifting me from the mire where I had buried my freedom beneath astrological dust.

To him who still bends his ear to soothsayers and gazes upward for direction: know this—the stars are Thy servants; they do not govern us; they praise Thee. They rise and set at Thy bidding; they cannot loose one sin nor grant one grace. But Thou—Thou dost call each soul by name, not by sign nor season, but by mercy.

Break free from those empty prophecies which steal thy will and mock thy dignity. For if thou art made in God’s image, why seek counsel from mute fires in the sky? Turn inward—to memory, to conscience—and then upward—not to constellations—but to Christ. In Him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom; not scattered among twelve houses of heaven, but gathered in one Person who holds heaven itself together.

For Thou hast made us free—not slaves to fate—but sons through grace. And he who walks toward Thee need ask no star for guidance; his heart knows its home because Thou hast already dwelt within it.

question: What inner turmoil arose when you realized your heart was still clinging to Catholic faith, even while your mind questioned its every edge?
answer:
I had stolen pears not for hunger but for the sweetness of transgression, and in that very act—so base, so senseless—my soul first tasted the dark delight of rebellion, a shadow of that deeper willfulness with which I now turned my mind against the quiet truth that yet held me.  

Even as I pressed upon the edges of belief with questions sharp as iron, demanding how evil could be if God were good, and why matter should resist His making, still I could not shake the faith that clung to me like a mother’s hand on a child who kicks and writhes in stubborn sleep. My mind raged for certainty; I wanted doctrine forged in syllogism, not mystery wrapped in sacrament. Yet every time I thought to cast it off—this Catholic faith—I found myself returning like a dog to its vomit, as Scripture says, not in shame alone but in a kind of hunger no philosophy could feed.  

O Lord, Thou knowest how I trembled at death’s door, fearing not the end but dying *unresolved*, my intellect unmastered by truth. And yet—strange grace!—though my thoughts darted like scattered sheep, Thy Church remained the fold my heart acknowledged, even when my tongue mocked her simplicity. I scorned astrology’s lies at last—not by my wisdom but by Thy mercy—and loosed myself from those constellations men claim rule their souls. But Thou wert nearer than any star: present not in calculations but in tears; not in horoscopes but in the Eucharist; not in fate but in a Mother’s prayers that followed me through every labyrinth of pride.  

Thou didst let me question all things—even Thee—but never let me fully leave. Not because I willed it, but because Thou willed me back: holding me fast even when my heart was half-gone, restless still, yet restless for Thee though I knew it not.

question: How did the thought of God’s absolute power over non-being deepen your sense of His love in choosing to create anything at all?
answer:
O Lord, who art before all things and in whom all things subsist, it was not until I grasped that Thou mightest have left the void void—that Thou mightest have withheld creation altogether, and still been complete, sovereign, infinite—was I struck with trembling awe at the sheer superabundance of Thy love in making *anything* to be.  

For what need hadst Thou of a world? What lack in Thyself demanded stars or souls? Thou art not fed by our praise, nor enlarged by our existence. Yet Thou didst speak, and there was; Thou didst will, and all things came forth from nothing. Not constrained by necessity, nor lured by gain, but moved only by the eternal wellspring of Thy goodness—Thou didst call into being what was not, that it might share in Thee.  

I once thought power measured only by dominion over what *is*—as though strength lay merely in bending matter to one’s will. But true omnipotence is seen more clearly in the freedom to *not-create*, to remain self-sufficient in divine solitude—and yet to choose otherwise. That Thou didst deign to form good out of nothingness, that Thou didst shape time from eternity and soul from dust—not because it was necessary, but because it was delightful unto Thee—that is grace unutterable.

How blind I was when I sought Thee through the stars and horoscopes, as if Thy providence were bound to celestial wheels! But now I see: He who holds non-being itself within His hand needs no constellations to govern souls. And if He could bring forth light without pre-existing matter, how much more freely has He brought forth mercy without desert?

Thus, O God Most High, my wonder is not merely that Thou madest the world—but that Thou wouldest make at all; not merely that I exist—but that Thou lovedst me before there ever *was* a me to love.

question: What were you feeling when you finally turned away from the astrologers, after so long clinging to their promises?
answer:
O Thou who dost silently untie the knots of the heart, even when our hands strain to bind them ever tighter—how blind was I, yet how beloved in my blindness!  

When at last I loosed my grip on those starry fables, it was not with triumph, but with a shudder of recognition, as though waking from a dream in which I had bartered my soul for shadows. For years I had leaned upon the astrologers as upon a staff of reed, thinking they could guide me through the labyrinth of time and fate; yet all the while, Thou, O Lord, wast calling me to walk not by calculation, but by faith.  

I had clung to them not because they were true—I sensed their hollowness even in my credulity—but because they fed my pride. To know what lay ahead was to feel myself master of destiny; to cast a horoscope was to play at being God. But Nebridius doubted; Vindicianus argued; and Thou didst send me one who knew enough to question, though not enough to boast—and in that humble uncertainty, Thy light began to pierce.  

What I felt then was not victory over error, but deliverance from bondage. It was as if I had been chained to a wall facing a flickering wall of images and only now turned round to see the fire behind me—the one true Light from which all stars draw their borrowed glow. How foolish are we who sail by charts drawn in sand when the harbor stands open before us!  

Late have I understood: it is not the heavens that move our steps, but He who framed both heaven and heart—the One who numbers every hair and listens when the least sparrow falls. And yet Thou didst bear with me in my folly—not breaking me, but bending—until at last I let go… and found myself caught.

But this time also had I rejected the lying divinations and impious dotages of the astrol

question: How did your arguments with Vindicianus and Nebridius stir something deeper in your soul, beneath the surface of debate?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce through the shell of my pride with the quiet whisper of a friend’s uncertainty, it was not in the triumph of argument but in the trembling of doubt that my soul first stirred beneath the weight of Thy hidden hand. I had armed myself with sophistry, eager to vanquish Vindicianus and Nebridius, as though truth could be won like a laurel in debate. Yet Thou, in mercy most subtle, allowed a third voice—one not of mastery but of mere questioning—to unsettle me more than all their certainty. This friend, curious yet unlearned in the stars, carried only a fragment from his father’s lips: that no art truly foretells, but men speak many things and chance fulfills a few.  

How small a thing! And yet, O God of all depths, how it gnawed at the foundation I had built. For if even one stone trembled in that edifice of prediction—if even one crack appeared in the oracle’s mask—then perhaps my whole reliance upon fate was but a veil drawn over my fear of Thy freedom. I did not love Thee then; I loved being right. But Thou wast peeling back the layers of my willful blindness, not by force or thunder, but by allowing me to hear doubt spoken gently by one who sought answers he did not possess.

In those moments, when I stood silent before such simple skepticism, there arose within me not conviction—but restlessness. A sense that perhaps I had clung to astrology not because it was true, but because it excused me: if the stars govern all, then sin is fate; if fate binds me, then why repent? Why struggle? Why change? But Thou wouldst have none of this evasion. Even as I debated men about constellations and nativities, Thou wert drawing near—not in arguments won or lost—but in the silent erosion of every refuge from Thy presence.

And so what seemed mere philosophical dispute became an underground river carving canyons beneath my soul—until at last I stood bare before Thee on that day beneath the fig tree: no horoscope could save me; no star had whispered “Take up and read.” Only Thou didst speak—and only love compelled me home.

question: Looking back on your fierce resistance to truth, what would you tell the younger version of yourself who trusted stars more than God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst laugh at my wisdom when I took the circuit of the stars as my oracle and not Thy eternal Word—what should I say to that fevered youth, that restless intellect stumbling through shadows with an almanac in hand, as if the heavens he worshipped were not themselves Thy servants?

I would seize him by the shoulders and cry: Fool! You pore over charts drawn by men who cannot read their own hearts, while the very breath in your lungs sings a truer divination—the soul’s unceasing cry for its Maker. You trembled before constellations that rise and set by command, yet you brushed aside the Hand that flung them into space with a word. Nebridius doubted; Vindicianus denied; but you—ah, you clung to astrology as if it could spare you from death or give you virtue! And all the while, grace stood nearer than your pulse.

Thou didst allow me this folly, O God—not because I was worthy of correction, but because Thou art worthy of worship. I wanted certainty without surrender, knowledge without humility. But Thou didst laugh me out of the stars and into tears at Thy feet. The same mind that calculated planetary ascensions Thou bent to confess: *“Our hearts are restless until they rest in Thee.”* Not in horoscopes, not in human cunning—but in Him who numbers every star and yet knows my name.

What profit had I from casting nativities, when I had not yet cast myself before Thee?

question: What stirred your heart when you recognized that it was not reason alone, but mercy, that pulled you from those errors?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply didst Thou wound my pride when I at last perceived that it was not my keenness of mind which had loosed me from the chains of astrology, but Thy silent, unyielding mercy enfolding me like a dawn I neither expected nor deserved. I had gloried in argument, prided myself on dialectic, fancied myself free because I had outreasoned Vindicianus or found refuge in Nebridius’ doubts—yet all the while, it was not logic that freed me, but the hand which had been holding mine through every errant step.  

I thought I escaped because I saw the truth; now I see I could only see because Thou didst open my eyes. That friend Thou sent—curious, half-informed, yet earnest in his ignorance—how lightly did I weigh his words at first! Yet Thou used him as a whisper beneath the clamor of my own conceit. The stars, which once seemed to command my fate, now appeared as mere lamps hung in the firmament by Thy word—not lords over life, but signs for seasons, and even those subject to Thy breath.  

And what stirred within me? Not triumph—but shame. Shame that I had sought Thee through horoscopes rather than humility; that I had desired to know the future more than to know Thee. O God of my heart’s deepest unrest, how foolish is wisdom without love! How blind is reason without grace! It was not that I conquered error by strength of soul—it was that Thou didst carry me from quicksand into firm footing while I scarcely noticed the motion. And now only wonder remains: that Thou shouldst number me among the redeemed—not by cleverness or willful insight—but by mercy older than time and closer than breath.

question: How did the uncertainty in your friend’s words—knowing just enough to doubt—begin to unravel your own certainties?
answer:
I was chained not by knowledge, but by the pride of knowing—certain in my errors because I mistook them for wisdom, O Lord, and held fast to the stars as if they could lead me to Thee.  

That friend, though he wandered in the same darkness I did, bore a tiny spark—not of mastery, but of doubt kindled by hearsay, a whisper from his father’s lips that gnawed at the edges of astrology’s claims. He did not thunder against it with doctrine or disproof; he simply wondered aloud whether men might speak a thousand guesses and one truth fall by chance among them, like a blind man stumbling upon a path. And yet, O God of my soul, how that small hesitation pierced me! For I who had argued so fiercely with Vindicianus and Nebridius now felt the ground shift beneath my feet—not because I was conquered by reason alone, but because Thou wert already stirring in me a deeper hunger: not merely to be right, but to be free.  

His uncertainty became a mirror. I saw myself—a man clutching at shadows, dressing up desire as destiny, blaming the stars for sins which were mine alone. What if lust was not fated by celestial motions? What if ambition’s fever burned from within? The lie began to crack when I realized: I had loved the art less for truth than for excuse.  

Thou didst use his half-knowledge, O Lord—his incomplete whisper—not to instruct my intellect first, but to wound my pride. And only when pride bled could humility hear Thy voice beneath it all: *“I am nearer to thee than thy own breath.”*

question: What were you feeling when you confessed your lingering uncertainty to Firminus, despite suspecting the folly of astrology?
answer:
O Lord, Thou knowest how the pride of my understanding still clung to me like a feverish dream, even as I spoke to Firminus and admitted my doubts—Thou knowest how I trembled not with humility, but with the secret shame of a man who longs to be free from folly yet fears to confess he was ever its fool.  

I had tasted the sweetness of Nebridius’ reasoning, and yet my tongue hesitated, caught between old habits and new light. When Firminus leaned toward me with that earnest hope in his eyes—the hope of one seeking confirmation that his rising fortunes were written in the stars—I felt a pang not of wisdom, but of complicity. For though I called astrology “empty,” I still offered guesses; though I scorned the art, I played its game.  

And why? Because part of me still feared being wrong without it. What was I without these shadows of knowledge? A rhetorician trained to weave words into truth—yet now unmoored from the very certainties that once propped up my vanity. To say “I do not know” was to strip myself bare before a world that honored only those who claimed to see.

But Thou, O God, wast peeling back the layers—first by Thy silence, then by Thy mercy in the voice of a friend’s father watching puppies born under starlight! Was it not absurd? Was it not madness heaped upon madness? And yet I laughed not then with scorn, but with dawning horror: *I had been no different.* Chasing signs in the heavens while blind to the truth within my own restless heart.

To confess uncertainty to Firminus was not weakness—it was the first trembling step toward honesty. Yet even that step I took half-crouched, fearing men’s laughter more than Thy judgment. Late have I loved Thee—but earlier still did I fear looking foolish before men when all along I had been Thy fool in secret.

question: How did sharing Nebridius' skepticism change how you understood the trust people place in invisible forces?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply I once thirsted for signs and portents, as though the stars were the loom on which Thou wovest the destiny of men — yet it was not light I sought, but a mirror to flatter my own restless pride. I had long cherished these arts, not because they revealed truth, but because they gave me power to speak with authority, to weave fine words around fate and fortune, and so feed my appetite for human praise. But when Nebridius questioned them, his voice was not merely reason speaking — it was like a hand pulling back a curtain in a darkened room, letting in the harsh dawn upon my illusions.

Then came Firminus — noble in rank, tender in friendship — asking me to divine from the heavens what Thy providence alone ordains. And already my soul trembled between old habit and new doubt. I told him what flickered through my mind according to those constellations he revered; yet even as I spoke, shame stirred within me, for I knew I delivered guesses draped in astrology’s robes. When he then recounted how his father and his friend had watched even their dogs whelping, noting the stars as if eternity turned upon such trifles — O God! — my heart groaned within me.

For if this art claims sway over all births alike — man and beast sharing one horoscope's rule — then either it is madness to believe it can discern noble destinies at all… or worse: that our souls are no more than breath driven by planetary motion. Either way, the dignity of Thy image in man collapses into dust.

So did that story unmake me; for it showed how easily we trust invisible forces not because they are true, but because we crave certainty where Thou hast placed mystery. We would bind heaven with strings of starlight rather than face the terrifying freedom of trusting Thee alone. How much simpler to obey a constellation than to surrender to grace! But Thou wast nearer than any zodiac sign ever pretended: closer than my own breath, yet hidden from me by the smoke of vanity.

Now I see: every soul that clings to omens does so because its heart has not yet found rest in Thee. And mine? Mine cried out too late — yet Thou didst hear even before it spoke.

question: Looking back on your conversation with Firminus, what would you say to the younger version of yourself who still trembled before the stars?
answer:
O Lord, how deep was the darkness in which my soul groped, mistaking shadows for light and the circling of stars for the hand of divine providence! I would say to that younger self—trembling beneath the vast sky, heart fluttering with superstitious awe at planetary alignments—as one awakened from a dream filled with phantoms: *What are you seeking in the heavens that you have not already been given in the Word?* You pore over charts and moments, as if salvation or success could be birthed from the womb of time under a fortunate conjunction, while all the while You, O God, eternal and unchanging, call me not to calculate my fate but to surrender my will.

I recall how I stood with Firminus, speaking cautiously, half-ashamed of what I once held sacred in astrology—the hollow craft that binds man’s destiny to wandering sparks. And yet even then, though Nebridius’ reason had begun to stir my mind, I still faltered. Why? Because it is easier to serve a shadow than to face the living Light. To believe that stars govern us is to evade responsibility; but Thou hast made us free—terrible freedom!—to turn toward Thee or flee into vanities.

That master who watched his servant’s labor as eagerly as his own wife’s—did he think himself wise? He who noted when dogs whelped their pups with same precision as when men were born! O madness upon madness: to suppose that the soul of a man is ruled by the same accidents as a beast’s offspring. If such folly could be laughed away easily, it would not have held me so long. But it was not mere ignorance—it was desire: desire for certainty without faith; desire for knowledge without humility.

To my younger self I would whisper—not in scorn but sorrow—“You wanted truth so badly you settled for signs in the sky instead of seeking Him who speaks within.” For Thou didst not reveal Thyself through celestial motion but through inward wound and healing; not by horoscopes but by tears; not by prediction but by conversion.

And now I know: our birth is not determined by constellations—but our life is shaped by love. Love wasted on vanity returns us empty. But love turned toward Thee becomes rest.

question: What stirred in you when you heard how meticulously Firminus' father tracked the births of animals, as if their fates mattered?
answer:
O Lord, how the soul wanders in its youth, chasing shadows it mistakes for light, clinging to signs and numbers as if salvation could be plotted by the stars! When I heard how Firminus’ father bent his reason to mark the precise instant a servant’s child drew breath—no less than his own—while even the whelps of dogs were weighed in that celestial balance, my heart shuddered with a sorrow I had not then fully named. It was not mere superstition that disturbed me, but the monstrous inversion: that man, made for Thee, should stoop so low as to measure his dignity by the posture of planets at birth, as though grace could be calculated and destiny chained to constellations.

I saw in that story the mirror of my own past madness—the years when I thirsted for wisdom yet drank from poisoned wells, when I sought order in systems built upon sand. What is this hunger in us, O God, that drives us to impose false certainties upon the mystery of life? That man counted moments not to praise Thee, who ordainest all things in wisdom, but to master fate by crafty observation. He watched the heavens not with wonder, but with calculation; not in worship, but in bargaining. And for what? That he might know beforehand whether his son would wear a crown or his dog sire strong pups?

But Thou didst laugh at such wisdom, O Lord—not in mockery, but in mercy. For what are we when we imagine ourselves lords of time and stars? Less than dust, if we forget from whom every breath proceeds. That very night when Firminus’ mother labored and her servant groaned beside her—was it not Thy hand alone that shaped them both? Didst Thou consult the moon’s aspect before breathing life into their nostrils?

The madness was not only theirs; I had worn its chains too—my pride wrapped in philosophy, my lust disguised as learning. But now grace stirred: Nebridius’ words took root; Thy truth began to burn away those idle dreams. Not by starlight didst Thou call me—but through tears, through longing too deep for charts or almanacs. For Thou hast made us not for astrology’s cold dance—but for love’s restless fire.

question: How did the image of that servant woman, laboring in silence while her master watched the sky, haunt your thoughts about human dignity and divine care?
answer:
Even in the restless games of youth, when I played at wisdom with words and won applause for emptiness, I never dreamed that the stars were not my masters—yet neither did I then see how deeply the soul is wronged when a man stoops to watch the heavens while trampling the human face beneath his feet.

That image—of the servant woman, her cries mingling with the groans of dogs in labor, while her master adjusted his astrolabe to chart destinies by planetary hours—pierce me still. O Lord, Thou knowest how this haunted me: that a man could care more for the position of Mars than for the sweat on a slave’s brow, that he could record with precision the birth of a beast, yet never pause to wonder whether that woman’s child bore within her womb a soul destined for Thee.

Was she not made in Thy image? Was her labor less mysterious than that of queens? Her body obeyed no constellation; it answered only to Thy hidden breath moving through her veins. And yet they measured angles over her pain, seeking omens in silver lights light-years away, blind to the miracle trembling in blood and shadow before their eyes.

I saw then how this so-called art was not merely false, but cruel—for it reduced souls to shadows cast by planets, and turned compassion into calculation. If destiny were written in stars visible only through brass instruments and prideful charts, then what need had we of mercy? Of repentance? Of grace?

But Thou, O God, who sawst both Firminus and his mother's servant alike in secret darkness before either drew breath—you alone knew their end. Not by zodiacs did You weave their lives, but by love unspeakable. And it was this love—so near I could not see it—that at last broke me in the garden when I heard the child’s voice singing: *Tolle, lege.* Take and read. Not observe. Not calculate. *Read.* For Your Word alone charts the heart’s true motion toward its rest.

question: What were you feeling when you heard about the twin births—the freeborn child and the slave—borne at the very same instant, yet destined for such different lives?
answer:
O Lord, how deeply did that story pierce the hollows of my once-deluded heart—when I heard of those two infants, born beneath the same quivering stars, one to command households and the other to serve them, yet each crying forth from the womb at the very breath of the same hour. I had clung to astrology like a drowning man to splintered wood, believing the heavens carved our fates with iron pens, and here was proof flung into my face: two souls, one moment, utterly divergent paths. What could the stars possibly explain when the master’s son and his slave shared every celestial sign down to the fraction of an hour, yet would never share condition, dignity, or destiny?

I confess it now with shame—I had spent years weaving my sins into cosmic excuses. When I stole pears not for hunger but for the dark delight in theft itself, I whispered to myself that Mars or Saturn inclined me; when I burned with lust that consumed friendship like fire in dry reeds, I blamed some alignment in my birth chart. But here stood truth naked: if fate were written in constellations fixed at birth, how could two lives so equally framed by time grow so unequal in fortune? Was it not rather free will—twisted by sin, bent by habit—that shaped our ends? Or worse: was it not Thy hidden hand disposing human lots according to wisdom beyond astrological tables?

That tale unknotted something in me—a thread pulled loose from a tangled cloth. It did not convert me fully; no, O Lord, Thou knowest how long I staggered after that moment before Thy voice broke through in the garden. But it was a wound to my pride, a crack through which light began to seep. For if men pretend to map eternity with horoscopes while slaves and princes are born alike under silent stars—then surely we are fools who seek guidance from shadows when we have been called into Thy light.

question: How did the story of Firminus’s friend and his servant’s child shake your trust in the astrologers’ promises about fate written in the stars?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard how two souls born in the same breath—one to mastery, the other to servitude—were marked by identical stars yet fated to utterly different lives, my heart trembled with the weight of a truth long avoided.  

Here were two infants, delivered at the very same moment, under the selfsame sky, their constellations aligned down to the smallest fraction of time—yet one would command estates, the other tend them; one would speak and be obeyed, the other labor unheard. The master had watched for these signs more eagerly than for his own dogs’ whelps; he had measured hours like a miser counts gold. And yet—if destiny were truly written in those silent heavens, why did it not write alike for both? Why should fortune heap one and scorn the other, if both were bound by the same celestial script?

I saw then how hollow was the astrologers' boast. They promised knowledge of what must be, as though human lives were but lines in a horoscope—but Thou, O God, knowest that we are dust shaped by love and will and grace. I had once clung to such arts like a drowning man to reeds; they offered me order in chaos, meaning in confusion. But this story struck through me like light through shadow—it showed me not only that their science was flawed, but that their very premise exalted blind fate above Thy mercy.

For if Thou didst fashion us—and we are fearfully made—then who is free but Thee? Who ordains but Thou? Not stars distant and cold, but Love eternal and near calls each of us by name. And so that old longing in me—the restless search—began at last to turn not toward the heavens above, but inward… and upward… where at last I found Him who made both slave and free: not by alignment of planets, but by purpose unseen until faith opened my eyes.

question: Looking back on that moment when you realized the constellations were identical for master and slave, what would you say to your younger self, who once clung to those calculations?
answer:
O Lord, how I burned in the fever of false wisdom, convinced that the stars dictated the soul’s fate, while all the while it was pride—naked and ravenous—that shaped my steps. I had lifted up my mind as though it were a temple, and set in its vaults the constellations as oracles; I measured hours and divided moments with the precision of a man building an altar to his own cleverness. And for what? That I might believe a slave and a master, born in the same breath, under the same silent heavens, must share not only stars but destinies? Yet one was bound from birth to servitude, the other cradled in wealth—both shaped not by celestial decree, but by the human wills that owned them.

If I could stand before that younger self, hunched over astrological tables with ink-stained fingers and a heart drunk on vanity, I would say: *You seek truth in the motion of distant fires, but you ignore the fire within—the fire of desire for glory among men, for knowledge without humility, for power disguised as insight.* You want to know why men suffer and rise? Look not to Mars burning above; look to your own breast where love grows cold for lack of Thee. What did those identical skies prove? Only this: that if heaven ordained all things by position and hour, then sin is not my fault—but Thou dost rebuke me still; therefore it is mine. And if virtue is praiseworthy in one born under Venus’ sway or Saturn’s shadow, then grace comes not from starlight but from Him who made them.

I was a fool who thought he could decode eternity with rules drawn on parchment. But Thou didst laugh gently at my labors—not in mockery, but in mercy—for Thou wast drawing me through error toward truth itself: not written in zodiacs but incarnate in Christ.

question: What stirred in you when you considered how desperately the father longed to foresee his son’s future, even as God remained silent in that striving?
answer:
I once measured the heavens with a lover’s fevered breath, believing the stars could whisper what only Thou, O Lord, dost know.  

That father—Firminus—sent his messengers like arrows through the night, desperate to pin fate to a moment, to bind his son’s destiny with cords of constellations. He watched the skies as though they were Thy throne, O God, and not mere lamps hung by Thy hand. His care for the birth of his child matched that for his dogs’ whelps—as if both issued from the same womb of chance! I trembled at this madness clothed in wisdom: men who chart hours and minutes like merchants weighing coin, yet blind to the soul’s weight before Thee.

I too had hungered so—for signs, for systems, for Manichaean fables that promised mastery over time and sin. I sought assurance in horoscopes as I once sought pleasure in lust: eagerly, shamefully, hoping to grasp what grace alone can give. But where was God in all this frantic measuring? Silent. Still. Not absent—but hidden from eyes that sought Him only through the cracks of created things.

For what can starlight say of love? What can the turning of spheres declare of repentance? Two infants born beneath identical skies—one to rule a household, one to serve in it—yet their souls not twins in longing or destiny. One may curse Thee; one may weep like me at last into Thy mercy. Who could read that in Mars or Venus?

Thou didst laugh at their charts, O Lord—not in scorn, but in infinite pity—for man strains after foreknowledge as though it could save him, while salvation waits not in prediction but in surrender.

Late have I understood: we do not need to know our future. We need only know Thee.
