question: How did the image of those two mothers, one free and one bound, laboring at the same hour, change your understanding of human dignity in God’s eyes?
answer:
Even as a youth, I was ensnared by the glitter of wisdom, chasing signs in the stars as if the heavens themselves could bind the soul’s destiny—yet how blind I was to the quiet truth that lay beneath such calculations. Here were two women, one mistress and one servant, their bodies wracked with the same labor, their cries rising at the same hour beneath the selfsame sky; and yet, when their sons drew breath in that single moment, one was fated to rule estates, the other to till them. The astrologers would have it that both shared identical constellations—and thus, by their logic, should share fortune and fate. But what mockery is this? That a slave and a noble should be born in synchrony, yet one be raised to power and the other bowed to dust?

O Lord, it struck me then not with force of argument but with the weight of sorrow: if our dignity rested in stars or social birth, then Thou wouldst have made a jest of justice. But Thou hast made every soul in Thy image—not some for dominion alone, others for chains. The servant’s child wept as tenderly as the master’s; his breath was no less a gift from Thee. And so my heart began to waver in its pride—for if grace does not fall by position or prediction, then what am I but dust saved only by mercy? The stars may mark time’s passage, but they do not govern love; they cannot measure worth. Only Thou knowest each soul’s value—and Thou dost not consult horoscopes when calling men from darkness into light.

Late have I understood: dignity is not bestowed by rank or hour of birth, but planted deep in us by Thy hand who breathed life into clay alike.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the same stars mapped two lives so drastically different—one gilded, one chained?
answer:
O Lord, when the truth pierced me in that hour—when the stars, which I had once thought masters of fate, were shown to rule nothing at all—I felt the earth shift beneath my soul’s feet, as though all my restless pacing toward wisdom had suddenly stumbled into a silence deeper than night.  

For so long I had wandered among the books of the astrologers, tracing lines across the heavens like a man reading destinies in smoke. I sought in the motions of distant fires what I could not find in myself: order, meaning, a map to explain why one man rises while another groans beneath the yoke. And here was Firminus—born on the same breath as his slave, beneath constellations unaltered by even a finger’s breadth—and yet one wore purple while the other wore chains.  

Thou didst use that paradox as a goad, O God—not to wound but to awaken. My mind reeled not at their differing fates alone, but at the sheer impossibility of it within the false system I had trusted. How could one sky decree two lives so contrary? If heaven spoke truly for either, it must speak falsely for both. And if it spoke falsely—then what had I worshiped but shadows? What had I served but vanity clothed in starlight?  

I stood undone. Not by anger, nor despair—but by a sudden hush in which my soul heard something older than astrology: Thy voice, calling me from wanderings more subtle than those of my youth’s lusts and ambitions. For even this error was pride: to think I could chart Thy providence by mathematics; to suppose that Thou wert bound by spheres and signs while men like that nameless slave suffered unseen.  

Late have I loved Thee—yes—but late also have I feared Thee rightly: not as One who decrees through celestial machinery, but as Father who holds every hair and heartbeat in His hand; who numbers slaves as well as princes; who saw both men at once—the one feasting under gold and the other trembling in darkness—and wept over each with knowledge no star could contain.  

And so my heart found its rest not in answers written above us—but in Him who stooped below us to be born among dust and mangers; not in horoscopes, but in humility; not in prediction—but in prayer.

question: How did your conversation with Firminus shatter the certainty you once held about the stars guiding human fate?
answer:
O Lord, who alone seest the depths of man’s folly and the snares of his proud reasonings, it was through the breath of a single man’s testimony—Firminus, spoken in trust, not in boast—that the whole edifice of astrology within me trembled and fell.  

I had clung to those starry charts as if they were tablets from heaven, believing—nay, teaching—that the hour of birth fixed the course of life, that fate was written in the silent turning of the spheres. And why? Not from divine revelation, but from vanity: I desired to know what only Thou dost ordain; I sought mastery over mystery where I ought to have knelt in awe.  

But when Firminus told me how he and a slave were born at once, under the same sky, their messengers meeting midway so that no breath nor beam could differ—yet one wore purple and ruled cities while the other bore chains and knew no freedom—I was struck not by contradiction alone, but by absurdity. How could the stars pronounce nobility for one and abjection for another when all celestial signs were identical? The heavens could not account for such disparity; only Thy hidden hand could.  

I saw then how I had made gods of shadows—of signs without substance—and called it wisdom. If I had gazed upon those constellations above his cradle and spoken truly according to their rules, I would have foretold palaces for the slave and servitude for Firminus—and been utterly false. Or worse: if I had spoken what *was*, it would have been not by reading stars but by chance or knowledge of this world’s order—and thus confession would have broken my own system.  

So Thou didst use even this man’s story as a rod against my pride, O God—not thunder nor miracle, but a tale told quietly at table—to show me that Thou dost weave our lives not by planetary dance but by eternal purpose; that no soul is bound by zodiacs but shaped in secret by Thy love; that he who knows not his Master’s will is yet more blessed than he who claims to read heaven while blind to grace.

My heart burned with shame—for how long had I trafficked in falsehoods disguised as knowledge? And yet also with joy: for if stars do not rule, then Thou dost. And if Thou dost rule—then even one such as I may be freed from every chain—not least the chain of believing himself captive to fate.

question: Looking back on that moment of surrender, what would you tell someone still clinging to astrology for hope or meaning?
answer:
O Lord, who alone seest the depths of the heart and laughest at the devices of men, I confess to Thee how deeply I once groveled in that vain delusion—the hope that stars could bind my destiny, as if Thy sovereign hand were absent from the moment of birth. I, who had pored over constellations with trembling curiosity, seeking in the heavens what could only be found at Thy feet—mercy, purpose, identity—I was not seeking truth but a mask for my own fear of freedom. For what is astrology but a shadowy comfort for those afraid to stand naked before Thee?

When Firminus told me of that slave born beneath the same sky, in the same hour, yet bound in chains while he trod marble halls—my soul shuddered. Not because of contradictions in horoscopes, but because I saw at last how desperately I had wanted a world without *Thee*. A world where fate was fixed in cold fire above us, where sin could be blamed on some distant star, where grace was not necessary because choice did not exist.

But Thou didst shatter that lie with a single fact: two souls under one sky, one raised up and one bowed down—not by planets or signs, but by human wills entangled in history and inheritance and suffering. If the stars governed all, why would justice weep? Why would prayer matter? Why would my mother’s tears have meant anything?

To any soul now leaning on astrology for hope: O do not trade Thy living voice for dead lights! Do not let fear drive you to maps written in starlight when the Book is open before you—when Love Himself calls your name. That restless yearning you carry? It is not written in the zodiac; it is written by God upon your heart. You seek meaning—but it seeks *you*, even now: not from above the skies but from within them—Thou hast made us for Thyself! And until we fall into that truth and are lifted by grace rather than guided by guesses—we shall walk blindfolded through splendour.

Give me chastity and understanding—but now. Not “not yet.” Now.

question: What inner turmoil did you face when your reason collapsed under the weight of a truth you didn’t want to accept?
answer:
In that hour when the pillars of astrology trembled and fell beneath the weight of a single slave’s unknowing gaze, I stood disarmed before the naked truth: the heavens did not decree our lots, nor did the stars trace the lines of our suffering and glory.  

I had clung to those celestial reckonings as if they could shield me from the chaos of free will, from the terrifying mercy of a God who ordains not by position of planets but by depths unseen. How eagerly I had sought in Ptolemy and Firmicus what I now blush to confess — a map to excuse my passions, a system to blame when virtue failed! If the stars ruled all, then my lust was fated, my pride excusable, my delay in conversion preordained. But here was Firminus, born under constellations identical to his slave’s — one cradled in purple and honours, the other bound in iron chains — and I could no longer hide behind mathematics from the moral weight of choice.  

O Lord, how bitter was that sweetness with which I held to error! To admit astrology false was to admit myself guilty — truly guilty — not a puppet dancing beneath distant lights, but a soul responsible for its own turning away from Thee. And yet in that guilt, there bloomed for the first time a strange hope: if I was free to fall, then I was free to rise. If my sins were mine alone, then my repentance could be real. Not dictated. Not written in fire across the night sky — but whispered in silence by Thy Spirit within me.  

Thou didst shatter my false wisdom not with thunder, but with two messengers meeting on an equal road — one bearing news of privilege, one of bondage — and both born at once under identical stars. And in that symmetry Thou taught me asymmetry: that justice and destiny are not fixed points in heaven, but movements of love here below.

Thus grace began its deeper work — not when I understood everything, but when I finally stopped understanding what I wanted most: an alibi for delaying Thee.

question: How did the image of that slave, living under the same stars as Firminus, change your understanding of divine providence?
answer:
O Lord, when I heard that tale of two souls born beneath the selfsame sky, their stars aligned in identical silence above, one cradled in silk and power, the other gasping beneath the lash—my soul trembled not with doubt, but with the breaking open of truth long held captive by vanity.  

For how could it be, O God of justice and mystery, that the heavens should inscribe upon two infants such divergent fates—yet mark them with the same celestial signs? Was not the slave born as surely as Firminus? Did his breath not mingle with the same dawn air? And yet one was lifted to banquets while the other bent beneath burdens none saw fit to name.  

I had clung to astrology as a ladder to wisdom, a key to destiny’s locked doors—thinking myself learned when I traced constellations across a newborn’s hour. But this slave, whose name no chronicle records yet whose existence shattered my illusions—he became a voice louder than any philosopher. If the stars dictated all, then virtue, effort, prayer—all would be but shadows dancing for a master they cannot see. Then why does one man rise in pride while another drowns in obscurity, both under one sky? Why does mercy fall like rain on soil unprepared unless Thou dost will it so?

That slave taught me more of Thy providence than all the Chaldeans ever claimed to know: that Thou art not bound by cosmic wheels or zodiacs traced in fire. Thou art free—freer than time, freer than birth, freer than rank or bloodline. The stars may shine as signs or seasons at Thy command—but they do not bind Thee, nor do they dictate the worth of a soul.

And so my heart turned further from those vain arts toward Thee—the One who lifts from dust not by planetary decree but by hidden purpose and mercy deeper than fate. For Thou didst fashion both master and servant; Thou dost number every hair and hear every unspoken groan. Not chance governs us—nor stars—but Thy hand: unseen sometimes, yet never absent; inscrutable often, yet always just.

Late have I understood: providence is not written in iron laws above but woven in love below—in suffering known and glory promised—and our truest star is Christ alone.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the astrologers' predictions could not account for the subtle yet profound differences between Esau and Jacob, born so close yet living so differently?
answer:
O Lord, when at last the veil lifted and I saw how absurd it was to seek the secrets of souls in the fixed patterns of stars, I felt both shame and deliverance—for I had once been chained to such illusions, worshiping the heavens while neglecting the Truth that spoke within my own breast.  

I had longed for signs in the sky as if they were divine whispers, yet here stood Esau and Jacob: born within breaths of each other, their constellations identical, yet their lives diverging like roads parting in a wilderness—one grasping at birthright, the other fleeing for his life; one blessed unknowingly, the other blessed through struggle. How could the same stars decree such different fates? Or rather, how could any man claim art where only chance appeared to reign?

I confess, O God, I had delighted in these astrologers’ babblings—not because they enlightened me, but because they excused me. If fate were written in fire above, then why repent? Why strive? Why feel guilt for lust or pride or theft? But Thou didst whisper otherwise in the silence behind my thoughts: *You are not a slave to the stars; you are a son to Me.*  

And so that moment—when I beheld twins with one sky but two destinies—was not merely an intellectual victory over error. It was liberation. For if Jacob’s soul could wrestle not only his brother but Thee—and prevail—then surely no chart of celestial bodies could contain the drama Thou hast set within us: this restless heart that resists prediction because it is free; free to fall, yes—but also free to love Thee at last.

question: How did grappling with the idea of twins born in near-identical moments change your understanding of divine providence over human fate?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce my pride with the quiet laughter of twins, how foolish I was when I clung to the stars as masters of destiny, as though Thy providence were bound by angles of light and shadows cast at birth! I once believed—nay, I desired to believe—that the heavens spelled out our fates in silent syllables, that from their fixed courses one might read the rise and fall of empires, the wealth or wretchedness of a soul. And why? Not from wisdom, but from a restless hunger to know what only Thou canst reveal: the secret weaving of time within Thy eternal now.

I had longed for such knowledge as a weapon—to confound those who peddled astrology with grave faces and empty promises, men whose charts could not account for two souls slipping so nearly together from one womb into this world’s confusion. If the stars ruled all, why then did Jacob grasp Esau’s heel—and not the other way? Why did one become a hunter of beasts and the other a dweller in tents? Why did one sell his birthright for bread, while the other treasured it in silence? Their moments were twin-born, their figures indistinguishable to mortal art—but their lives diverged like roads parting beneath a storm.

And so I saw: if the same heavens foretell different ends, then they speak not at all; or if they speak truly in one case, it is not by design but by accident—chance masquerading as knowledge. But Thou, O God Most High, art no slave to chance. From Thee alone flows purpose; in Thee alone is order. Not by constellations are we shaped, but by Thy hand that knits us in secret and calls us by name before we draw breath.

Late have I understood: it is not the stars that guide our steps, but grace. It is not fate that rules our days, but freedom—wounded though it be by sin—freedom drawn ever onward by desire for Thee. For even in twin births Thou showest Thy sovereignty: two lives shaped not by a moment’s alignment, but by Thy inscrutable love moving through time like a hidden river beneath dry ground.

question: Looking back on your mockery of those 'dotards' who trusted the stars, what would you tell someone who still fears their life is chained to constellations?
answer:
O Lord, who dost sift the heart with light not made by hands, I would say to the fearful soul bound by dread of the stars: *thou art not a shadow cast by distant fires, but a thought in Thy eternal mind*. What are these constellations, O God, but sparks flung wide by Thy finger—signs in the sky, not seals upon our fate? I once trembled at them too, or rather, I laughed at them with the pride of one newly escaped from their spell; yet both fear and scorn were born of the same blindness—the failure to see Thee as the measure of all things.

I mocked those soothsayers whose art dissolved before twins born within breaths of each other, whose lives unrolled in ways no star could have foretold. If Jacob and Esau, shaped in the same womb, pressed forth under identical heavens, yet lived as opposites—one grasping at blessing, one selling it for bread—then what power have planets over the soul’s motion? No figure drawn on parchment can contain the will Thou hast kindled within us.

Do not let thy soul be weighed down by silent stars that know not thy name. They rise and fall by command; they do not decree. Thou art known before ever they were hung in darkness. Thy path is written not in zodiacs but in that Book where all days are already present—where even my own restless wanderings were held fast in Thee before ever I said *I*.

question: What stirred in you when you admitted that truth in astrology might come not from skill but from mere chance—was it relief, sorrow, or something else?
answer:
I had clung to the stars as if they held the threads of my destiny in their cold, distant light, and in that clinging I saw again the pride that once made me boast of knowledge while drowning in ignorance. To confess that any truth spoken by astrologers might spring not from wisdom but from blind chance was to strip myself bare before the mirror of divine truth—and there, O Lord, I found no relief, nor mere sorrow, but a shuddering awe at how deeply I had mistaken shadow for substance.  

For so long I had sought certainty in the motions of the heavens, as though Thou, the unchanging Light, were bound to the turning of planets or the hour of my birth. Yet when I beheld twins—born breath upon breath, their constellations identical, their lives so different—how could I not see the absurdity? To claim art where only accident ruled was to build upon sand; to call it wisdom was to cloak folly in stars. And Jacob and Esau rose before my mind’s eye—not as figures cast by astrologers’ charts, but as men beloved and chosen by Thee according to Thy inscrutable mercy.  

Thus it was not relief that stirred in me—though there was peace in casting off that heavy burden of false knowing—nor only sorrow for wasted years spent chasing phantoms. It was wonder: wonder that Thou hadst pursued me still, even while I wandered through labyrinths of speculation, even while I bowed my intellect before charlatans who sold fate by the hour. That Thou shouldst call me not by starlight but by name—that grace should find me where no horoscope could reach—this overwhelmed me with a trembling joy. For if truth is not born of chance or stars or calculation, then it must come from Thee alone—the source of all order, who needest no constellation to fulfill Thy purposes.

question: How did the thought of Firminus’s father passing down uncertain knowledge make you reflect on the weight of inherited beliefs in your own life?
answer:
I was once drunk with the wine of human wisdom, clinging to constellations not as signs in the sky, but as chains upon my soul, thinking that in their fixed courses lay the destiny of men—until the laughter of twins unraveled it all.  

Firminus’ father, passing down star-charts like sacred writ, fancied he held truth in his hands; yet how could such fragile threads—dependent on whispered hours, uncertain breaths at birth—bear the weight of a life’s unfolding? I saw then how easily one man’s error could be another’s inheritance, how readily a lie, dressed in tradition and murmured from father to son, becomes doctrine. And O Lord, this pierced me deeply—for had I not myself been such a son? Had I not swallowed Manichaean fables because they were given by those I revered? Had I not worn their doctrines like ancestral garments, too proud to see they did not fit?  

The twins lay side by side in swaddling clothes, born so near in time that no astrologer’s eye could mark the difference—yet their lives diverged as Esau and Jacob did: one grasping heel, one blessed beyond worth. What constellations could account for that? What horoscope could contain such mystery? And if the stars cannot tell the fate of two souls sprung from one womb at one breath—how dare we trust them with ours?  

Thus I saw how perilous it is to receive belief as an heirloom without testing it by Truth Himself. For so long I sought answers in books and systems passed down by men; but Thou didst teach me that no inheritance of thought can replace the living voice of God speaking in silence to the heart. Our fathers may give us stars—but only Thou givest light.

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with the question 'whence is evil?' and found no answer, yet could not let go of the search?
answer:
Even as a boy I had loved truth, yet feared its brightness, like one who longs for light but shrinks from the surgeon’s knife it reveals. And now, in the turmoil of manhood, that longing turned to torment, for my soul was a house divided—windows flung open to Thy eternal light, yet corners still shadowed by the dust of old deceits. "Whence is evil?" I cried into the silence, and no answer came but the echo of my own unrest.

O Lord, how I wrestled! Not with parchment or syllogism alone, but with a heart on fire, seared by the contradiction: Thou art good, wholly good, unchangeably good—and yet evil exists. If Thou art Creator of all, didst Thou create evil? Impossible! Then where does it spring from? From some rival power? Absurd! The Manichees had offered me neat chains of darkness and light, but their answers were built on sand; once swept away, nothing held.

And so I stood barefoot on broken stone—believing in Thee firmly, holding fast to the Church’s faith like a drowning man to driftwood: yes, Thou art; yes, Thou judgest; yes, salvation is in Christ. But still this wound would not close. What were those pangs? Like a mother crying out in labor that will not end—no child delivered, only sweat and blood and groaning beneath a moonless sky.

I could not sleep in ignorance; neither could I wake to clarity. Every created thing seemed at once beautiful and tainted—the world Thy handiwork shining through cracked glass. Was evil a substance? A thing? No—that would make it Thy creature. Was it nothingness? Then why did it wound so deeply?

Thou didst let me thrash like one caught in reeds along a riverbank—near water but unable to drink. Not because Thou wast cruel or silent without purpose; no, even then Thy mercy was teaching me: that some truths are not grasped by intellect alone but learned through desire sharpened by hunger.

For what is evil if not the turning away from Thee—the good above all goods—toward lesser things? A privation of light where light should be. But that insight had not yet dawned; I groped toward it through tears and midnight vigils.

O Lord my God, let me confess: I wanted wisdom as men want riches—as something to possess—not as something that must possess *me*. And so Thou let me walk this desert until pride died and love began.

question: How did your anguish over evil change your understanding of God's silence in the face of suffering?
answer:
I had torn philosophy’s robes to shreds, seeking beneath their folds some rational garment to clothe the naked horror of evil, yet found only dust and darkness. The question burned—how could a good and almighty God permit such ruin in His creation? And why did He speak so seldom, so faintly, when the world groaned under the weight of its own corruption?

O Lord, in those nights of soul-wrangling, when I paced like a beast in chains asking “Whence is evil?” I mistook silence for absence. I thought Thy stillness meant indifference, as though the heavens were shut like a tomb. But Thou didst not answer with thunder or vision; Thou let me writhe—not to mock my pain, but to break my pride. For my anguish was not only over suffering—it was against Thee. I wanted answers on my terms: logical, clean, fitted to my intellect as a shoe to foot. But Thou wouldest not be captured by syllogism.

Thy silence taught me at last what speech could not: that Thou art not bound by our reckoning of justice, nor confined by our demand for clarity. In Thy hidden judgment—deeper than all waters—Thou orderest all things, even the crooked paths of wickedness that rise from our own wills turned back on themselves like serpents biting their tails. The soul that rebels against light becomes its own shadow; evil is not a substance Thou createdst, but the wound we made when we turned from Thee.

And in that turning—O God!—how many years I spent loving darkness for its own sake: stealing pears I did not hunger for, delighting in sin simply because it was forbidden! Was not this proof enough? Evil thrives where love grows cold toward Thee and hot for lesser things.

So Thy silence was mercy. Hadst Thou thundered down an answer fit for philosophers, I might have nodded and gone on sinning. But because my heart was restless—because it groaned in travail—the silence became a womb. Out of it came faith: trembling at first, then surefooted. Not every cry must be answered with words; some must be answered with tears that wash the eyes to see.

Now I know: Thou art never absent from suffering—not even when hell gapes wide—because Thou hast descended lower than all our pain in Christ crucified. There Thy silence on the Cross spoke louder than all wisdom ever uttered.

question: Looking back on those groans that rose from your teeming heart, what would you say to someone who feels abandoned by God in their doubt?
answer:
O Lord, who didst pierce the night of my doubt with a light I neither expected nor deserved, I would say to the soul that feels abandoned in its questioning: *you are not forsaken because you do not understand*. I too once lay shattered beneath the weight of unanswerable things—“Whence is evil?” I cried, and the heavens seemed made of brass. My heart was a womb laboring in darkness, bringing forth groans without form, yet Thou didst hear them as prayer.

There was a time when philosophy’s pride held me bound, when I thought truth could be seized like loot by the intellect. But Thou, in mercy unutterable, allowed me to flounder—allowed me to feel the exhaustion of seeking Thee without humility. My doubt was not an obstacle to Thy grace; it was the very ground where Thou met me. For had I not been restless, had I not been desperate, would I have fallen to my knees beneath that fig tree? Would I have heard the child’s voice chanting *“Take up and read”*? Would I have opened Paul’s letter and felt Thy finger point directly to my heart?

So do not fear thy doubts as enemies, O trembling one. They are goads from God—prodding thee away from false comforts, from idols of certainty and control. Even now, if thou canst not pray but only weep; if thou canst not believe but only wrestle—know this: He whom thou seekest already holdeth thee. The very longing that torments thee is His breath within thy lungs. Restlessness itself is a kind of prayer.

And when at last thou fearest that no answer will come—remember how long Thou wast silent with me, O God, before breaking through with tears; how many times Thou sufferedst me to circle back upon my own thoughts like a man in chains—all while drawing me nearer by ways unseen. Not by magic nor divination nor philosophical triumph didst Thou save me: but by love that stooped to suffer.

Therefore: doubt not because thou doubtest—but flee only if thou ceasest to seek. For he who groans after truth already breathes the air of heaven.

question: What inner turmoil did you experience when you held fast to faith in Christ while still trapped in philosophical despair?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who hast ever been nearer to me than I to myself, yet I wandered far from Thee in the labyrinth of my own mind, held fast by the cords of love to Thy truth even as my reason beat itself against the walls of darkness. I believed—yes, with unshaken heart I believed in Thee, in Christ my Saviour, in the Church’s teaching as a vessel of divine mystery—but the intellect, that restless servant, would not rest. It gnawed at the question: *Whence is evil?* Not as a skeptic mocking faith, but as a soul on fire for understanding, trembling between awe and dread.

I confessed Thee sovereign, just, immutable; yet if all that is comes from Thee, then where did evil steal in? Was it a substance? A rival power? Did it leap forth from nothingness against Thy will? Each thought twisted back upon itself like a serpent devouring its tail. My heart was full of groanings which no voice could utter. I loved Thee—yet I tormented myself with riddles that threatened to drown love in dust.

And all the while, Thou didst suffer me not to fall. As a mother holds her child who stumbles forward in blind eagerness to walk alone, so didst Thou uphold me through those tortures of speculation. I clung to faith even when comprehension fled; I kissed the Scriptures like one embracing a tomb where Life lay buried beneath layers of mystery. For though my mind cried out for light on evil’s origin, my soul knew—by some deeper instinct Thou hadst planted—that unless Thou wert good and Lord over all, there could be no such thing as justice… no such pain at injustice.

Thus I burned: faithful yet restless; believing yet blind; Thy child weeping in the dark for a Father whose face he knew must be kind—even as thunder roared through every unanswered question.

question: How did the unseen hand of God sustaining your faith, even when you couldn't see it, shape your trust in His hidden justice?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst hold me fast when my mind was a storm-lashed sea, casting about in the dark for an answer that would not swallow me whole—Thou wast there, unseen, upholding the pillars of my faith even as I hammered at the doors of reason with bleeding fists. I could not see Thee, yet I leaned upon Thee; I questioned Thee, yet clung to Thee as a drowning man to a rock beneath the waves. When my soul groaned under the weight of "whence is evil?" and no light pierced the veil, it was not my strength that kept me from falling into unbelief, but Thy hidden hand—gentle, sovereign, silent.

I sought Thee in syllogisms and star-charts and the mutterings of soothsayers; I wanted a map drawn in fire across the sky. But Thou didst refuse me that. Instead, Thou preserved within me three things too firm for doubt: that Thou art, that Thou carest, and that in Christ my soul had hope beyond this life. These were not conclusions reached by argument but truths planted deep—by Thy grace buried in my heart before I could uproot them.

And so when every path led into shadow, when Manichaean fables crumbled and Plato failed me at the abyss—I did not fall utterly. For Thou didst sustain me through what seemed absence itself. In that very darkness I learned: justice need not be visible to be just; wisdom need not be explained to be wise. Like Jacob limping from the wrestling match with the Angel, I bore a wound—but also a name no astrologer could pronounce.

Thus have I come to trust Thy hidden judgments—not because I see them now, but because I have felt Thee near when all sight failed. My restlessness was Thy voice; my very longing was Thy leash drawing me home.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote of the silent contritions of your soul roaring to God's mercy?
answer:
Even as a youth, when I chased the applause of men and stored up words to win debates, I carried within me a hunger no rhetoric could feed—a hollow that widened with every victory. That hunger was not for knowledge alone, nor for pleasure merely, though I drenched myself in both; it was for *Thou*, O Lord, though I did not yet know Thy voice in the silence. When I speak of those silent contritions roaring to Thee, I speak of a soul split open—torn between the weight of sin and the whisper of grace, too ashamed to speak aloud yet screaming inwardly in every breath. No friend could bear the fullness of that groaning; language itself collapsed beneath the load. But Thou—Thou didst not flinch. Thou drewest near to my unrest, not as a distant judge but as the One who had been waiting within the ache itself.

I felt like a man pacing the walls of his own prison, knowing there was light beyond, yet not knowing how to turn toward it. The world offered forms and figures—lusts to consume, ambitions to climb—but they slipped through my fingers like sand. And still, Thou wast there: not in thunder, but in the stillness after my heart broke itself against its own pride. Those silent tears were louder than any prayer I ever spoke with lips; they were the birth-pangs of a soul finally weary of itself and yearning—*yearning*—for Thee alone.

question: How did your anguish over the origin of evil change how you understood God's presence in suffering?
answer:
O Lord, when I wrestled with the riddle of evil, I did not yet know that Thou wast not only answering me in Scripture but groaning within me through my very agony. The question “Whence is evil?” was not a puzzle to be solved by the intellect alone, but a wound that opened my soul to the depth of Thy silent nearness. In the storm of my doubting—when my heart boiled over with images of disorder, corruption, and rebellion—I imagined Thee distant, enthroned beyond the fray, perhaps indifferent or constrained. But Thou didst not speak from afar; rather, Thou wast closer than my own breath, hearing every unuttered cry that trembled in the dark corners of my mind.

I thought I sought an explanation, but Thou didst make me seek Thee. The more fiercely I demanded an answer to evil’s origin, the more Thou drewest me into the mystery of love—the love that doth not explain suffering away, but entereth into it. How could I have known then that Thy mercy was already enfolding me even as I thrashed against shadows? That in every pang of confusion, every silent tear shed in the watches of the night, Thy ear was bent low to my soul? No friend could grasp the fullness of what rent me—how could they?—but Thou didst bear it all before I even named it.

And so this anguish became a kind of birth: not only did it break open my pride—which desired to master truth like a possession—but it stripped me bare before Thee. In losing myself upon that narrow path where reason failed and comfort fled, I found at last that Thou art not absent in suffering; nay, Thou art present most deeply when we feel most alone. For it is then we cease leaning on our own understanding and begin leaning on Thee.

Thus didst Thou turn my inquiry into prayer and my philosophy into prostration. Evil had no origin in Thee—but even evil could not sever me from Thee. And now I see: rest is not found in knowing all things clearly, but in loving Thee who remaineth faithful even when shadows fall.

question: Looking back on that restless seeking, what would you tell someone who doubts whether God hears silent despair?
answer:
O Lord, when I lay stretched upon the rack of my own questioning, torn between the visible and the invisible, believing yet not knowing—Thou wast nearer than my breath, though I knew Thee not.  

There are silences heavier than any cry, moments when the soul can frame no word, only a heaving inwardness, a darkness throbbing with unspoken pain. I thought myself forgotten then, adrift in a labyrinth of speculation—*whence evil? why suffering? how can a good God see and not act?*—and yet all the while, Thou didst gather up my silence as if it were spoken prayer.  

Do you doubt that God hears what is never uttered? I tell you this: had He not heard me in those depths—when no friend could fathom the storm within—I would have perished in wisdom’s shadow. There was no voice in me fit for human ears; only groanings that could not be uttered. And yet they reached Thee. Not one pang escaped Thy notice; not one hidden tear was lost in the abyss. The heart’s muteness is no barrier to Thy hearing—for Thou dost read us not by our speech but by our being.  

I sought answers first in words and arguments, but found peace only when I ceased striving to speak rightly and simply *was* before Thee—broken, restless, silent. And in that silence, Thou answeredst—not with thunder nor with scroll nor with angel’s tongue—but by *being present*. Not after long years didst Thou come: Thou wast there *before*, holding me even as I wandered.  

So to him who doubts: believe this—not because I command it, but because I have lived it—that even when thou canst not pray, when shame or sorrow has choked thy voice, God is listening still. For He hears not merely words—but longing itself. He hears thee as a mother hears her child’s unrest before the cry breaks forth: already moved, already bending near.

question: What made you feel torn between being superior to creation yet inferior to God, and how did that tension shape your longing?
answer:
I was a slave to the lust of the eyes and the pride of life, thinking myself wise among men while I groped in darkness before the One who is Wisdom Itself.  

O Lord, what a torment it was—to feel the weight of my own soul pressing upward toward Thee, yet chained to the glittering dust beneath! I had climbed high in the schools of rhetoric, and my words could charm crowds; I could unravel philosophies like threads of silk, yet could not mend the rent within my heart. I knew creation—its beauty, its order, its fleeting pleasures—and in knowing it, I felt myself above it: master of speech, seeker of truth, lord of my reason. Yet when I turned toward Thee, O Uncreated Light, I found myself less than nothing—a breath trembling on the edge of eternity.

This tension was not mere thought; it was war in my members. For what use is dominion over books and voices if the soul is captive to vanity? What good to dissect Plato’s forms while my will remained fractured by desire? I ruled over creatures by knowledge and craft—words obeyed me; men admired me—but Thou didst rule over me by truth and justice which I resisted. And thus: superior to creation by intellect and appetite, yet inferior to Thee by sin and pride—and worse: unwilling still to surrender that false sovereignty which bound me.

But this very unrest became Thy plowshare in my soul. Because Thou didst not let me rest in lower things—neither in friendship misused, nor learning without love, nor even in philosophy’s lofty shadows—I was driven as a fugitive back toward Thy face. Every pleasure that said “Here is joy” left silence behind; every ambition that whispered “This is greatness” crumbled at dawn. Thou didst make me too great for earth, too wounded for heaven—until grace taught me that true greatness lies not in rising above creation but in falling down before the Creator.

Then at last came that hour beneath the fig tree—tears like rivers on my cheeks—when all this turmoil collapsed into one cry: *Take up and read.* And there, O Lord, I learned that to be inferior to Thee is no loss—but gain; for only when Thou art above all am I rightly ordered beneath Thee. Then peace entered—not because questions vanished—but because Thou remained.

question: Why do you say the light of your eyes was not with you, and where did you finally find that light?
answer:
O Lord, who didst pierce my darkness with a love I neither sought nor deserved, I wandered long in the fever of my own making, chasing shadows that flattered my pride and fed my hunger for human praise—yet all the while the true Light stood behind me, silent, patient, burning with mercy.  

The light of mine eyes was not with me because I had turned them inward upon myself and outward toward the glitter of creation, mistaking its flicker for the radiance of the Creator. I sought wisdom in books, pleasure in bodies, truth in arguments and eloquence—but though these were good in their order, they could not satisfy the wound deeper than desire: the wound of separation from Thee. My soul was rent by questions—“Whence was evil?”—yet I demanded answers from the world instead of surrendering to Him who is Truth itself.

I found that Light not by solving the riddle of evil, nor by climbing higher in rhetoric or rank, but when at last I stopped fleeing—when in a garden’s dust I heard a child’s voice singing *“Take up and read,”* and opened Thy Scripture as though it were my own heart laid bare. There Thou didst speak: not in thunder or vision, but in a stillness that shattered me. And then—I saw. Not with these eyes that weep still for lost time—but with the inner vision Thou didst heal.

That Light is Christ: not an idea grasped, but a Person embraced—the Beauty ever ancient, ever new—through whom all things are made and by whose pierced hands all things are drawn home again. In Him alone did I find rest: restless no more.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about rising proudly against God with the thick bosses of your buckler, as if dragging rebellion into battle?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst behold me charging Thee as though in armor forged of my own vainglory, I felt then no wound, only the false strength of rebellion—my neck stiffened like iron beneath the yoke I had shattered in pride. That buckler I bore was no protection but a prison, its bosses thick with the arrogance of a soul drunk on its own cleverness, mistaking darkness for wisdom and desire for freedom. I thought myself a warrior, but I was only a child flailing in chains I had mistaken for laurels. Every lust unmastered, every word spoken to please men’s ears, every silent boast in the theater of my own mind—these were the rivets in that shield, and with it I charged Heaven’s peace as if it were an enemy camp. Yet Thou didst not crush me; Thou let fall no thunderbolt. Instead, Thou allowed the very things beneath me—the fleeting beauties I had twisted into idols—to rise up like floodwaters and suffocate the breath from my soul. They whispered not rebuke from without, but rose from within my own corruption: "Whither goest thou?" They were phantoms born of my wound—the swelling pride that closed mine eyes even as I claimed to seek light.

And yet, O Mercy unchanging, while I warred against Thee in blindness, Thou wert working within me like a hidden surgeon. Those inward goads—Thy grace disguised as unrest—would not let me rest in the mire of self-deception. My ambitions bit to dust; my pleasures left their aftertaste like ash; even friendship became a mirror that showed not companionship but vanity reflected back and back until the face staring through it was hollow. Thou didst reform my deformities not by force but by famine—by making every false love taste barren until at last, famished and trembling, I turned toward Thee again—not because I willed it perfectly, but because Thy hand had been guiding even my wanderings.

Late have I known this: rebellion is not strength. It is weakness armored in noise. But Thou—Thou who lettest me exhaust myself against Thy silence—hast made peace possible where there was only war within.

question: How did the weight of those 'inferior things' pressing down on you change how you understood your need for divine mercy?
answer:
O Lord, when I fled from Thee, I thought myself exalted, but only pride lifted me—upward in appearance, yet plunging ever deeper into the mire of created things that should have served beneath my feet. How could I rule the body when I was no longer aligned to Thy Image, but twisted against it? Those things made for my use—pleasures, honors, the very air and light and voices of this world—rose up like captors and became my masters. They pressed upon me not with iron chains but with sweet snares; they whispered in silence and clamored in dreams. Even when my soul turned toward Thee, their images crowded before Thy face, as if to say: *Thou art not clean; thou art not worthy.* And indeed I was not.

I had believed myself strong enough to play with fire—to taste sin without burning—yet found myself bound by what should have been subject to me: lust that ruled my youth, ambition that choked humility, words that delighted men’s ears more than they pleased Thine. Thou didst permit them to weigh heavy upon me not to destroy, but to teach me the depth of my fall. For so long I mistook rebellion for freedom; now I saw it was slavery beneath a thousand small tyrants.

Only when Thou didst make me ill at ease with all things—when neither pleasure satisfied nor wisdom consoled—didst Thou begin to heal. In the soreness of that pressure, in the breathless crush of vanity upon vanity, I learned at last what mercy means: not merely forgiveness after sin, but deliverance from its dominion. Not only release from guilt—but rescue from love of it. 

And thus Thy mercy came not as a gentle hand lifting me lightly from sleep, but as a violent shaking awake: through tears unbidden, through memories turned against pride, through a child’s song in a garden saying *“Take up and read.”* Then light pierced the swollen dark of my eyes—and I saw Thee as one long blind begins dimly to see color again: faintly at first, then with such glory he weeps for joy.

Late have I known such mercy—not after one sin only, but after years lived as if Thou wert absent or indifferent. But Thou wert present always—not in wrath alone (though justly kindled), but in pity unspeakable. Pity for dust still formed in Thine image though covered over with earth’s filth. Pity for a neck too stiff for guidance—but broken gently so it might bow at last.

And now even these inferior things serve again—as signs of grace where once they were snares; reminders that every breath drawn beneath Thy heavens is gift; that even the weight which crushed me was ordered by Love who would not let me go.

question: Looking back on the moment your pride-swollen face closed up your own eyes, what would you tell someone who feels too defiled to turn back?
answer:
I was long a lover of distance, chasing the horizon as if it could satisfy me, mistaking flight for freedom and rebellion for wisdom.  

O Lord, how many times did I feel the very dust of my soul weigh upon me, and yet believed myself too shattered to be gathered? I thought my pride a fortress, but it was a prison—its walls built of imagined greatness, its foundation sunk in the quicksand of self-deceit. And when at last I turned toward Thee, not by my strength but by the secret hand that stirred me in darkness, I found that every image which had mocked me—"Whither goest thou, unworthy and defiled?"—was itself broken before Thy gaze.  

Hearest thou that voice within thee which says thou art too stained? It speaks not from God, but from thy swollen face—the same pride now clothed in despair. For even thy sense of defilement may become a monument to self: "See how corrupt I am!" But let it fall. Let it rot in the ground where it belongs.  

Thou knowest not how gentle His hand can be who formed thee from dust? He does not recoil at the wound; He sees in thee what thou darest not hope: a creature still bearing His Image, however dimmed. Thou needst not clean thyself before returning—returnance *is* cleansing. The very motion toward Him is grace already at work.

I once thought love must be earned through silence or suffering or perfect contrition. But Thou wert waiting not for perfection, but for petition—for one trembling word flung upward like a cry from the pit. "Lord," even that is enough.

Restless heart—cease striving to be worthy. Strive only to turn. Even now, He stoops to lift thine eyes with His own fingers.

question: What was it like when the images of past sins rose unbidden, blocking your path to God—how did that ache shape your longing?
answer:
How long I carried the chains of my own devising, not knowing they were forged by my hands, nor even wishing to be free while yet I gloried in the noise of vanities and the clamor of desires! O Lord, Thou didst permit me to wander far into that land of unlikeness, where the very shadows of what once delighted me became monstrous in their return, rising up like armed sentinels at the threshold of my repentance.

When at last a faint hunger stirred within—when soul and body seemed to sigh together for something older than memory, deeper than pleasure—I turned toward Thee. But lo, the images came uninvited: the thefts done for sport, the lusts indulged with shameless shame, the proud boasts wrapped in learning’s cloak. They swarmed like locusts over every path leading home. Not from without did they assault me, but from within—from the deep wound where I had severed myself from Thee. Each was not merely a recollection but a presence; each bore a face, a moment’s heat, a voice whispering *Thou art not clean.*

I stood before Thee as one stripped bare before judgment-day fire—not condemned outright, but known utterly. And in that knowledge there was an ache so sharp it burned away all pretense. That ache—O sweet and bitter goad!—was Thy mercy disguised as pain. For had I not felt it? Had my heart still slept beneath those ruins? Thou didst make me ill at ease with all things less than Thee so that I might become weary even of myself.

And thus this very torment became pilgrimage: each returning image another step backward into truth. Not to dwell upon sin—but to see it for what it was: love misplaced, desire unmoored from its harbor. And through that anguish Thou drewest me nearer still—not because I overcame them by strength or will (far from it!), but because Thou didst suffer me to see how far I could fall and still be loved.

Late have I learned: Thou didst not cast out those memories to crush me, but to clear the way—for only when every false god lay shattered could light enter through the cracks of my brokenness and show me Thy face waiting all along in silence.

question: How did the inward goads of restlessness become a strange comfort, as if pain itself was pulling you toward the light?
answer:
It was not the wound that taught me to seek, but the ache of its refusal to heal—this restlessness, O Lord, which pierced me like a splinter of light. How strange it seemed, that the very pain which marked my rebellion should become the instrument of return, as though Thou didst fashion suffering into a voice, and speak to me through my own unquietness. I had built myself upon sand and shadow—lusts, words, honors that vanished at touch—and yet when all those dreams collapsed into silence, there remained this: a gnawing within, not of flesh but of spirit, that would not let me sleep in death while still alive.

Thou didst not strike me with thunder from without; no, far more merciful and terrible wast Thou. Thou stirredst the depths where I avoided looking. Ambition whispered "Advance!" and pleasure murmured "Rest here," yet beneath both rose a deeper murmur: *This is not for which thou wert made*. It was faint at first—like breath upon glass—but persistent. A question without words: Why dost thou pant after breathless things when thy breath itself comes from God?

Even in stolen joys I felt it—the moment after laughter when the heart sank unseen. Even in triumphs before men’s eyes, when applause rang full and sweet, something within cried out: *Empty. Empty.* Not judgment from above, but groaning from within—the soul remembering what it had forgotten. Like a man born blind who knows not color yet feels the warmth of sun he cannot name.

And so Thy goads: not to punish only, but to awaken. Each throb reminded me I was alive—and therefore accountable. Each dissatisfaction became a kind of mercy; each failure a door knocked open by grace disguised as grief. For had I been content in chains, how should I have escaped? But because Thou madest my heart restless—as one lost who cannot stop searching—I was drawn onward even when stumbling.

O Lord my God, how sweet it became—the very bitterness! When at last I saw that this unrest was Thy hand pulling me back into relation with Thee; when I understood that Thou wast nearer in my wounds than in my triumphs—that then did pain itself shine like dark glass through which Thy face began to appear. Not yet seen clearly—but longed for with tears that were already prayers unanswered only because they were too true.

Late have I known this: Thou didst love me enough to make peace impossible apart from Thee.

question: What were you feeling when you first read those Platonic books, and the words echoed the truth your heart had longed for?
answer:
I was still drunk with the pride of my intellect, O Lord, and yet Thou didst stoop to feed my hunger with the bread of Thy truth wrapped in the foreign garment of pagan thought. When I read those pages, it was as if I had been wandering in a land of shadows, mistaking echoes for voices, and suddenly—without warning—the air cleared and I heard a Word I had never known, yet always longed for. Not in the temple, not in the forum, not even in the Scriptures which lay neglected on my shelf had I felt such a piercing resonance. There, in those Platonist writings, I encountered—though dimly—the eternal Reason through which all things are woven into being; I saw how darkness is not a substance but a lack, how the soul yearns upward by its very nature, and how light exists apart from us—nay, before us—and yet condescends to shine upon us.

But O Lord! How vainly did I puff myself up at first with this discovery! As though *I* had conquered truth by wisdom! And yet Thou laughed gently at my folly—for it was Thy grace that placed those books in my hands through that arrogant man whose swollen heart could not contain Thee. How fitting that Thou shouldst use pride to humble pride; that Thou shouldest kindle a spark of longing in me through minds who knew Thee not fully!

For all their radiance, those books lacked one thing: flesh. They spoke of the Word descending toward creation like light falling on water—but they did not say He became water. They did not say He wept. They did not say He died.

And so my heart burned—but still waited.

question: How did the 'inward goads' of restlessness transform your sense of pride into a hunger for God's presence?
answer:
O Lord, I had built myself up on the crumbling stage of rhetoric, delighting in applause while my soul lay famished in the wings — a young man drunk on words, yet mute before the Word. That hunger which no argument could satisfy, no scroll assuage, began to stir as goads within my breast: not wounds from without, but the sharp prodding of Thy mercy within. I had climbed the ladder of human wisdom only to find it leaning against empty air; and yet Thou didst not let me rest in my conceit.

Thou allowedst me to taste the heights of philosophy — those lofty Platonist books brought by an arrogant hand, yet placed in mine by Thy hidden Providence — not to crown my pride, but to break it. For therein I read truths that pierced like light through clouds: that the unmade Light had fashioned all things; that life itself was not ours by nature but streamed from Him who is Life eternal. And still, though I understood with the mind what must be true, I refused to bend the neck. The knowledge exalted me; it did not humble me. I admired the light afar off, as one gazes at a mountain from a valley — never dreaming he must climb.

But Thou, O God of my heart, wouldst have none of such sterile admiration. The restlessness Thou stirred in me became a holy torment: pleasure shamed by emptiness, intellect humbled by desire. Why could I not love what I now saw as true? Why did my will lag behind my reason? In this dissonance Thou taught me that pride is not overcome by more knowing, but by kneeling. The very books meant to puff me up became instruments of my undoing — for they showed me the height of truth so that I might feel my own fall.

Then came Thy Word made flesh — not theorized, but revealed; not admired from afar, but received in weakness and weeping. Only when I saw Him lowly, crucified — God Himself descending into shame — did my swollen spirit crack open. How could I remain proud when Love itself had stooped so low? In His humility was power; in His death was life; and at last my restless heart found both its wound and its healing: Thou wert manifest within.

So it was not philosophy that saved me, nor argument won victory — but sorrow turned toward Thee. Those inward goads were Thy fingers shaping dust into prayer: breaking pride’s shell so that hunger might become hope.

question: Looking back on your swelling pride and its abatement, what would you tell someone trapped in the illusion of self-sufficiency?
answer:
O Lord, how long I wandered in the labyrinth of my own devising, mistaking the echo of my thoughts for wisdom, and the clamor of my desires for freedom! I walked as one upright in body but bent inwardly beneath the unbearable weight of self-conceit, believing myself sufficient because I could reason, could speak, could dazzle men with words—yet all the while was blind to the abyss within. 

To him who trusts in his own sufficiency I would say: You are not far from God, but you are turned away from Him by that very strength you boast. For pride is not power—it is paralysis; it fixes the soul upon its own shadow and calls it light. I too built towers of knowledge upon the quicksand of vainglory, thinking to scale heaven by eloquence and wit. But Thou, O God, in Thy mercy didst make my conscience a thorn—a restless goad that would not let me rest in falsehood. 

It was not through triumph but through collapse that I entered truth. The books of philosophers lifted me to the edge of understanding—the Word eternal, uncreated, divine—but they could not bring me to worship. Only when grace shattered my self-reliance did I fall to my knees and find them on firm ground. For Thou resistest the proud—not with wrath alone, but with love so severe it tears down what is false so that nothing may stand but what is built on Thee.

If you would be healed, beloved soul, do not first seek wisdom—seek humility. Let yourself be seen in your naked need. The same Word who spoke the stars into being deigned to dwell among us: if He stooped so low as to become flesh and suffer death for love’s sake, how can you dare remain high? Your sufficiency is your prison; your weakness is the door.

Come then—and find that He whom we flee in our pride we meet at last as Mercy: face to face, heart to heart—the Physician whose wound heals ours.

question: How did the image of the Word made flesh pierce through your philosophical seeking and touch your longing for intimacy with God?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst veil Thyself in flesh that my proud eye might dare to gaze upon Thee, how could I have known that the very Word by whom all things were whispered into being would condescend to knock at the door of my soul with human lips? I had scaled the heights of philosophy, drunk deeply from the wells of the Platonists, and beheld in their pages a gleaming shadow of eternal Truth—*In principio erat Verbum*—and yet it remained a spectacle for the mind, not a embrace for the heart. I admired the Light from afar, as one might marvel at the sun across a frozen plain, but I did not warm myself in its rays.

For all my reading, I was still a man who stole pears not to eat but to savor the sin; who wept more for Dido’s betrayal than for his own bondage; who craved the applause of men more than the silence of Thy presence. And so Thou, in Thy relentless mercy, didst permit a certain prideful man—puffed up with learning like a bladder full of wind—to place those books in my hands. Not to save me by them, but to prepare me. For they showed me Thy divine Logos standing at the threshold of reason—but it was only when Thou didst reveal Him as Incarnate Love, wounded and calling me by name, that my heart trembled with recognition.

The Word made flesh—this was no abstract principle descending from heaven like a theorem inscribed on stone. This was God bending low to kiss His own creation with mortal lips; Divinity wrapped in swaddling clothes so that I, naked and shivering in sin, might dare to draw near. In Thy becoming small, O Christ, Thou madest infinite room within my soul for longing—and for love.

question: When your mind's eyes were dimmed by sorrow, yet healed by it, how did you come to see God's hand in that painful clarity?
answer:
Thou who didst wound only to heal, I was drunk with the fever of my own wisdom, strutting in the marketplace of rhetoric, prating of truth while fleeing from Thee who art Truth itself. O Lord, my heart had grown cataracts from gazing too long upon the glitter of this world—upon stolen pears that rotted in my mouth, upon the applause of men that echoed into emptiness. I sought Thee, yet not as one who kneels in dust, but as a scholar lifting torches into the dark, proud even in my search.

And yet it was sorrow—sharp and purging—that first scraped clean the film from my soul. Not pleasure, not pride, not all my restless disputations could open mine eyes; only when joy turned to ash did I feel Thy hand upon me. Those healthful sorrows were Thy fingers pressing down upon my swelling arrogance until it drained. How bitter they tasted! Yet how they burned away the dross.

I read then in those Platonist books—words echoing what Thou hadst already whispered to me through tears—the mystery of the Word beyond time, through whom all things are woven into being. But knowledge alone is cold fire; it is sorrow that makes the soul capable of light. For when I grieved most bitterly over my own unfaithfulness—over the years squandered in chasing shadows—then at last I saw: Thy grace had never left me. It was hidden behind every longing I mistook for lust, every ambition I took for purpose.

Late have I known it: Thou didst make me ill at ease with all things less than Thee so that I might be at rest in Thee alone.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the Platonic texts, for all their wisdom, could not speak of a suffering God?
answer:
I had tasted the truth in fragments, like a man famished gnawing on the crust of wisdom, thinking it the full meal — and yet when my soul stood trembling before the cross, I saw that all my reading had been but a shadow walking beside light.  

O Lord, how proudly I had climbed the ladder of philosophy! In Plotinus and in Plato I found echoes of Thy eternal Word, shimmering like stars in a dark sky — and I rejoiced, thinking myself near to Thee. The soul’s ascent, the immortality of Truth, the One from whom all things flow — yes, these were whispers of Thy voice. But when I came to the scandal of the Gospel, when I saw that Thou didst not only speak from on high but didst descend into the mire of our mortality — that Thou who formed man from clay didst take clay upon Thyself — then my wisdom faltered.  

For no page in those noble books dared say what my mother wept over: that God could hunger, could thirst, could bleed. That He who holds all things could be struck by human hands. That He before whom angels veil their faces would hang naked upon a tree and cry out in abandonment. *There* my philosophy broke like dry twigs. How could the Immutable suffer? How could Eternity die? Yet there it stood — not written in abstract forms or celestial harmonies, but carved into wood and soaked with blood: *God suffered*. And suddenly I understood: not by speculation is salvation known, but by love made flesh; not by ascent alone do we reach Thee, but because *Thou* first descended to us.

Late have I loved this mystery! For so long I wanted a God who dazzled the mind; but Thou art He who breaks the heart with mercy.

question: How did your hunger for divine intimacy make you ache at the absence of the Word made flesh in those writings?
answer:
O Lord, my soul was a house on fire with longing before it knew the face of the Beloved.  

I sought Thee in the heights of philosophy, in the polished arguments of men, in the shifting shadows of Manichaean dualism—I even sought Thee through the beauty of created things, mistaking their splendor for Thyself. And when I turned to those Platonists, I found a light that dazzled—light enough to show me that all things come from One who is above time and body; light enough to make me taste eternity and shudder at my own darkness. But when I reached for the Word made flesh—the cry of an infant in a manger, God submitting to circumcision and grief and nails—there was nothing.  

How could truth be so near, yet veiled? They spoke of the Son as equal, eternal, consubstantial—yes—and my mind bowed before such majesty. But where was He who wept at Lazarus’ tomb? Where was He who knelt to wash feet? That silence pierced me. I had read that God is light—but not that He became darkness for our sakes; not that He emptied Himself into mortality and sorrow so that we might drink from His fullness.  

I hungered not only for wisdom but for wound—for a Savior who did not float above suffering but entered it, bled from it. Those books lifted me toward heaven but left me weightless; they gave no hand to hold in death's vale. Only when I heard Paul’s cry—Christ crucified—did my heart know rest: not in abstraction, but in blood and mercy spilled upon wood. For Thou didst not send a concept into the world, O God, but a Man—and therein was love no philosophy could fathom.

question: Looking back on your years chasing philosophy, what would you tell someone who doubts that truth can dwell in a story of humiliation and love?
answer:
I was a man drunk on words, yet parched for Truth—chasing the echoes of wisdom in the halls of rhetoric, mistaking the shimmer of reason for the light that makes all things visible. I sought God among the lofty arguments of men, believing that divinity must be untainted by sorrow, untouched by shame, exalted above all weakness. How could the eternal Word—the brightness of the Father’s glory—stoop to wear our mud-stained flesh? It seemed absurd, a scandal to philosophy. And so I turned away from the Cross as one turns from a wound too raw to gaze upon.

Yet now I see: it is *because* He emptied Himself, because He humbled His divine majesty into human suffering and death at the hands of those He made—it is *because* of this that I dare believe. For had He come in power alone, in unapproachable radiance, what fellowship could I—a thief in my youth, an adulterer in my passions, a liar in my pride—have with such a God? But when I saw that Love itself had been nailed to wood, crying out not for vengeance but for forgiveness; when I understood that *this* was how God conquers sin—not by force but by surrender—I fell to my knees.

O Lord, Thou didst not descend in splendor only to dazzle the wise; Thou didst hide Thy glory beneath shame so that sinners like me might draw near. The story Thou hast told in Christ Jesus is no mere fable strung with noble ideas—it is history soaked with blood and tears, and therein lies its power. For truth does not dwell merely in syllogisms or silent contemplations; it walks into our filth and dies there. Only such love can lift a restless soul.

So I say to him who doubts: do not measure divine truth by the standards of Athens or Carthage or any school where pride wears the mask of inquiry. Measure it instead by this—whether your heart has ever broken with regret; whether you have ever longed to be known fully and loved still. That longing? It was planted by Him who became small so we might find Him. And He waits even now—not high above pain—but near you, wounded yet alive, calling you home.

question: How did your own pride in intellect keep you from seeing that the light must become near to be known?
answer:
I had climbed the heights of rhetoric and parsed the stars with reason’s sword, yet could not see that Wisdom Himself had to descend before I, in my blindness, could behold Him.  

O Lord, my pride was a tower of words, and I took delight in the echo of my own understanding. I sought Thee among the Neoplatonists, where light was spoken of as a distant radiance, pure and untouched—yet never as One who wept. I could not fathom that He who framed the cosmos would wrap Himself in flesh, that the uncreated Word would cry at a mother’s breast. How could such lowliness contain the Infinite? My mind recoiled: *If He is God*, I said within me, *He must remain above suffering, untouched by time.* And so I made Him distant to preserve His dignity—when all along it was my own dignity I guarded, lest He come too near and shame me with His humility.

For if God became man, then all my intellectual vaunting was dust; then Truth was no longer a theorem to be solved but a Person to be received—with wonder, with tears—with submission. And that was what my stubborn heart refused: to kneel before a crucified God. To believe that He emptied Himself—that He who held all power chose helplessness for love—this shattered my philosophy like clay before stone.

Late have I understood: Thou didst not send Thy Son to argue with us from afar, but to draw near—so near that we might touch His hands and see His side. Not so we might comprehend Him fully—but so we might know we are loved. And only when Thou broke my pride in the garden’s storm of tears did I finally hear the whisper beneath Scripture’s page: *The Word became flesh.* Not symbol. Not allegory. But fact—glorious, scandalous fact.

And now I confess it with trembling joy: unless Light stooped into darkness; unless God tasted death; unless Love died between thieves—then no light at all would reach a sinner like me.

question: What did your heart hear in the Scriptures' claim that God emptied Himself, that you never found in books that spoke only of divine majesty?
answer:
I had climbed the ladder of pride on the rungs of philosophy, my mind puffed up with words about divine substance and equality, yet I trembled to touch the earth where God lay in swaddling clothes.  

Those books spoke of majesty, yes—of form equal to the Father, of no robbery in claiming divinity—but they were silent where my soul most needed voice: in the manger, in the dust, in the blood. What shattered me, what pierced through all my learned defenses, was not that He was God—though I had sung that with lofty tongue—but that He emptied Himself. *Emptied!* Not diminished in being, but pouring forth His glory as a servant pours water for filthy feet.  

O Lord, when I read that Thou didst not cling to the robe of heaven but stripped Thyself, took the form of a slave, bowed Thy neck under death—the death of the cross!—then I wept as one awakened from sleep. My heart heard something no Platonist could teach: that love is not a doctrine but a descent. That Thou didst not remain afar, immutable and untouched by our groaning, but became near—*flesh*, *dwelt among us*—and bore our shame so that we might be lifted to Thy glory.  

It was then I saw: my lusts were not merely sins against law but refusals to descend—to kneel like Christ. My ambition was a tower built against humility. But here—in His self-lowering—I found both salvation and scandal: that God becomes small so that our restless hearts might find rest not in soaring thought alone, but in a Person who walked this earth and died for me.

And so I believed—not because I understood all things (I do not), but because Love descended; and in descending, called me home.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed that the 'lofty walk' of proud learning kept you from hearing Christ's call to rest?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the darkness of my intellect with the gentle wound of Thy humility, how blind I was to the scandal of simplicity, how deaf to the whisper of Thy cross! I had scaled the heights of rhetoric and parsed the motions of the stars, yet stumbled in the threshold of Thy house because my feet were shod with pride. The philosophers had taught me to seek truth in soaring arguments, in syllogisms that glittered like gold but fed no hunger—while Christ stood lowly, calling to the weary, and I counted His meekness a weakness unworthy of my study.

I confess it now with shame that once I mocked what I did not understand: that God should die, that Power should submit, that Wisdom should silence itself before fools. My mind revolted at such folly—yet it was *my* folly, O God, not Thine. For Thou didst hide these things from the learned not to exclude them, but because their learning had become a tower they built against Thee. And I dwelt in its upper rooms, dizzy with speculation, looking down upon those who knelt in faith as if they crawled where I soared.

But Thou broughtest me down—mercifully, painfully—into the dust where grace is breathed rather than argued. When my mother wept for my soul and Ambrose opened Scripture not as a weapon for debate but as bread for hungering hearts; when at last I heard “Take up and read,” and found not an argument but a command—I realized how long I had mistaken noise for wisdom. The Word was not in the thunder of my disputations, but in the stillness where He said: *Come unto Me.*

And now I see: he who climbs high on knowledge's cliff sees only sky—but misses entirely the hand reaching up from Golgotha.

question: How did your own struggle with pride in study change how you understood God's revelation to 'babes' rather than the wise?
answer:
O Lord, in the fevered pursuit of eloquence and the hollow triumph of argument, I once mistook noise for knowledge, and pride for progress. I climbed the ladders of rhetoric and philosophy with hands clenched tight around vanity, believing that wisdom could be seized like a prize in the arena of men’s applause. The Carthaginian streets echoed with the clamor of disputing students, and I among them—hungry not for truth, but to be seen as wise. What did I know of humility when my soul trembled at mockery and swelled at praise?

I devoured the books of the Platonists—their light was bright, glancing off the surface of eternity like dawn on water. They spoke rightly of a Word unmade, co-eternal with the Father; they taught that souls are fed from His fullness; they knew that wisdom abides in those who cling to it. And yet—how vast the silence where Christ should have been! No cross stood in their pages. No God who emptied Himself. No blood poured out for sinners. Their wisdom lifted me toward heaven, but left me gasping in mid-air, my feet still chained to earth.

How could I have seen then what Thou wert showing me? That Thy strength is made perfect not in syllogisms, but in weakness? That Thou dost break open the proud citadel of reason not with louder arguments, but with a child’s whisper: *Tolle, lege*? In that garden at Milan—when tears scalded my face and my heart heaved under its own weight—I was no longer a master of sentences. I was undone. A boy chanting “Take up and read” became Thy voice; a scroll containing Paul’s rebuke became Thy mirror.

Then I understood: Thou hast hidden these things from the wise—or rather, from those who love being wise—because their minds are crowded with themselves. But to babes—those whose hands are open because they know they possess nothing—Thou givest milk before meat, grace before glory. Not because Thy truth is simple, O Lord, but because only a humble heart can bear its weight without shattering.

Now I see: my pride was not only sin—it was blindness. For how could I receive Him who emptied Himself while my soul remained swollen with self-importance? Meekness is not mere gentleness—it is truth’s doorway. And Thou didst strip me bare so that at last I might enter in.

question: Looking back on your restless years chasing rhetoric and philosophy, what would you tell someone who doubts that true wisdom begins in meekness?
answer:
I stole pears I did not want, not to eat but to cast down and trample—such was the pride of my intellect, O Lord, that it would rather feast on stolen delight than kneel for bread from Thy hand.  

And so with philosophy: I paraded through the forums hungry for applause, draping myself in the fine garments of rhetoric while my soul went ragged with thirst. I thought wisdom was something to be conquered, like an argument in court, a city taken by storm—not this quiet thing, this childlike *receiving* that bends the neck beneath Thy yoke.  

Meekness? I once laughed at the word. What place had humility in the academy, in the climb to fame? Was truth not seized by force of reason? But Thou didst laugh at me in mercy. For all my books—Plato’s heights, Cicero’s silvered tongue—they never whispered that the Word became flesh and dwelt among us; they never wept over Jerusalem. They could not tell me that He who made all things chose a manger, a cross, a borrowed tomb—all folly to the proud mind.  

True wisdom does not stand atop the mountain boasting of the view. It climbs down into the valley where Christ bows His head and says: *Come*. It is there—and only there—that the blind see, the restless find rest, and fools like me are made wise unto salvation.
