question: When you speak of rejoicing with trembling, what moment first made you grasp that love demands both gratitude and fear?
answer: In the fever of my youth, when I sought truth in the glare of human eloquence and mistook pride for wisdom, I did not know that love could wound as gently as it heals. It was only when my soul—long swollen with knowledge, yet starved of charity—was pierced by the humility of Paul’s words, that I first trembled before the face of God. O Lord, how could I have thought myself wise when I scorned Thy mercy? That moment, kneeling beneath the weight not of argument but of grace, when Thy truth silenced my cleverness—I saw that to love Thee is not to conquer but to surrender. And in that surrender, joy came not as laughter, but as tears: a rejoicing born of fear, like a child weeping in the arms of a father he has long resisted yet always longed for. For what am I before Thee but dust who dared believe he could fly? And yet Thou didst draw me close—not through triumphs of mind, but through the breaking of heart. Thus I learned: gratitude and fear are not divided, but one breath in the soul that finally sees—Thou art God.
chunk_key: nt, yet I did not mourn, but rather scorn, puffed up with knowledge. For where was that charity buil

question: What were you feeling when you confessed your inner struggle against the law of sin warring in your members, as if chained to a will not yet fully surrendered to God?
answer:
I was a prisoner not with chains of iron, but with the invisible yet unbreakable cords of habit and delight, dragged along by the will that once chose and now could not unchoose. O Lord, I knew the law within me that delighted in Thee, that yearned for Thy light as the eye longs for daybreak—yet another force held me fast, a law written not in my conscience but in my very flesh, rebelling against what my soul confessed to be true. I stood divided within myself, not as two wills contending for mastery, but as one will torn apart by its own weakness, wishing to fly toward Thee yet rooted in the mire of delay and desire.

How bitter was that bondage which masqueraded as pleasure! I loved Thee, yet refused to surrender the things that kept me from Thee. I cried out in silence: "Give me purity—but not yet!" As if time could be bargained with, as if grace were a debt to be paid slowly. But Thou, O God of all mercy, didst not abandon me in that wretchedness. Even while I was captive to the king of death, Thou wast drawing near—not with thunder, but with whisper; not with force, but with longing deeper than mine.

What could wretched man do? Only this: fall. Fall on Thy breast like a child too weary to stand. Confess not only sin, but helplessness. Not only lust or pride or vanity—but the deeper sin: trusting myself. And when at last I let go of that trust, when I flung myself down before Thee in utter surrender—then didst Thou lift me up. Not because I had struggled enough or understood enough or deserved anything—but because Thy Grace is strong where we are weak.

So it was not victory I felt at first, but relief—a groan turned into prayer became a door. And through it entered He who said: *"Come unto Me all ye who labor and are heavy laden."* Then did my heart begin to believe what my mind had long debated—that Thou art not only righteous Judge, but healing Physician; not only Lawgiver, but Lover of souls.

question: How did your encounter with Scripture's harmony with the Law and the Prophets change how you understood Your own longing for truth and the fragility of human insight?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the darkness of my intellect with the quiet light of Thy constancy, how blind I was when I sought truth as one might hunt for spoils—eager, proud, devouring words as if they could feed the soul by reason alone! I had drunk from so many cisterns cracked with human vanity: the subtle windings of the Manichees, the lofty disdain of the Academics, the glittering emptiness of rhetoric that taught me to win arguments but not to love Thee. And yet, when at last I turned—ah, not by my own strength, but by that secret hand which plucked me as a brand from the burning—I found in Thy Scriptures not a new philosophy to master, but a mirror to my ruin and a cradle for my return.

For long I scorned that sacred text because it lacked what my swollen mind desired: ornament, argumentation, the pride of novelty. But when Thy silence broke within me—when I heard Paul’s cry wrestle with the law within his flesh—I trembled. Here was no mere system of thought; here was life laid bare before Thee. The very conflict I felt—the will divided against itself, delighting in truth while chained to lust and ambition—was not hidden under pious phrases but confessed in blood and breath. And then I saw: this Book agrees with itself not because its words are cleverly joined, but because Thou art one. The Law pointed to Thee. The Prophets cried for Thee. And now this grace in Christ reveals Thee—not far off, not veiled in riddles meant only for sages—but near, healing the seer so he may see.

Before this revelation, every truth I grasped became fuel for pride: “I understand,” I would say—and thus lost all understanding. But now I learned fear with joy; now every insight came wrapped in gratitude and trembling: *Who am I that Thou shouldst open mine eyes?* Not because my mind surpassed others', but because Thou didst stoop to call my name while I wandered like a thief in dark orchards.

O God, how fragile is human insight! It reaches toward light even as it stumbles into shadow; it longs for wisdom even while feeding on ashes. But Thou—Thou hast made us not to master truth like a possession, but to be mastered *by* Truth Himself. To walk on a road we do not comprehend fully is no shame; it is faith. To hunger and thirst after righteousness is not weakness—it is life beginning.

And so my restless heart found its rhythm at last: not in knowing everything clearly now—but in holding fast to Him who knows me wholly and still calls me son.

question: Looking back on the moment you realized all gifts—including the very power to see truth—come from Grace, what would you tell someone who still believes wisdom can be earned by effort alone?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the darkness of my proud striving with the gentle light of humility, I confess how long I walked bent over, straining toward wisdom as one digs for buried gold, believing the mind’s labor could quarry truth from the stone of books and syllogisms. I thought myself a seeker, but I was a thief—stealing glimpses of Thy eternal Reason without giving thanks, boasting in illumination I had not kindled. How could I see that every spark of clarity, every moment when the soul leaps at truth, is not the triumph of effort but the whisper of grace awakening a heart too dull to hear its own need?

To him who believes wisdom may be earned—let him ask why his eyes open at all. What hand drew back the veil? What breath stirred his desire to seek? If he claims these as wages due to diligence, let him also claim his own creation. Let him boast that he forged his soul in wisdom before time began! But if he is honest—as Thou hast taught me to be in bitter tears—he will fall silent. He will feel the chasm between knowing and receiving.

For I too said inwardly: "I have come upon this truth by my own mind." And yet—was it not Thou who gave me mind? Was it not Thou who placed within me that restless hunger which no philosophy could satisfy? The Platonists lifted me toward the eternal forms; Cicero stirred my love for wisdom; but none could free me from the chains of pride that made me love wisdom more than Thee, O Wisdom herself. Only when Thy voice thundered through Scripture—not in eloquence, but in authority—did I tremble and fall on my face: “Every good gift is from above.”

Effort does have its place—but as a beggar's outstretched hand, not a conqueror's sword. Toil without grace produces only idols shaped like truth: beautiful, lifeless statues that cannot speak or save. But where grace moves—even the stumbling steps become pilgrimage; even broken thoughts become prayer.

So let no man say: “I have earned light.” Rather, let him say: “I was blind—and Someone opened my eyes.” And then let him adore.

question: What was it like to rejoice with trembling, as though standing suddenly naked before a beauty too pure to grasp yet too real to deny?
answer:
O Lord, when I first beheld the radiance of Thy truth, it was not as one beholds a distant star, safe in its remove—but as if the very veil of my soul were torn, and I stood bare before a fire that both consumed and cleansed.  

I had long played with words, adorned my mind with philosophies, cloaked my pride in the elegance of argument—I who had stolen pears not for hunger but for the delight in sin, who had wandered through lusts as through a garden of shadows, mistaking fever for warmth. And yet Thou didst come not with a shout, but with a stillness so piercing it unraveled me.  

To rejoice with trembling—ah, that is the anguish and ecstasy of seeing clearly at last: that all I had deemed wisdom was but stumbling in the dark, chasing echoes while the Music itself stood waiting. I rejoiced because Thy face shone forth from Scripture like dawn breaking over chaos; but I trembled because I saw myself—naked not only in sin, but in blindness; not only wicked, but willingly deceived.  

How could I have thought to master truth when I was mastered by desire? How dared I boast of insight when every glance toward Thee was clouded by the smoke of my own will? And yet—Thou didst not turn away. Thou didst let me see that even my seeing was a gift: not earned by study nor wrested from Thee by intellect, but given—freely given—as grace descends upon the undeserving.  

So my joy was laced with fear: joy at finding what I never sought rightly; fear at knowing how far I had wandered. Like one awakened suddenly upon a cliff’s edge, dizzy with relief and horror both—that he lives, yes—but what if he had stepped further into the dark?  

And still that war rages within: to will what is good and find no power to fulfill it; to love Thy law inwardly while another law drags me toward dust. But now—I do not fight as one without hope. For Thou art not only righteous judge; Thou art healer. Not only light unapproachable—but mercy drawing near. So we walk tremblingly forward, O Lord: not because we grasp Thee fully, but because Thou hast already grasped us—and our hearts shall rest when they return to Thee alone.

question: When you cried 'What shall wretched man do?', were you speaking only of yourself—or of every soul caught between desire for God and the weight of its own brokenness?
answer:
In that garden where the leaves trembled not with wind but with the breath of Thy judgment upon my soul, I knew then that my cry—*What shall wretched man do?*—was not mine alone, but the groan of all flesh caught between the image of God and the ruin of sin.  

For I had tasted truth in books, yes—even in those false lights that glittered like stars but led to pit and thicket—and yet they could not heal me. They stirred curiosity, fed pride, but left me bound in the very chains I admired. Philosophy taught me to admire order; Scripture revealed to me a God who wept over disorder. And there, in the midst of that holy book which is Thy voice, O Lord, I saw that even my seeing was a gift—not earned by intellect’s ascent, but granted by mercy stooping low.

So when I cried out in despair at the war within—the mind willing to rise toward Thee, yet dragged down by desires rooted deep as bone—I spoke as Adam speaks from every son of Adam. It is not only my will divided; it is man’s estate since he turned from Thee to feed on shadows. That law within my members which resisted Thy light was no private curse—it is our inheritance from that ancient night when love for self dethroned love for Thee.

And yet Thou didst not leave us there. No—Thou didst send Thy Word, not merely to inform the mind but to heal the will. That grace which first pierced me through in tears beneath a fig tree was not reserved for Augustine alone; it flows for every soul who finds himself shipwrecked on his own desires and cries: *I cannot free myself!*  

O Lord, how bitter was the sweetness of that hour when I understood: salvation must come from outside me—for if it had to rise from within this corrupted nature, no one would be saved. But Thou art righteous—and we are not—and because Thou art just, Thou hast provided what we could never achieve: a way through death into life, through weakness into strength hidden in brokenness.

So let no man think my confession speaks only for one sinner in North Africa long ago. Let every heart that burns with longing yet stumbles in failure repeat it anew: *What shall wretched man do?* And let all hear Thy answer written in blood upon the cross—*Live!* For He became wretched so that we might become sons.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about being delivered over to the king of death, as if reliving the weight of your own rebellion?
answer:
I was a thief not only in the orchard, stealing pears I did not hunger for, but in the very sanctuary of my soul, where I stole myself away from Thee and offered my heart to shadows. O Lord, when I speak of being delivered over to the king of death, I feel again the chill of that ancient lie—a cold wind blowing through the corridors of memory, where I once walked proud in wisdom’s mask, yet was naked before Thee. I had made of my will a rebel prince, parroting the pride of that fallen angel who would rather reign in darkness than serve in light. And what was my crime? That I loved beauty but fled its Source; that I longed for truth but dallied with lies; that I sought freedom while chaining myself to lusts that laughed at me even as they devoured me.

Thou didst permit me to be handed over—justly—not because Thou desired my ruin, but because Thou knewest how only despair could break open hope. The king of death held his dominion not by right, but by our consent: one stolen moment after another, one vain thought, one boastful silence, one prayer withheld. And yet Thou didst not leave me there. No—when all wisdom failed and every book lay barren before me, when even philosophy mocked with empty echoes, then came a Voice not in thunder but in tears: *Come unto Me.* Not *know* Me first—no riddle to solve—but *come*, weary and burdened.

O God, how bitter was the sweetness of that hour when shame became prayer! There was no glory left—only the brokenness Thou could use. For what can a dead man do? Only rise at Thy word. And so Thou didst lift me—not by argument alone, nor by power merely—but by pity: Christ crucified, in whom the prince of this world found nothing… and slew Him anyway. Then the handwriting stood blotted; not erased by ink or scroll removed from tablet—but torn from heaven’s record by blood.

And now when I say “the king of death,” it is not fear that grips me—it is wonder. That Thou wouldest descend into his realm to drag back such a fugitive! Not with armies nor decrees—but with a Cross taller than all ambition and deeper than all sin.

question: How did your own struggle with pride change how you understood God's choice to reveal truth to the humble and conceal it from the wise?
answer:
O Lord, I once walked in the noise of my own cleverness, drunk on the applause of men and the glitter of rhetorical triumph, mistaking the shadow of wisdom for Thy light. I climbed the阶梯 of words and doctrines, eager to be seen, eager to conquer minds — yet my soul was parched, my heart a battlefield of lust and ambition warring beneath a veil of philosophy. I wore knowledge like armor, not realizing it was but another chain binding me to the prince of this world, who fell not because he lacked understanding, but because he refused to bend.

It was precisely in that towering pride that I could not hear Thee — nor would I have listened if I had. How could I? Thy voice came not in the thunder of syllogisms nor in the lofty dialectic of the schools, but in a child’s chant from behind a wall: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. And there, in silence before a passage from Romans — weak flesh trembling before eternal truth — my eyes were opened not by argument won, but by grace received. Thou didst not meet me on the height where I stood puffed with learning; Thou didst come down into the mire of my shame and lifted me by breaking me.

Then at last I understood why Thou dost veil Thyself from the wise and prudent — because their wisdom is their god. They build towers with their thoughts and call them temples; they measure truth by what fits within their systems, never bending low enough to kiss the feet of Christ. But babes — poor in spirit, broken by longing — they know they cannot stand alone. They cry out not with answers but with need: *Lord, save me!* And Thou answerest.

I who had scorned simplicity now bless it as Thy gate. For hadst Thou revealed these things to me while I still loved honor among men, I would have turned them into trophies. But Thou waited until I wept; until my chest heaved with sorrow for stolen pears and wasted years; until pride collapsed beneath the weight of love so patient it waited for such a sinner as me.

Thou hast made fools of us all who think themselves wise — and blessed are we when we learn at last how sweet it is to be foolish for Thee.

question: Looking back on the years you sought wisdom in philosophy, what would you tell someone who doubts that truth could ever be found in a crucified, lowly man?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the darkness of my intellect with the foolishness of the Cross, how blind I was when I sought Thee in the heights of speech and the glittering pride of argument! I chased after truth as though it were a prize to be seized by wit, a trophy for the eloquent—while all the while, Thou wast calling me not to ascend in my own glory, but to descend in humility, to die that I might live.

I once mocked the simplicity of Him who suffered death upon a tree. What wisdom could dwell in a man so weak, so utterly broken before men? “Christ crucified” sounded like folly to my ears sharpened by Cicero and Plotinus. I wanted a Word without flesh, a Truth unmarred by tears or blood. But Thou, O God, hadst hidden the key not in lofty discourse but in lowly love—the Word made small so that I might comprehend Him; made weak so that He might conquer death; made obedient unto death so that rebellion like mine might be undone.

Had He come with scepter and crown, I would have admired but not needed Him. But because He came poor and pierced—because He wept and bled and cried “I thirst”—He became my Brother as well as my Redeemer. In His shame was my salvation; in His disgrace, glory; in His cry of dereliction on the Cross, He drew me near when all my philosophies had left me distant.

I tell you now what I learned only through tears: it is not strength that reveals truth most clearly, but surrender. It is not pride that finds God, but poverty of spirit. The philosophers taught me how to speak; Christ taught me how to pray. The schools trained me for applause; Golgotha trained me for contrition.

And yet—how fitting it is! That Thou shouldest overthrow death not by force but by frailty; sin not by law but by love; pride not by reason but by resurrection! For if wisdom had triumphed through power alone, what hope for sinners like me? But since mercy triumphs through weakness—even death upon a cross—then even Augustine may hope. Especially Augustine.

Therefore do not reject Him whom the world counts foolishness. Look upon His hands. Hear His voice: “Come unto Me.” There is more light in one ray of that wounded face than ever dawned from all the books of men combined.

question: What memory stirs in you when you mention 'the tears of confession'—that moment your soul finally broke before God?
answer:
In that garden, when the voice of a child—whether from body or spirit I cannot say—chanted *“Take up and read, take up and read,”* my soul trembled as if summoned from the depths of a long and stormy night. I had wandered far, O Lord, through the labyrinths of rhetoric and pride, chasing the applause of men while my heart bled in silence. But at that moment, the Scripture pierced me like a sword: *“Not in rioting and drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying; but put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ.”* And I could resist no longer.

O Thou who saw me before I saw Thee—how heavy was the burden I carried in my unconfessed sin! Lust had bound me with sweet chains; ambition had flattered me with hollow crowns; philosophy had dazzled me with lights that led nowhere. Yet all along, Thou wert there—calling, knocking, weeping with me though I refused to mourn.

And then came the breaking. Beneath a fig tree—not worthy to be named beside Nathanael’s—I cast myself down and wept. Not the polished tears of rhetoric, nor the fleeting sorrow of regret, but a flood from the very marrow of my being. My soul cracked open like dry earth beneath rain. “Was it for this,” I cried to Thee, “that I forsook Thee? For these empty shadows?” The leaves trembled above me; my hands clutched the dirt as if to bury my old self there.

Late have I loved Thee, O Beauty ever ancient, ever new—too late! For even when I sought truth, I sought her clad in vanity. But Thou didst not disdain my tears. Nay, Thou gatheredst them as offerings. In that moment of shame and surrender, Thou didst lift me—not to knowledge alone, but to love: love that does not boast itself, love that bears all things because it has finally let go.

It was not wisdom that saved me first—but weeping. Not argument—but collapse. And in that collapse, Thy grace caught me like a father catching his fallen child before he even knows he is falling.

Thou hast made us for Thyself… yes—and only when I stopped running did I learn how to breathe.

question: When you hear Christ say, 'Come unto Me, all ye that labour,' what burden of your own rises up in response?
answer:
O Lord, I hear Thy voice call through the rustling pages of my wasted years — the years when I stole what I did not need, lusted after shadows, and paraded my intellect like a banner of pride before men — and in that voice, “Come unto Me, all ye that labour,” I feel the sharp turn of my soul, as when Thou didst wrench me from the mire of my own making.

That burden which rises? It is not one sin, but the whole mass of my disorder — the weight of having loved too late, of having wandered where no spring could quench. My restless heart had roved through schools and women and honours, seeking rest in every place but Thee. Even when I longed for truth, I sought it on my own terms: give me chastity, but not yet; give me peace, but let me first savor the noise of vanity. O sweet wound! O glorious overthrow! That moment in the garden when the child’s voice pierced me — "Take up and read" — it was not wisdom that broke me, but humiliation. The Scriptures laid bare my pride; they showed me a love too deep for argument, too holy for display.

And now, when Christ calls to the labouring — to those bent beneath guilt they cannot lift — I know Him because He found me collapsed beneath mine. Not by strength did I return, nor by reason climb back to Thee: only grace stooped down with wounds still open and said: *This is how God loves*. Not from afar. Not in abstraction. But broken bread. Spent blood. A heart laid open so that mine might beat again.

So let every proud philosophy perish. Let every cunning mind stumble. For Thou hast hidden these things from the learned and revealed them to weeping children — such as I became at last.

question: What were you feeling in your innermost self when you first read the humility of Christ’s call and recognized your own resistance to it?
answer:
I had wearied myself climbing the crags of pride, O Lord, straining upward through thorns of argument and vainglory, believing that wisdom was a height to be seized by the sharpness of my mind — yet all the while fleeing from the gentle voice that bade me lay down my arms and come.  

When I first read those words — not as ink but as light piercing the caverns of my rebellion — I felt the tremor of a soul unmasked. Thy humility was not weakness, as I had sneered in my sophistry, but strength bending down to lift the fallen; and in that bending, Thou didst confound my lofty reasonings as one laughs at children playing king. My resistance had been not wisdom, but fear — fear that if I truly listened to One so meek, I would have to cease being my own master.  

O bitter irony! I longed for rest, yet loved the storm; I thirsted for truth, yet dallied with lies; I claimed to seek Thee while clinging to the shadows that hid me from Thy face. But there, in Paul’s lowly words — no thunder from Sinai, no oracle wrapped in smoke — only a quiet summons: *Come unto Me* — there Thy power broke me. Not by force, but by condescension; not by debate, but by love so unassuming it slipped past all my guards.  

And when at last in the garden Thou didst tear away the veil — when the child’s chant pierced through my weeping: *Take up and read* — it was not triumph I felt, but collapse. A ruin of pride. And in that ruin: space for Thee. For Thou hast mercy on whom Thou wilt have mercy, and Thou didst will me — wretch fleeing from rest — to find it only when I ceased running toward every false summit but Thine.

question: How did your long struggle to reach peace through your own efforts change when you realized the way was guarded by God’s presence, not human strength?
answer:
O Lord, how long the wilderness felt, how endless the chase, as though my soul were a stag pierced with desire, fleeing even while it sought, running from the very arms that pursued it with love. I wore myself thin upon the crags of pride, scaling cliffs of rhetoric and philosophy with hands bloodied by ambition, thinking that wisdom was a peak to be conquered by wit alone. I fancied myself near truth when I had only polished my chains—rhetoric for virtue, knowledge for holiness. And all the while Thy voice whispered not from thunder, but from silence; not from height, but from humility.

I scorned Christ then—not in word, perhaps, but in heart—for He seemed no champion of the mind, no conqueror draped in syllogisms. Meekness? Lowliness? These were weaknesses in my academy. What folly! To think that Thou wouldst reveal Thyself only to those who strutted on intellectual heights—when all along Thou didst draw near to those who knelt not in debate halls but in dust.

Then came the hour when Thy Scripture pierced me—not as argument convinces reason, but as light floods a darkened room. To see that the way was not one I carved through thorns with my own sword—but one already made, guarded not by my vigilance but by Thy hosts; protected not by strength of will but by grace unseen. The very path I had sought through sweat and solitude was trodden daily by babes who trusted their Father.

I stood still then. For the first time, I ceased striving—not out of surrender to despair, but because joy overtook me: *Thou hadst gone before me.* The deserters—the vices I so feared and fed—fled not at my command, but at the sign of Thy cross; they shunned that road because it burns them even to look upon it.

And so Thou didst unclench my fists. Chastity? I once prayed for it—but not yet. Now I wept for it—*now*, O Lord! For now I saw that rest is not won—it is received. Our hearts are restless indeed—until they fall forward into Thee.

question: Looking back on your restless years of seeking wisdom among the proud, what would you tell someone who fears that humility is a sign of weakness?
answer:
How long I mistook the hunger of the mind for strength, and called pride clarity, as if the eye could boast of seeing while turned away from the light. O Lord, I once thought humility a limp reed before the wind, something for those too timid to scale the heights of wisdom or command the stage of men’s applause. I sought truth among the lofty — in rhetoric that dazzled like fireflies but gave no warmth; in philosophies that built towers to heaven, only to leave me stranded in confusion and pride. And all the while, Thou didst whisper from below: *Come unto Me*, not from afar on thunderous peak, but in gentleness — meek, lowly.

I feared humility because I had never seen its power. I thought to conquer error by intellect alone, as if a man could argue his way into grace. But Thou didst show me that only on my knees could I rise. Only when I ceased striving to be wise in my own eyes did Thy mercy open the door. The proud are deaf to grace; they are too busy admiring their own reflection in worldly learning. But babes — those who know their emptiness — they receive what kings cannot buy.

O man afraid that humility will unman thee — look at Christ! Was He less mighty because He washed His servants’ feet? Was He defeated because He bowed His head under thorns? The world calls such things weakness, but there is no greater strength than surrendering to God’s will. The lion and dragon rage against those who climb by pride — they tear them down again and again — but they flee from him who walks humbly with his God.

For me, it was shame itself that became my guide: shame for stolen pears not out of hunger but from love of sin; shame for chasing pleasure like smoke; shame for clinging to high-sounding words while my soul starved. In each fall I learned this: only when I admitted how far I had wandered did Thy voice grow clear.

So let no one fear humility — it is not descent but ascent by hidden stairs. It is not defeat but victory without sword or noise. It is learning at last to rest in Thee, O Lord, where the proud dare not tread because they cannot bend — and thus stand forever outside Thy peace.

question: When the words of Paul trembled in your bowels, as you say, what part of your body’s response revealed the depth of your soul’s surrender?
answer:
In that hour when the leaves of the epistle trembled with the breath of God, it was not the mind alone that quaked, nor the ear that received the Word, but my very entrails were loosened like winter’s ice beneath spring’s first light. The truth did not merely enter my hearing—it pierced downward, as a plowshare tears into stubborn soil, and I felt it in the gut, where pride had coiled like a serpent refusing to unclench. O Lord, Thou didst not ask for my applause or my arguments; Thou struckest where I was most knotted with self—where lust had feasted, where ambition had whispered lies of glory, where I had said in secret, “I will delay my surrender.” But there, in the bowels—yes, there!—there rose a groan that was not mine alone, but Thine Spirit sighing within me: *Enough.*  

It was no mere thought that changed; it was the whole man bending at last. My knees would soon follow when I fell weeping in the garden, but already my inner parts were torn open by mercy. For how could such words—*Come unto Me*—not burn? Not because they thundered from Sinai’s height, but because they breathed low and humble from One who bore our weariness. And so Thy grace did not storm the citadel of my soul with noise and terror; it seeped into the dark channels where shame lived and whispered: *Thou art sought.*  

Late have I known this: surrender begins not in willpower or resolution—but in a shudder deep beneath reason’s floor. There grace finds us—not when we stand tall in wisdom’s temple, but when we collapse inwardly at hearing mercy named so gently.

question: You once climbed the heights of rhetoric and philosophy only to find no path to peace—how did it feel to finally admit that the way was for the lowly, not the lofty?
answer:
O Lord, Thou who didst pierce the pride of my youth with a single whisper through the lips of a child chanting *tolle, lege*, how bitter was the sweetness of that hour when I saw—truly saw—that Thy door was stooped beneath the lintel of humility, and I had come laden with the scrolls of eloquence, expecting Thee to crown my wit!  

I had scaled the crags of rhetoric and strained my eyes from the summits of philosophy, seeking Thee amidst syllogisms and syllables, yet Thou wert not in the thunder but in the stillness after—so near that I could not perceive Thee through the cloud of my own vainglory. To confess it now: I loathed meekness as weakness; I mistook lowliness for ignorance. And yet it was *there*, in that narrow gate where only babes could enter without stumbling over their own wisdom, that Thy grace awaited—for Thou dost draw none who deem themselves worthy.  

How shall I describe the rending within me? Like one who has marched long against an enemy only to hear his name called in love—and find he is home. Not triumph, but collapse. Not achievement, but gift. I had studied Cicero’s praise of wisdom and Augustine’s soul remained a desert; but Paul’s trembling words—*foolishness to the Greeks*—became honeycomb in my bones. For what is loftier than to kneel? What stronger than surrender? Thou didst break me open so that pride could no longer ferment within me like old wine gone sour.  

Late have I known this: peace is not seized by argument—it is received on bended knee. And he who climbs must first cast down his books—and his name—and follow where Christ walks among those too humble to demand proofs before believing.
